subject: Neuro
stem: A 19-year-old college student is brought to the emergency department after collapsing in his dorm room. His roommate reports that he had been complaining of worsening headaches, especially in the mornings and when bending forward, along with recent episodes of nausea. He had no significant past medical history and took no medications. On arrival, the patient is unresponsive. Vital signs reveal hypertension and bradycardia. CT scan of the head shows enlargement of both lateral ventricles, while the third and fourth ventricles appear normal. Which of the following is the most likely underlying lesion?

options.D: Colloid cyst of the foramen of Monro

answer_text: Colloid cyst of the foramen of Monro
explanation: This patient presents with acute obstructive hydrocephalus, evidenced by rapid neurologic deterioration, morning headaches, vomiting, Cushing triad (hypertension and bradycardia), and enlargement of both lateral ventricles. The imaging pattern-dilated lateral ventricles with normal third and fourth ventricles-strongly suggests an obstruction at the foramen of Monro, which connects each lateral ventricle to the third ventricle. The classic lesion here is a colloid cyst, a benign, mucin-filled tumor that can intermittently block CSF flow, sometimes leading to sudden death if untreated. Colloid cysts typically occur in young adults and may present with position-dependent headaches, nausea, and eventual rapid decompensation due to elevated intracranial pressure. They can be visualized on CT or MRI and are surgically resectable. Their location makes them particularly dangerous despite being histologically benign.
bottom_line: This patient presents with acute obstructive hydrocephalus, evidenced by rapid neurologic deterioration, morning headaches, vomiting, Cushing triad (hypertension and bradycardia), and enlargement of both lateral ventricles.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 46-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his wife because of progressive changes in mood, thinking, and movement. Over the past several years, she has noticed that he has become more forgetful, often misplacing items or repeating the same questions. Recently, he has struggled to keep track of bills and could no longer manage his work as a financial consultant, leading to his early retirement. She also reports that he has become irritable and at times displays inappropriate laughter or anger. In addition, he has developed frequent, unpredictable jerking movements of his face, arms, and legs that worsen with stress and interfere with daily activities. The patient denies headaches, vision changes, or episodes of loss of consciousness. He does not use alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs. His father reportedly had similar symptoms in his 50s, though no formal diagnosis was made. The patient takes medication for mild hypertension but no psychiatric drugs. On physical exam, he is oriented to person but not to time or place. His speech is fluent but tangential, and he has difficulty recalling words and following complex instructions. Neurologic exam shows irregular, nonrhythmic, involuntary movements involving the upper and lower extremities as well as facial grimacing. Reflexes and strength are otherwise intact. On mental status testing, he is unable to recall any of three objects after five minutes and struggles to interpret common proverbs. Cranial nerve exam is normal. Gait is wide-based and irregular due to sudden, unpredictable jerks. MRI of the brain is obtained. Which of the following findings is most likely to be seen on imaging?

options.D: Increased T2 signal in the putamen
options.E: Lesions in the frontal white matter

explanation: This patient presents with a classic triad of progressive cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms, and choreiform movements, which are hallmark features of Huntington disease. Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by CAG trinucleotide repeat (TNR) expansion on chromosome 4. A key genetic feature is anticipation, in which the number of repeats increases across successive generations, leading to earlier onset and more severe disease in children compared to parents. The pathologic hallmark is neuronal degeneration in the caudate nucleus and putamen (together known as the striatum). Caudate atrophy leads to loss of inhibitory GABAergic neurons, resulting in disinhibition of motor thalamic nuclei and hyperkinetic movements such as chorea. MRI typically shows bilateral atrophy of the caudate, which causes enlargement of the frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, giving them a “boxcar” appearance. The neuropsychiatric symptoms are equally important. Early in the disease, patients may develop depression, irritability, apathy, or inappropriate behavior. As the disease progresses, executive function declines, and memory impairment becomes severe. The involvement of the frontal-subcortical circuits explains the behavioral changes and impaired recall. Progressive worsening ultimately leads to dementia, loss of independence, and severe motor disability. The strong family history (especially with younger onset in affected relatives) is explained by anticipation, making genetic counseling essential. From a pathophysiologic standpoint, degeneration of the caudate nucleus and putamen disrupts the indirect pathway of the basal ganglia, which normally suppresses unwanted movements. This loss of inhibition produces the choreiform, dance-like, irregular movements seen clinically. Later in the course, rigidity and bradykinesia may emerge as the disease progresses. Understanding this localization helps differentiate Huntington disease from other basal ganglia disorders such as Parkinson’s (substantia nigra degeneration) or Wilson’s disease (putamen pathology).
bottom_line: This patient presents with a classic triad of progressive cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms, and choreiform movements, which are hallmark features of Huntington disease.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of weakness and numbness in his right upper extremity. He works as a warehouse loader and says the symptoms began two weeks ago after lifting several heavy boxes overhead. He reports that the weakness seems to be worse when gripping objects tightly or pushing open doors. He also describes a tingling sensation that radiates from his shoulder down the posterior aspect of the arm and forearm, and into the middle finger. The symptoms worsen when he tilts his head to the right or looks upward for prolonged periods. On examination, there is decreased triceps reflex on the right compared to the left. Strength testing reveals reduced strength with wrist extension and elbow extension, though shoulder abduction and finger abduction are preserved. There is diminished pinprick sensation over the posterior forearm and dorsal surface of the third digit. Spurling’s maneuver reproduces his symptoms. X-ray of the cervical spine shows degenerative changes with disc space narrowing and osteophyte formation. Compression of which of the following spinal nerve roots most likely explains this patient’s symptoms?
explanation: The C7 nerve root innervates the triceps (elbow extension), wrist extensors, and provides sensation to the posterior arm and forearm, as well as the middle finger. It is the most commonly affected cervical root in radiculopathy. This patient’s combination of triceps weakness, decreased triceps reflex, wrist extensor weakness, and numbness in the third digit is classic for C7 radiculopathy, typically resulting from disc herniation or osteophyte impingement at the C6-C7 intervertebral foramen. Cervical radiculopathy frequently presents with dermatomal sensory loss, myotomal weakness, and reduced reflexes. In the case of C7, both the motor and sensory findings are highly localized and allow for accurate diagnosis without advanced imaging in many cases. The triceps reflex is a key differentiator since it is mediated by C7, while the biceps and brachioradialis reflexes are controlled by C5 and C6, respectively. Provocative maneuvers such as Spurling’s test (neck extension and rotation with axial loading) often exacerbate symptoms by narrowing the intervertebral foramina further. Management includes NSAIDs, physical therapy, and avoidance of aggravating activities. Most cases improve over time with conservative care, though persistent or progressive deficits may require MRI and neurosurgical evaluation. Recognizing the classic signs of C7 involvement-including middle finger numbness, triceps weakness, and wrist extension deficits-is crucial for early diagnosis.
bottom_line: The C7 nerve root innervates the triceps (elbow extension), wrist extensors, and provides sensation to the posterior arm and forearm, as well as the middle finger.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 68-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife due to confusion and difficulty attending to his surroundings. She reports that since waking up this morning, he has ignored food on the left side of his plate and does not respond when spoken to from his left side. He shaves only the right half of his face and seems unaware of the problem. On neurologic exam, he is alert and oriented, with normal strength and intact language function. When asked to draw a clock, he places all the numbers on the right side of the circle. MRI reveals an acute infarct in the right cerebral hemisphere. Which of the following brain regions is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient exhibits hemispatial neglect syndrome, a hallmark of lesions in the non-dominant (usually right) posterior parietal lobe, particularly the right superior parietal lobule. This region is responsible for spatial attention, especially for bilateral external space. The right hemisphere attends to both left and right space, whereas the left hemisphere attends primarily to the right space. Therefore, right parietal damage leads to profound neglect of the left side, often with anosognosia (lack of awareness of the deficit), while left parietal lesions produce little to no right-sided neglect. Classic findings include left-sided inattention, ignoring stimuli or motor planning for the left side, and drawing errors (e.g., clock numbers all crowded to the right). The exam may show normal strength, normal language, and no primary sensory loss, highlighting the cortical nature of the deficit.
bottom_line: This patient exhibits hemispatial neglect syndrome, a hallmark of lesions in the non-dominant (usually right) posterior parietal lobe, particularly the right superior parietal lobule.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision. Emergency medical services report that he was initially responsive but confused at the scene. During transport, his mental status worsened, and he became progressively less verbal. On arrival, he is unresponsive to verbal commands but withdraws to painful stimuli. His pupils are asymmetric, with the left larger than the right and sluggishly reactive to light. His vital signs are: temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure 198/104 mmHg, heart rate 44/min, respiratory rate 7/min, and oxygen saturation 91% on room air. The patient has multiple scalp abrasions and a large contusion over the left temporal region. Breath sounds are symmetric, and heart tones are normal. Peripheral pulses are intact, and extremities show no deformities. The patient is intubated for airway protection. A non-contrast CT scan of the head reveals a rapidly expanding extra-axial hemorrhage with midline shift. Which of the following best explains this patient’s hemodynamic and respiratory pattern?

options.A: Activation of baroreceptors due to systemic hypovolemia
options.B: Autonomic storm triggered by brainstem herniation
options.C: Collapse of intracerebral vessels from rising cranial pressure
options.D: Primary injury to the sinoatrial node in blunt chest trauma

answer_text: Collapse of intracerebral vessels from rising cranial pressure
explanation: This is a lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve palsy. LMN lesions of CN VII result in weakness of both the upper and lower facial muscles on the affected side, including the forehead. The absence of systemic findings or vesicles supports idiopathic inflammatory neuropathy (post-viral), the most common cause of LMN facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). The nerve is affected after it exits the brainstem, distal to the facial nucleus.
bottom_line: This is a lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve palsy.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset weakness of the right side of her face. She is unable to close her right eye, raise her right eyebrow, or smile symmetrically. She reports recent recovery from an upper respiratory illness. She denies rash, ear pain, and tick exposure. Neurologic examination reveals complete right-sided facial paralysis. Strength and sensation in the limbs are normal. Which of the following best explains the underlying cause of her condition?

options.A: Infarction of corticobulbar fibers in the left internal capsule
options.B: Mass lesion compressing the facial nerve branches within the parotid gland
options.C: Inflammatory demyelination of the right facial nerve distal to the facial nucleus
options.D: Reactivation of varicella-zoster virus in the geniculate ganglion
options.E: Infection with Borrelia burgdorferi acquired during recent hiking

answer_text: Inflammatory demyelination of the right facial nerve distal to the facial nucleus
explanation: Bell's palsy is a lower motor neuron facial nerve palsy that affects both the upper and lower face. In this vignette, the sudden complete hemifacial weakness after a recent upper respiratory illness is most consistent with idiopathic post-viral inflammatory demyelination of the facial nerve distal to the facial nucleus.
bottom_line: Bell's palsy causes complete unilateral facial weakness, including the forehead, due to a peripheral CN VII lesion.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 44-year-old man presents to the clinic due to progressive weakness in his right hand. He works as a carpenter and says he has trouble gripping tools and typing. On examination, there is wasting of the hypothenar eminence and interosseous muscles. When asked to extend all his fingers, his 4th and 5th digits remain flexed at the interphalangeal joints and hyperextended at the metacarpophalangeal joints. Sensation is decreased over the medial side of the hand and the fifth digit. Wrist flexion is preserved. Tinel sign is positive at the medial elbow. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient has signs of ulnar neuropathy, most consistent with compression at the cubital tunnel, located behind the medial epicondyle. The ulnar nerve (C8-T1) innervates the interossei, hypothenar muscles, and medial two lumbricals, all of which contribute to finger extension and fine motor coordination. When these muscles are weak, extension of the fingers becomes unopposed by the flexors, resulting in a “claw hand” deformity of the 4th and 5th digits-hyperextension at the MCP joints and flexion at the IP joints. Sensory loss over the medial hand and fifth digit, and a positive Tinel sign at the elbow, further localize the lesion to the cubital tunnel. Wrist flexion and pronation are preserved because the flexor carpi ulnaris and medial FDP are variably affected depending on the level of injury. This form of ulnar neuropathy is common in individuals with repetitive elbow flexion or pressure on the medial elbow (e.g., occupational exposure, leaning on elbows).
bottom_line: This patient has signs of ulnar neuropathy, most consistent with compression at the cubital tunnel, located behind the medial epicondyle.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 47-year-old man comes to the neurology clinic with progressive numbness and tingling in his hands and feet for the past 8 months. He also reports mild weakness when climbing stairs and occasional difficulty with fine motor tasks, such as buttoning his shirt. He has no history of trauma, diabetes, alcohol use, or recent infections. His family history is notable for a brother diagnosed with a hereditary neuropathy in his 30s. On examination, there is reduced vibration and pinprick sensation in a stocking-glove distribution, mild distal muscle weakness in the lower limbs, and absent ankle reflexes. Gait is broad-based but stable. The remainder of the neurologic examination is unremarkable. Nerve conduction studies show slowed conduction velocities in both sensory and motor nerves of the upper and lower extremities. The neurologist decides to obtain a nerve biopsy to confirm the diagnosis but wants to avoid any additional motor impairment in this patient. Which of the following nerves is the most appropriate site for biopsy?
explanation: This patient has a chronic, progressive, length-dependent neuropathy with both sensory and mild motor findings, confirmed by slowed conduction velocities on nerve conduction studies. Nerve biopsy is sometimes performed to clarify the etiology of a neuropathy when the diagnosis remains uncertain, such as in suspected vasculitic neuropathy, amyloidosis, leprosy, or certain hereditary neuropathies (eg, Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease). The key concern in selecting a biopsy site is to avoid causing any new or permanent motor deficits. For this reason, the sural nerve is preferred because it is a purely sensory nerve-removal does not result in motor weakness. The sural nerve is formed by branches of the tibial and common fibular nerves and runs superficially down the posterior-lateral aspect of the distal leg, posterior to the lateral malleolus, and into the lateral aspect of the foot. It carries sensory information from the posterolateral leg, lateral foot, and fifth toe. Because it has no motor fibers, sural nerve biopsy causes only a localized area of numbness in its sensory distribution, with no impact on muscle strength or motor function. This makes it the ideal choice for diagnostic biopsy when pathological confirmation is required in peripheral neuropathy evaluation.
bottom_line: Cutaneous nerves of the hand.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 67-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive muscle weakness, fatigue, dry mouth, and difficulty climbing stairs. He has a 50-pack- year smoking history and reports recent episodes of erectile dysfunction and constipation. Neurologic examination reveals proximal muscle weakness and absent deep tendon reflexes. Sensory examination is normal. A chest x-ray shows a right hilar mass. After repeated testing of muscle strength, his power improves noticeably with continued effort. Which of the following best explains the improvement in this patient’s muscle function?

options.A: Decreased acetylcholine reuptake at the motor endplate
options.B: Increased calcium influx with repeated depolarization
options.C: Inhibition of acetylcholinesterase at the synaptic cleft
options.E: Inhibition of voltage-gated sodium channel activation

answer_text: Increased calcium influx with repeated depolarization
explanation: This patient has Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS). The highest yield point of LEMS is being a classic paraneoplastic syndrome associated with small cell lung carcinoma. LEMS is caused by autoantibodies against presynaptic P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels, which reduce the release of acetylcholine (ACh) at the neuromuscular junction. This results in proximal muscle weakness, most commonly affecting the legs and pelvic girdle, along with autonomic dysfunction such as dry mouth, constipation, erectile dysfunction, and hyporeflexia. A hallmark of LEMS is that muscle strength improves with repeated use, a phenomenon known as facilitation. Repeated depolarization leads to gradual calcium accumulation in the presynaptic terminal, eventually enhancing ACh release into the synaptic cleft. This is in contrast to myasthenia gravis, where repeated use worsens symptoms due to depletion of postsynaptic receptors. Diagnosis is confirmed with voltage-gated calcium channel antibody testing, and treatment involves managing the underlying malignancy.
bottom_line: This patient has Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS).

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 19-year-old college student is brought to the emergency department by his roommate after noticing sudden changes in his face earlier that morning. The roommate reports that when the patient tried to smile, the left side of his mouth did not move, and milk spilled out while he was drinking. The patient himself says that his face “feels heavy” and that he cannot close his left eye completely. He also notes a dull aching pain around his left ear that started the night before, which has not been relieved by over-the-counter medication. He denies fever, rash, limb weakness, double vision, or numbness. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He has not had any recent trauma, and there is no history of autoimmune or neuromuscular disease in his family. He does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. He recalls having a mild upper respiratory illness about a week ago but otherwise has been healthy. On examination, his vital signs are within normal limits. Neurologic testing shows weakness involving both the upper and lower portions of the left face: his left eye cannot close tightly, his forehead does not wrinkle on that side, and the left corner of his mouth droops. Sensation over the face is intact bilaterally. There are no deficits in limb strength, reflexes, or coordination. His speech is clear, but when he tries to puff out his cheeks, air escapes from the left side. Hearing is intact, though he complains of mild discomfort near the left ear. The remainder of the physical examination is normal. Which of the following best describes the location of the abnormality?
explanation: This patient’s acute onset of unilateral facial weakness involving both the upper and lower face, accompanied by ear pain and normal facial sensation, is characteristic of a peripheral facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) lesion. A central lesion, such as a stroke, would typically spare the forehead due to bilateral cortical innervation of the upper face. Here, however, the involvement of both the forehead and the lower face points to a peripheral process. The presence of ipsilateral ear pain further supports inflammation or irritation of the facial nerve as it travels through the temporal bone. Peripheral facial nerve lesions disrupt both voluntary and reflex motor function of the muscles of facial expression. Patients cannot close the eye tightly, raise the eyebrow, or smile on the affected side. In addition, tearing, taste sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, and hyperacusis may also occur depending on the exact site of nerve involvement. Viral inflammation (often due to herpes simplex virus reactivation) is a well-known cause, though idiopathic cases are common. The pathophysiology involves swelling of the nerve within the narrow bony canal of the temporal bone, leading to compression and impaired conduction. This explains why patients often have accompanying ear pain. Recovery is variable, but many patients improve spontaneously within weeks to months, especially with early corticosteroid treatment. The key distinguishing feature is the complete unilateral involvement of facial muscles, which clearly localizes the lesion to the peripheral nerve rather than the brain, neuromuscular junction, or muscle fibers themselves.
bottom_line: This patient’s acute onset of unilateral facial weakness involving both the upper and lower face, accompanied by ear pain and normal facial sensation, is characteristic of a peripheral facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) lesion.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 33-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after collapsing during a marathon on a hot summer day. On arrival, his temperature is 39.5°C (103.1°F), and he appears flushed and confused. He is diaphoretic with hypotension and tachycardia. Neurologic exam is otherwise normal. Further evaluation reveals impaired heat dissipation and autonomic dysregulation. MRI shows a small lesion affecting part of the hypothalamus. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?
explanation: This patient has impaired thermoregulation due to anterior hypothalamic damage. The anterior hypothalamus controls heat dissipation (“A/C center”) by promoting vasodilation and sweating during hyperthermia. Damage to this region impairs the body’s ability to cool itself, leading to hyperthermia, flushed skin, and autonomic instability. The anterior hypothalamus receives input from thermoreceptors and coordinates parasympathetic responses to dissipate heat. The posterior hypothalamus, by contrast, promotes heat conservation. The hypothalamic nuclei can be remembered with a simple "animal farm" style mnemonic. The preoptic nucleus ("front of the eyes - eyes show lust") regulates GnRH secretion. The supraoptic nucleus ("above the eyes - lacrimal glands above eyes secrete tears") controls water balance via ADH secretion. The suprachiasmatic nucleus ("the eyes close during sleep") controls the circadian rhythm. The anterior nucleus ("wet nose - cooling system in animals") controls body cooling and parasympathetic activity. The lateral nucleus ("hunger center - animal eats"), when stimulated, increases hunger; while the ventromedial nucleus (VMN) ("satiety center - stops eating") promotes satiety. The dorsomedial nucleus (DMN) helps regulate gastrointestinal stimulation. The arcuate nucleus ("curved udder of a cow") produces hypothalamic releasing hormones including dopamine, which inhibits prolactin and milk secretion. The posterior nucleus ("farting posterior of a cow") controls heating and sympathetic tone. Finally, the mammillary bodies ("mammary = memory") are involved in memory processing as part of the Papez circuit, receiving input from the hippocampus via the fornix and projecting to the anterior thalamus via the mammillothalamic tract.
bottom_line: This patient has impaired thermoregulation due to anterior hypothalamic damage.

subject: Neuro
stem: A neuroscience lab is conducting an experiment using a transient cerebral ischemia model in aged rhesus monkeys to study selective vulnerability of brain regions to hypoperfusion. In the experiment, animals undergo a 20- minute occlusion of the posterior cerebral artery followed by reperfusion. Cognitive function is evaluated over the following week using spatial navigation mazes and object- recognition tests. Although gross neurologic function (motor strength, reflexes, coordination) remains intact in all subjects, a subset of animals exhibits a persistent inability to recall newly introduced information, despite preserved long-term memory for pre-ischemia tasks. Postmortem histologic analysis reveals localized neuronal loss in a bilateral medial temporal lobe structure characterized by a high density of glutamatergic synapses, NMDA receptor expression, and involvement in short-term to long-term memory consolidation. Which of the following brain regions was most likely affected in this experimental model?
explanation: This experiment models transient cerebral ischemia and evaluates selective vulnerability of brain structures to hypoperfusion. The posterior cerebral artery (PCA) supplies several medial temporal structures, including the hippocampus, which is known to be highly susceptible to ischemic injury due to its high metabolic demand and dense concentration of excitatory glutamatergic neurons. The animals show new-onset anterograde amnesia, characterized by an inability to form new memories despite preserved recall of previously learned tasks. This is a classic functional consequence of bilateral hippocampal damage. Histologic findings further confirm this localization: the lesion lies in a medial temporal structure, shows NMDA receptor expression, and is involved in consolidation of short-term to long-term memory. The hippocampus is rich in NMDA-type glutamate receptors, which mediate long-term potentiation, a synaptic mechanism crucial for memory formation. During ischemia, excessive glutamate release and NMDA activation can cause calcium-mediated excitotoxicity, leading to selective neuronal loss in this region. No deficits are observed in motor or sensory pathways, supporting a localized cognitive impairment rather than global brain dysfunction. In clinical analogs, such as transient ischemic attacks, the hippocampus is particularly vulnerable to brief but profound hypoperfusion, especially in the elderly or in PCA distribution strokes. It is also one of the earliest sites affected in Alzheimer disease, further emphasizing its role in memory processing and sensitivity to metabolic stress.
bottom_line: This experiment models transient cerebral ischemia and evaluates selective vulnerability of brain structures to hypoperfusion.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 61-year-old man presents with complaints of worsening morning headaches and nausea for the past 3 months, which improve as the day progresses. On further questioning, he reports occasional episodes of involuntary urine leakage without awareness of bladder fullness. The neurologic examination reveals increased tone and 3+ deep tendon reflexes in both lower extremities. The upper extremities have normal tone and 2+ reflexes. His gait is stiff and narrow-based, with the knees crossing midline while walking. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

options.A: Acute infarct of the internal capsule
options.B: Meningioma at the falx cerebri between the frontal lobes

explanation: This patient has a right cerebellar hemispheric lesion. The cerebellar hemispheres control coordination of the ipsilateral limbs, leading to right-sided dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, and falling toward the right. Limb ataxia is the hallmark of hemispheric lesions. In contrast, lesions of the cerebellar vermis primarily affect midline structures, leading to truncal ataxia, wide-based gait, and balance difficulty without limb-specific dysmetria. Because the cerebellum controls the same side of the body, the lesion is localized ipsilaterally.
bottom_line: This patient has a right cerebellar hemispheric lesion.

subject: Neuro
topic: Normal structure and function of the nervous system
stem: A 62-year-old man presents with unsteady gait and tremor. On exam, he has difficulty performing finger-to-nose testing on the right side, with overshooting of his hand (dysmetria). He also has intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia on the right, and falls toward the right when standing. His gait is otherwise wide-based. There is no limb weakness or sensory loss. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?
explanation: Right-sided dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, and falling toward the right localize to the right cerebellar hemisphere, which coordinates ipsilateral limb movement.
bottom_line: Cerebellar hemisphere lesions cause ipsilateral limb ataxia, dysmetria, and intention tremor.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motorcycle accident. He was thrown from the vehicle and landed on his back. On arrival, he is awake and alert but unable to move either leg. Temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure is 84/60 mm Hg, and pulse is 52/min. Neurologic exam shows complete paralysis of both lower extremities with flaccid tone and absent deep tendon reflexes. Pain, temperature, vibration, and proprioception are absent below the nipples. Anal tone is absent, and there is no response to bulbocavernosus reflex testing. Upper extremity function is intact. Foley catheter insertion yields 1.1 liters of retained urine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with acute spinal shock due to a complete transection of the spinal cord, most likely at the T4 level, given the sensory level just below the nipples (T4 dermatome). The initial phase of spinal shock is characterized by flaccid paralysis, areflexia, urinary retention, absent bulbocavernosus reflex, and hypotension with bradycardia due to disruption of sympathetic tone (if above T6). Over days to weeks, patients transition into a chronic phase of spasticity, hyperreflexia, and positive Babinski signs, indicating unmasking of upper motor neuron signs once spinal reflex arcs recover. Complete transection leads to total bilateral motor and sensory loss below the level of injury, including proprioception, vibration, pain, and temperature, as well as autonomic dysfunction.
bottom_line: This patient presents with acute spinal shock due to a complete transection of the spinal cord, most likely at the T4 level, given the sensory level just below the nipples (T4 dermatome).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 36-year-old woman comes to clinic reporting persistent difficulty lifting her right arm overhead several weeks after hospitalization for a severe lung infection. She describes trouble reaching high shelves and brushing her hair. Her prior admission involved chest drainage due to accumulation of infected fluid in the right pleural cavity. Recovery was complicated by discomfort near the upper chest wall, though the infection has resolved. On examination, motor strength and reflexes in the arm and forearm are preserved. Passive and active range of motion at the glenohumeral joint is mostly intact. When asked to push against the wall with both palms forward and arms extended, there is noticeable protrusion along the inner border of her right scapula. No sensory deficits are detected over the shoulder or arm. There is no muscle wasting or deformity over the deltoid, supraspinatus, or trapezius regions. A plain radiograph of the shoulder shows no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following muscles is most likely affected in this patient?
explanation: This patient’s clinical picture-difficulty raising her right arm and visible scapular prominence when pressing against a wall-strongly suggests dysfunction of the serratus anterior muscle, most likely due to long thoracic nerve injury incurred during a prior tube thoracostomy. The serratus anterior originates on the outer surface of the first 8 or 9 ribs and inserts on the anterior aspect of the medial border of the scapula. Its primary function is to anchor the scapula to the thoracic wall and assist in upward rotation and protraction during shoulder elevation. This stabilizing role is critical in actions like lifting the arm above the head, where the scapula must rotate laterally and tilt to maintain the glenoid facing superiorly. The serratus anterior is uniquely innervated by the long thoracic nerve, which arises from the anterior rami of the C5, C6, and C7 nerve roots. This nerve travels superficially along the lateral chest wall, making it highly susceptible to injury during surgical interventions such as lymph node dissection, radical mastectomy, or tube thoracostomy. Tube thoracostomy (chest tube insertion) is indicated for various pleural pathologies, including empyema, pneumothorax, hemothorax, or large pleural effusions. The standard approach for tube placement involves the "safe triangle," bordered by the lateral edge of pectoralis major (anterior), the anterior border of latissimus dorsi (posterior), a line superior to the nipple (fifth intercostal space), and the apex just below the axilla. Misplacement too posteriorly or superiorly can injure adjacent nerves, including the long thoracic nerve. Damage to the long thoracic nerve results in paralysis of the serratus anterior, leading to "medial winging" of the scapula-where the medial border lifts away from the thoracic wall during arm extension or wall push maneuvers. Other clinical associations include viral neuritis, repetitive overhead activity in athletes (e.g., baseball pitchers), and trauma. Elevation of the shoulder involves coordinated action of multiple muscles. The deltoid (axillary nerve) initiates abduction past 15 degrees, while supraspinatus (suprascapular nerve) initiates the first 15 degrees. Full elevation requires upward rotation of the scapula, mediated by the trapezius (spinal accessory nerve) and serratus anterior. Disruption in any component of this chain-rotator cuff, scapular stabilizers, or innervating nerves-can impair arm elevation. In this patient, the inability to elevate the arm fully and the scapular winging strongly localize the deficit to serratus anterior dysfunction.
bottom_line: This patient’s clinical picture-difficulty raising her right arm and visible scapular prominence when pressing against a wall-strongly suggests dysfunction of the serratus anterior muscle, most likely due to long thoracic nerve injury incurred during a prior tube thoracostomy.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother due to progressive difficulty seeing in school over the past few months. He reports frequently bumping into objects and says it’s harder to see things on the sides. He also complains of recent headaches and a decline in academic performance. His growth chart shows a noticeable drop in height percentile over the past year. On physical examination, confrontation testing reveals bilateral loss of peripheral vision. A brain MRI shows a suprasellar mass with calcifications and cystic components. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of a suprasellar mass in a pediatric patient-specifically, bitemporal hemianopsia (caused by optic chiasm compression) and symptoms consistent with pituitary dysfunction, such as delayed growth or puberty. These findings are hallmark features of craniopharyngioma, the most common pituitary region tumor in children and teenagers. Craniopharyngiomas arise from embryologic remnants of the Rathke pouch, a structure derived from the roof of the primitive oropharynx (oral ectoderm), making this an important embryologic association to recognize on USMLE. On imaging, craniopharyngiomas typically appear as calcified, cystic suprasellar masses. The cystic components may contain cholesterol crystals, and calcification is a frequent finding, which helps distinguish them from other pituitary or suprasellar lesions. These tumors are benign in histology but tend to be locally invasive and have a high rate of recurrence, often necessitating multiple surgeries or long-term follow-up.
bottom_line: This patient presents with classic signs of a suprasellar mass in a pediatric patient-specifically, bitemporal hemianopsia (caused by optic chiasm compression) and symptoms consistent with pituitary dysfunction, such as delayed growth or puberty.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 27-year-old graphic designer comes to the clinic with a 5-day history of visual blurring in her right eye. She was diagnosed with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis two years ago. She reports difficulty seeing fine details, especially in bright environments. On examination, both pupils are 6 mm in darkness and 4 mm in ambient light. When light is directed into the left eye, both pupils constrict to 2 mm. When the light is moved to the right eye, both pupils return to 4 mm. Extraocular movements are intact. No ptosis is noted. Which of the following structures is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient demonstrates a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD; aka Marcus-Gunn pupil) in the right eye, a classic early finding in optic neuritis, one of the most common visual manifestations of multiple sclerosis. In the swinging flashlight test, both pupils constrict when light is shown in the left eye (normal afferent input), but paradoxically dilate when the light is shown in the right eye. This is because the right optic nerve fails to deliver adequate afferent input. As a result, the brainstem generates a weaker parasympathetic efferent response, and both pupils constrict less. To understand this, recall the normal pupillary light reflex: light enters the eye and travels via the optic nerve (CN II) to the pretectal nucleus, which activates both Edinger-Westphal nuclei. These then send parasympathetic fibers via the oculomotor nerves (CN III) to the ciliary ganglia and onward to both pupillary constrictor muscles. When the afferent limb is impaired, the entire reflex is weakened, despite intact efferent output.
bottom_line: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: High Yield USMLE Audio Answer:

subject: Neuro
stem: A 6-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her father due to concerns about her vision. The child often tilts her head when trying to read and has trouble catching objects during play. She occasionally complains of seeing double, especially when focusing on things up close. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and there is no family history of eye disease. On ophthalmologic examination, her right eye is noted to deviate inward upon near gaze, while her left eye appears to fixate normally. Visual acuity is normal in both eyes, but the cover-uncover test demonstrates shifting fixation between the eyes. The remainder of her neurological examination is within normal limits. Which of the following best explains the underlying pathology responsible for this patient's presentation?
explanation: Strabismus is characterized by misalignment of the eyes due to dysfunction of extraocular muscles or their innervation, leading to one eye deviating inward (esotropia) or outward (exotropia) relative to the other. In children, this results in impaired binocular vision, loss of depth perception, and can cause diplopia (double vision) as the brain receives two non-fused images. If untreated, the brain suppresses the image from the misaligned eye, predisposing to amblyopia-a loss of visual acuity in the affected eye due to cortical adaptation.
bottom_line: Strabismus is characterized by misalignment of the eyes due to dysfunction of extraocular muscles or their innervation, leading to one eye deviating inward (esotropia) or outward (exotropia) relative to the other.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 63-year-old man is found in his living room clutching his head and vomiting repeatedly. His wife reports he had been complaining of the 'worst headache of his life' for the past hour. Neurological examination reveals a fully dilated right pupil that does not constrict in response to either direct or consensual light stimulation. The left pupil is normal and reactive. He is confused but able to follow simple commands. There is noticeable neck stiffness on passive flexion without fever or rash. Motor and sensory examinations are within normal limits; cranial nerve findings are otherwise unremarkable. There is no reported history of trauma, hypertension, or recent infections. Which vascular structure is most likely the origin of his acute symptoms?
explanation: The posterior communicating artery (PCOM) is the most common site of aneurysms that cause a third nerve palsy, presenting with an acute, severe headache, vomiting, and an ipsilateral dilated pupil that is non-reactive to light. Aneurysms at this location compress the oculomotor nerve (cranial nerve III), particularly the superficial parasympathetic fibers responsible for pupillary constriction. The oculomotor nerve passes between the posterior cerebral artery and the superior cerebellar artery, but the PCOM is the classic site for aneurysmal compression leading to isolated pupillary involvement. Subarachnoid hemorrhage from rupture can also cause meningeal irritation, resulting in nuchal rigidity without evidence of infection. The mechanism involves arterial blood under high pressure escaping into the subarachnoid space, irritating the meninges and causing classic signs such as neck stiffness, photophobia, and mental status changes. The third nerve palsy from PCOM aneurysm often presents as a 'blown' pupil (mydriasis), ptosis, and 'down and out' eye deviation, but the earliest and most reliable sign is the fixed, dilated pupil due to superficial parasympathetic fiber compression. Prompt recognition is critical as aneurysmal rupture carries a high mortality rate, necessitating neurosurgical intervention and blood pressure stabilization.
bottom_line: The posterior communicating artery (PCOM) is the most common site of aneurysms that cause a third nerve palsy, presenting with an acute, severe headache, vomiting, and an ipsilateral dilated pupil that is non-reactive to light.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with involuntary movements of her left arm and leg that started suddenly. On exam, she has large, flinging, ballistic movements of the left upper and lower extremity. Muscle strength, tone, and sensation are otherwise normal. MRI of the brain shows a small lacunar infarct. Which of the following areas is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient has hemiballismus, characterized by violent, flinging movements of the contralateral limbs. Hemiballismus results from a lesion of the subthalamic nucleus, part of the indirect pathway of the basal ganglia that normally suppresses movement. Loss of subthalamic inhibition leads to excessive movement. Because the symptoms affect the left limbs, the lesion must be in the right subthalamic nucleus. Small lacunar strokes from hypertensive microvascular disease are the most common cause.
bottom_line: This patient has hemiballismus, characterized by violent, flinging movements of the contralateral limbs.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) is caused by rotational acceleration-deceleration forces that shear axons, primarily at the gray-white matter junction, corpus callosum, and brainstem. The mechanical disruption of cytoskeletal elements impairs axonal transport, leading to axonal swelling, disconnection, and degeneration. CT imaging may show punctate hemorrhages at sites of high shear stress, but early imaging can often be deceptively normal despite severe clinical findings. Patients with DAI typically present in coma immediately after trauma and fail to regain consciousness due to widespread disconnection between cortical and subcortical structures. There is no focal lesion amenable to surgical treatment, and management is supportive; prognosis is generally poor, especially in severe cases. Unlike focal injuries that cause localized bleeding or contusions, DAI is often radiographically subtle on CT but evident on MRI as numerous small lesions. The clinical hallmark is prolonged loss of consciousness at the time of injury without a lucid interval, differentiating it from conditions such as epidural hematoma. Because of the widespread nature of the injury, there is no focal neurologic deficit; rather, patients present with diffuse dysfunction such as persistent vegetative state or death. Recognition of this entity is key to understanding prognosis after traumatic brain injury.
bottom_line: Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) is caused by rotational acceleration-deceleration forces that shear axons, primarily at the gray-white matter junction, corpus callosum, and brainstem.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 25-year-old primigravida presents for a routine prenatal ultrasound at 22 weeks’ gestation. Fetal imaging reveals an enlarged posterior fossa with cystic dilation where the fourth ventricle should be, along with underdevelopment of midline cerebellar structures and displacement of surrounding brain tissue. The lateral ventricles appear enlarged due to secondary hydrocephalus. The remainder of the fetal anatomy is unremarkable. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated thus far, and she takes only prenatal vitamins. The findings are most likely due to a developmental failure of which of the following embryologic brain regions?

options.A: Failure of cerebral hemispheres to separate
options.B: Failure of closure of the anterior neuropore
options.C: Failure of neural crest migration into the forebrain
options.D: Failure of prosencephalon to properly evaginate

explanation: The findings in this fetus-enlarged posterior fossa, cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, and hypoplasia of the cerebellar vermis-are classic for Dandy-Walker malformation. This condition arises due to impaired development and differentiation of the rhombencephalon (hindbrain) during embryogenesis. Specifically, the cerebellar vermis fails to form, and the fourth ventricle enlarges into a posterior fossa cyst. This structural disruption can lead to obstructive hydrocephalus and abnormal brainstem development, both of which may result in motor, cognitive, and coordination deficits postnatally.postnatally.
bottom_line: The findings in this fetus-enlarged posterior fossa, cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, and hypoplasia of the cerebellar vermis-are classic for Dandy-Walker malformation.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 68-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of right thigh pain and difficulty climbing stairs. He describes a dull ache in the anterior thigh that occasionally radiates toward the medial knee. The pain is aggravated by walking uphill and improves with rest. He also reports that his right leg feels weaker, especially when trying to rise from a chair or step onto a curb. He has no bowel or bladder incontinence, saddle anesthesia, or back trauma. His medical history includes long-standing lumbar osteoarthritis and poorly controlled hypertension. On physical examination, strength testing reveals 4/5 strength with right knee extension, while hip flexion and ankle movements are intact. The right patellar reflex is diminished compared to the left. Sensation to pinprick and light touch is reduced over the anterior thigh and medial shin. Straight leg raise test is negative. An MRI of the lumbar spine is ordered to evaluate suspected radiculopathy. Injury to which of the following spinal nerve root levels best explains this patient’s findings?
explanation: The L3-L4 nerve root level innervates the quadriceps muscle group, which is responsible for knee extension and partially assists in hip flexion. It also provides sensory innervation to the anterior thigh and medial leg. The patellar reflex is mediated by this root level, so diminished or absent patellar reflex is a key clinical sign. This patient’s constellation of symptoms-difficulty extending the knee, anterior thigh pain, and sensory loss over the medial leg-is classic for L3-L4 radiculopathy. The most common cause of L3-L4 radiculopathy is intervertebral disc herniation or foraminal narrowing due to degenerative changes at the L3-L4 disc space. Unlike more distal root involvement (e.g., L5 or S1), which affects the foot or toes, L3-L4 radiculopathy causes proximal leg weakness that interferes with movements like rising from a chair or climbing stairs. These symptoms are often mistaken for hip pathology unless a thorough neurologic exam is performed. Diagnosis is supported by MRI showing compression at the appropriate disc level. Treatment includes NSAIDs, physical therapy, and possibly epidural steroid injections. Most cases resolve without surgery, but significant motor deficits or failure to improve may require decompression. Recognizing L3-L4 involvement is critical to ensuring appropriate management and avoiding unnecessary hip interventions.
bottom_line: The L3-L4 nerve root level innervates the quadriceps muscle group, which is responsible for knee extension and partially assists in hip flexion.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 76-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his daughter after she noticed new left-sided weakness and confusion. The patient has a history of Alzheimer disease. He lives independently but has had multiple falls over the past year. She also mentions that he had a prior stroke six months ago and his memory has been declining rapidly. On examination, he has decreased strength in the left upper and lower extremities and is disoriented to time. His blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg, heart rate is 72/min, respiratory rate is 14/min, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. Which of the following factors in this patient’s history most likely increased his risk of developing this condition?
explanation: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy is the most common cause of spontaneous lobar hemorrhages in patients over 60 years of age, particularly those with Alzheimer disease. It results from β-amyloid deposition in the walls of small to medium-sized cortical vessels, leading to vessel fragility and a tendency to bleed. This condition typically causes recurrent hemorrhagic strokes in the cortical or subcortical regions. Alzheimer disease is a well-established risk factor for cerebral amyloid angiopathy, and patients often present with stepwise cognitive decline due to repeated micro- and macrohemorrhages. The presence of multiple prior strokes, advanced age, and Alzheimer disease increases suspicion for cerebral amyloid angiopathy in this patient. His imaging and clinical course align with this pathology. The mechanism involves weakened cerebral vessels prone to rupture, especially in cortical areas, and these bleeds are typically non-traumatic. The condition is also associated with progressive cognitive decline. Importantly, cerebral amyloid angiopathy leads to slow accumulating hemorrhage, and elderly patients have reduced cerebral volume, making their bridging veins and small cortical vessels more vulnerable. While other factors like trauma and anticoagulation can increase bleeding risk, amyloid deposition remains the key factor in this case.
bottom_line: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy is the most common cause of spontaneous lobar hemorrhages in patients over 60 years of age, particularly those with Alzheimer disease.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 31-year-old woman presents with progressively worsening headaches, nausea, and transient blurred vision for 1 week. She reports recent weight gain and has a BMI of 37. Her blood pressure is 146/92 mmHg, and pulse is 57/min. On physical examination, she has bilateral sixth nerve palsy and visual obscurations with bending over. Fundoscopic examination reveals papilledema and loss of spontaneous venous pulsations. Non-contrast CT scan of the head reveals no masses, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus. A lumbar puncture is performed, showing an opening pressure of 315 mmH2O, with normal CSF composition. She is not currently taking any medications, including oral contraceptives or vitamin supplements. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?
explanation: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), also known as pseudotumor cerebri, is characterized by increased intracranial pressure (ICP) without radiographic evidence of mass lesion or hydrocephalus and with normal cerebrospinal fluid composition. It classically affects overweight women of childbearing age and presents with headache, transient visual disturbances (including diplopia due to cranial nerve VI palsy), papilledema, and sometimes pulsatile tinnitus.
bottom_line: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), also known as pseudotumor cerebri, is characterized by increased intracranial pressure (ICP) without radiographic evidence of mass lesion or hydrocephalus and with normal cerebrospinal fluid composition.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 36-year-old construction worker is brought to the emergency department after falling from a scaffold and landing on his right arm. He reports pain and swelling along the mid-humerus. On examination, there is bruising and tenderness over the midshaft of the right humerus. The patient is unable to extend his wrist or fingers on the right side. His forearm is pronated at rest, and he is unable to make a firm grip. Sensation is decreased over the dorsum of the hand. Elbow flexion and shoulder movements are intact. Radiographs reveal a spiral fracture of the midshaft humerus. Based on the diagram provided, which of the labeled nerves (A-E) is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This patient presents with classic features of a radial nerve injury, most commonly associated with midshaft humeral fractures, especially those involving the radial groove. The radial nerve innervates the extensors of the wrist, fingers, and elbow. Injury at this level disrupts motor control of the extensors, leading to the hallmark sign: wrist drop, where the hand hangs in a pronated, flexed position due to unopposed flexor tone. The inability to extend the wrist and fingers, along with a pronated forearm and weak grip, is diagnostic for radial nerve palsy. Additionally, sensation over the dorsal hand is provided by the superficial branch of the radial nerve, which explains the patient's sensory loss. Elbow flexion is preserved because the injury occurs distal to the triceps innervation, and shoulder function is spared as it is mediated by the axillary nerve. This injury mechanism-falling on the arm with a spiral humeral fracture-is a classic high-yield trigger tested on USMLE. In the brachial plexus diagram, label C corresponds to the radial nerve, which emerges from the posterior cord and courses along the radial groove of the humerus, making it vulnerable in such trauma.
bottom_line: This patient presents with classic features of a radial nerve injury, most commonly associated with midshaft humeral fractures, especially those involving the radial groove.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the urgent care clinic after twisting his ankle while trail running. He states that his right foot rolled inward when he landed on uneven ground, and he felt a sharp pulling sensation along the outer part of his lower leg. He applied ice at home and was able to walk, although with some discomfort. He denies numbness or weakness in the toes, thigh pain, or back symptoms. He has no history of prior injuries or chronic joint disease. On examination, there is mild tenderness along the lateral lower leg without swelling or ecchymosis. The patient has normal strength in dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and inversion, but eversion of the foot is weak compared to the contralateral side. Sensation is decreased over the lateral lower leg and dorsum of the foot, while deep tendon reflexes are intact. There are no skin lesions, and no proximal motor or sensory deficits are noted. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient presents with isolated eversion weakness and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral lower leg following an inversion-type ankle injury. These findings are characteristic of injury to the superficial peroneal nerve, which innervates the lateral compartment of the leg, including the fibularis (peroneus) longus and brevis muscles, responsible for eversion of the foot. On USMLE, superficial peroneal nerve is the answer if the patient loses only eversion of the foot. The preserved dorsiflexion rules out involvement of the deep peroneal nerve, which supplies the anterior compartment muscles, including the tibialis anterior. Likewise, the absence of plantarflexion or inversion deficits indicates that the tibial nerve is intact. The superficial peroneal nerve also provides cutaneous sensation to the dorsum of the foot (except the first web space) and lateral lower leg, consistent with the sensory loss described. This type of injury commonly occurs due to stretching or entrapment of the superficial peroneal nerve during ankle inversion injuries or direct trauma to the lateral leg. The nerve becomes superficial in the lower leg, making it vulnerable in this region. Symptoms are often mild and may resolve with conservative management, though persistent weakness or numbness may require further workup.
bottom_line: This patient presents with isolated eversion weakness and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral lower leg following an inversion-type ankle injury.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with new-onset headache and scalp tenderness for two weeks. The pain is localized to her right temporal region and is described as a constant, dull ache. She reports associated jaw discomfort when chewing, as well as generalized fatigue and weight loss. Over the past few days, she has also noticed transient blurring of vision in her right eye. On examination, there is tenderness over the right temple with decreased pulsation of the right temporal artery. The patient’s erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is markedly elevated at 85 mm/hr. She has no history of migraine, cluster headache, or recent trauma. Neurological examination is otherwise unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis) is a granulomatous large-vessel vasculitis that primarily affects older adults, particularly women over 50 years of age. It typically involves branches of the external carotid artery, most notably the temporal artery, leading to symptoms such as new-onset headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and constitutional symptoms including fatigue and weight loss. A critical complication is ischemic optic neuropathy due to involvement of the ophthalmic artery, which can cause irreversible vision loss if not promptly treated. Histopathologically, this condition features intimal thickening, elastic lamina fragmentation, and multinucleated giant cells within the vessel wall. Diagnosis is strongly suggested by clinical presentation and elevated inflammatory markers such as ESR or CRP but confirmed by temporal artery biopsy showing granulomatous inflammation. Immediate initiation of high-dose corticosteroids before biopsy is essential to prevent permanent vision loss. Patients may also have associated polymyalgia rheumatica, manifesting as shoulder and hip girdle stiffness.
bottom_line: Temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis) is a granulomatous large-vessel vasculitis that primarily affects older adults, particularly women over 50 years of age.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 34-year-old woman comes to the emergency department due to worsening symptoms of pelvic inflammatory disease. She was diagnosed with chlamydial cervicitis one week ago and started oral doxycycline, but has had persistent lower abdominal pain and fever. She has no significant past medical history but takes ibuprofen occasionally for menstrual cramps. Family history is notable for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother and a sibling with type 1 diabetes. On arrival, her temperature is 38.4°C (101.1°F), and pelvic examination shows cervical motion tenderness. She receives an intramuscular injection of ceftriaxone in the right gluteal region. Several hours later, she reports new-onset numbness in her right leg and weakness in foot dorsiflexion. Examination shows decreased strength in the right foot with impaired ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. Sensation is reduced over the posterior thigh, leg, and foot. Reflexes are intact. Which of the following nerves was most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: An improperly placed gluteal injection can injure the sciatic nerve, producing weakness below the knee with sensory loss in the posterior thigh, leg, and foot. The combination of dorsiflexion and plantarflexion weakness after an IM gluteal injection is most consistent with sciatic nerve injury.
bottom_line: Improper gluteal injection can injure the sciatic nerve and cause motor and sensory deficits below the knee.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 68-year-old woman undergoes a total hip arthroplasty via the anterior approach for advanced osteoarthritis. The procedure is uneventful, and she is transferred to the recovery unit. On postoperative day 1, she reports new-onset difficulty walking and feels that her leg “keeps giving out” when trying to stand. She denies numbness in the foot or ankle, and her ability to dorsiflex and plantarflex the foot is intact. On neurologic exam, strength is reduced in the right thigh adductors, and she has difficulty crossing her legs while seated. Sensation is decreased along the medial aspect of the thigh, but there is no sensory deficit in the lower leg or foot. Reflexes are normal, and there is no evidence of hematoma or surgical site infection. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: The obturator nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and passes through the obturator foramen to innervate the medial compartment of the thigh. It provides motor innervation to the adductor muscles (adductor longus, brevis, magnus, gracilis, and obturator externus) and sensory innervation to the medial thigh. Injury to the obturator nerve most commonly occurs during pelvic or anterior hip surgeries, such as total hip arthroplasty, pelvic lymph node dissection, or obstetric procedures. In this patient, the nerve was likely injured during anterior hip replacement. The clinical features include weakness with thigh adduction, making it difficult to bring the legs together or cross the legs while seated, and sensory loss over the medial thigh. The patient’s motor and sensory deficits are confined to this distribution, while distal leg function remains intact-she can dorsiflex, plantarflex, and has no numbness in the foot, ruling out more distal nerve involvement. Importantly, reflexes remain normal, and there's no hematoma or structural complication at the surgical site, further localizing the issue to a focal nerve injury. Most cases of obturator nerve palsy improve over time, though physical therapy and precautions to prevent falls are essential in the interim.
bottom_line: The obturator nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and passes through the obturator foramen to innervate the medial compartment of the thigh.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 61-year-old man presents after sudden onset of vertigo, nausea, and unsteady gait. On neurologic exam, he has right-sided facial weakness, decreased sensation over the right side of his face, hearing loss in the right ear, and unsteady gait with falling toward the right. He also has decreased pain and temperature sensation on the left side of his body. Proprioception, strength, and extraocular movements are normal. MRI shows a small infarct in the pons. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?
explanation: This patient has right lateral pontine syndrome, caused by infarction of the anterior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA), which supplies the lateral pons. The most important localizing sign is ipsilateral facial weakness (Bell’s palsy) due to involvement of the facial nucleus (CN VII). Facial weakness is the key clinical clue that helps distinguish lateral pontine syndrome from lateral medullary syndrome. Involvement of the vestibular nuclei causes vertigo and imbalance, while cochlear nuclei damage produces hearing loss. The spinal trigeminal nucleus is involved, leading to ipsilateral facial sensory loss. Injury to the spinothalamic tract causes contralateral body pain and temperature loss. The middle cerebellar peduncle is affected as well, leading to ipsilateral limb ataxia and falling to the right. The mnemonic “FACIAL” helps recall that the Face is affected, and AICA is embedded backwards inside the word FACIAL.
bottom_line: This patient has right lateral pontine syndrome, caused by infarction of the anterior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA), which supplies the lateral pons.

subject: Neuro
stem: ccessory nerve B. Axillary nerve C. Dorsal scapular nerve D. Long thoracic nerve E. Lower subscapular nerve F. Musculocutaneous nerve G. Radial nerve H. Suprascapular nerve Submit A 26-year-old man comes to the clinic with difficulty lifting his left arm above his head and worsening shoulder discomfort. He is a boxer and reports that the symptoms began gradually after increasing his training intensity. He has no history of trauma, cervical spine symptoms, or prior surgeries. On physical examination, there is prominence of the left scapula when he is asked to push against the wall with outstretched arms. Strength testing reveals preserved shoulder abduction and normal sensation in the upper extremity. There is no muscle wasting or fasciculations. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This patient has a winged scapula, evidenced by the medial border of the scapula lifting away from the thoracic wall when pushing against resistance. This is due to paralysis of the serratus anterior muscle, which anchors the scapula to the chest wall and facilitates upward rotation necessary for full overhead abduction. The long thoracic nerve (C5-C7), which innervates the serratus anterior, is uniquely susceptible to injury because it courses superficially along the lateral thoracic wall. It can be injured by repetitive trauma, surgical procedures (e.g., mastectomy), or activities involving prolonged or forceful shoulder movement, such as boxing or weightlifting. Because the long thoracic nerve is a pure motor nerve, sensation is preserved, as in this case. Other shoulder muscles such as the deltoid or rotator cuff may compensate partially, but patients still notice weakness or discomfort with overhead movements. Winging is accentuated when patients perform a wall push test. Management is typically conservative, with physical therapy; severe or persistent cases may require surgical intervention.
bottom_line: This patient has a winged scapula, evidenced by the medial border of the scapula lifting away from the thoracic wall when pushing against resistance.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department after falling off his bike and striking his right upper arm on a concrete curb. He reports immediate pain and swelling in the upper arm but denies loss of consciousness or other injuries. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness localized to the upper portion of the right humerus. Passive and active range of motion at the elbow and wrist is intact, but the patient is unable to lift his arm away from his body beyond 15 degrees. Sensation is decreased over the lateral aspect of the right upper arm. The contour of the shoulder appears slightly flattened compared to the left. The handgrip is preserved, and distal pulses are intact. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This patient presents with clinical findings consistent with axillary nerve injury, most likely due to a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. The axillary nerve arises from the C5-C6 nerve roots and innervates the deltoid muscle, which is responsible for shoulder abduction between 15-90 degrees. The patient’s inability to lift the arm beyond the initial range of motion, along with flattening of the shoulder contour, strongly suggests deltoid muscle dysfunction caused by axillary nerve damage. In addition, the decreased sensation over the lateral upper arm supports involvement of the axillary nerve, which provides cutaneous innervation to the skin overlying the deltoid region. This pattern of motor and sensory deficits-impaired shoulder abduction and sensory loss over the deltoid-is frequently tested on USMLE and is considered classic for axillary nerve injury. The flattened appearance of the shoulder further reinforces this diagnosis, reflecting underlying deltoid atrophy or paralysis due to nerve injury. Importantly, this type of injury is most commonly seen with trauma to the surgical neck of the humerus, a region where the axillary nerve winds around the bone.
bottom_line: This patient presents with clinical findings consistent with axillary nerve injury, most likely due to a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of persistent numbness along the inside of her right lower leg that began four days ago. She reports recently returning from a three-day road trip where she spent long hours in the passenger seat with her legs tightly crossed. Since returning home, she has noticed an area on her right leg that “feels like it’s asleep” and hasn’t improved. She denies any pain, weakness, tingling in the foot or thigh, or trouble with walking. She has no history of diabetes, spinal disorders, or recent trauma. On examination, strength and reflexes in the lower extremities are normal and symmetric. The only abnormal finding is reduced pinprick sensation along the medial aspect of the right lower leg, extending from just below the knee to the upper ankle. There is no swelling, redness, or muscle atrophy. Proprioception is intact, and gait is normal. No deficits are noted in the foot, thigh, or contralateral limb. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient presents with isolated sensory loss over the medial lower leg after prolonged sitting with legs crossed - a posture that can cause nerve compression, particularly of superficial cutaneous nerves. The saphenous nerve, a pure sensory branch of the femoral nerve, provides cutaneous innervation to the medial leg and ankle and is the most likely structure injured here. It becomes superficial as it exits the adductor canal and travels along the medial tibia, where it can be compressed by posture or external pressure. The absence of motor findings is key. The saphenous nerve does not supply any muscles, so strength, reflexes, and gait remain completely normal. The numbness described - localized to the medial leg with no extension to the foot or thigh - is classic for saphenous nerve involvement and not consistent with radiculopathy or injury to deeper mixed nerves. Compression neuropathies from habitual postures (e.g., tight leg crossing or kneeling) are well-described causes of mononeuropathies in otherwise healthy individuals. Importantly, no other structures match this precise sensory distribution without associated motor deficits. orrect answer explanation / general explanation:
bottom_line: This patient presents with isolated sensory loss over the medial lower leg after prolonged sitting with legs crossed - a posture that can cause nerve compression, particularly of superficial cutaneous nerves.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 44-year-old man comes to the clinic because food has tasted bland for the past 2 months, ever since he was in a minor car accident in which his head struck the steering wheel. He did not lose consciousness at the time. The rest of the neurologic and physical examination is unremarkable. Which of the following cranial nerves is most likely affected in this patient?
explanation: Although the patient perceives “loss of taste,” flavor depends far more on olfaction than on the tongue’s gustatory pathways. The olfactory nerve (cranial nerve I) transmits sensory fibers from the nasal epithelium through the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone to the olfactory bulb. In head trauma such as a car accident, shearing forces can sever these delicate fibers as they cross the cribriform plate, leading to anosmia or hyposmia. When smell is impaired, patients experience food as bland or tasteless even though the taste buds innervated by cranial nerves VII, IX, and X remain intact. This patient’s car accident provides a clear mechanism for olfactory nerve injury. The fact that he has an otherwise unremarkable neurologic and physical examination, implying normal facial movement, normal tongue motion, and intact swallowing, argues strongly against facial, glossopharyngeal, hypoglossal, or vagus nerve lesions. True isolated taste loss from gustatory nerve dysfunction is rare, while olfactory nerve injury from trauma is a well-established cause of this symptom.
bottom_line: Although the patient perceives “loss of taste,” flavor depends far more on olfaction than on the tongue’s gustatory pathways.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 69-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his wife because of progressive difficulty with daily activities. Over the past 3 years, he has developed stiffness in his limbs, slowed movements, and a tendency to shuffle when walking. He also reports reduced sense of smell, constipation, and difficulty initiating sleep. His wife has noticed that his facial expression is often “blank,” and that he sometimes takes several seconds to respond to questions. He denies recent head trauma, alcohol use, or family history of similar symptoms. On examination, he has a resting tremor of the right hand that diminishes with voluntary movement, cogwheel rigidity of the upper extremities, and bradykinesia. His gait is stooped with decreased arm swing. Cognitive testing reveals slowed processing but intact orientation. MRI of the brain shows age-appropriate atrophy without focal lesions. Degeneration of neurons that primarily release which of the following neurotransmitters is most closely associated with this patient’s condition?
explanation: This patient presents with isolated sensory loss over the lower lateral leg, with normal motor function, reflexes, and proximal sensation, following prolonged postural compression. These findings strongly indicate an injury to the sural nerve, a purely sensory nerve that innervates the lower lateral calf and lateral foot. The sural nerve is formed from branches of the tibial and common peroneal nerves and is commonly injured through compression near the posterior calf or ankle, especially during prolonged kneeling or tight leg positions. The distribution of numbness in this case - confined to the lower lateral calf - is classic for sural neuropathy. Importantly, there are no motor deficits, ruling out involvement of the parent nerves themselves. The sural nerve is superficial along the posterolateral leg and vulnerable during meditation, yoga, or positions involving direct pressure or stretch on the calf. Its function is entirely sensory, so patients will never present with weakness or gait disturbance. The absence of sensory deficits in the medial leg (saphenous nerve) or upper lateral leg (common peroneal nerve) helps localize the lesion more precisely. A key distinction tested on USMLE is between sural and saphenous neuropathy, with lateral calf loss pointing to the sural branch and medial loss indicating saphenous involvement. The mnemonic “suraL is Lateral” helps differentiate the two. The sural nerve can also be tested on USMLE as a donor nerve for grafting due to its purely sensory function and ease of access, making it ideal for autologous nerve repair procedures. Its removal does not result in any motor deficit, which is why it's commonly harvested in clinical practice.
bottom_line: This patient presents with isolated sensory loss over the lower lateral leg, with normal motor function, reflexes, and proximal sensation, following prolonged postural compression.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of numbness on the outside of her right lower leg that she noticed after returning from a weekend yoga retreat. She recalls spending an extended amount of time in kneeling and cross- legged positions during several seated meditation sessions but denies any falls or trauma.
