The numbness began gradually and has remained localized. She reports no pain, weakness, tingling in the foot, or balance problems. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. On examination, the patient has normal strength, reflexes, and range of motion throughout both lower extremities. Pinprick sensation is diminished over the lower lateral portion of the right calf, just above the lateral malleolus. Sensation is intact elsewhere, including the foot, medial leg, and upper lateral thigh. Gait is steady, and there is no tenderness or swelling of the leg. No deficits are observed proximally or contralaterally. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: The sural nerve is a purely sensory nerve supplying the lower lateral calf and lateral foot. External compression from kneeling or cross-legged positioning can cause isolated sensory loss in that distribution without weakness or reflex changes.
bottom_line: Sural neuropathy causes isolated sensory loss over the lower lateral calf and lateral foot without motor deficits.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to difficulty drinking fluids without dribbling from the left corner of her mouth and a strange loss of taste when eating. She states that food tastes “dull” on the front of her tongue but normal at the back. She denies headache, fever, or recent illness. Examination reveals decreased taste sensation on the left anterior tongue, incomplete closure of the left eyelid, and mild asymmetry of her smile on the left. There is no sensory loss over the face, no tongue deviation, and normal jaw strength. This patient’s condition is most likely due to damage to a cranial nerve that is also responsible for which of the following?
explanation: The facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) provides motor innervation to the muscles of facial expression, including orbicularis oculi, which acts as the efferent limb of the corneal reflex. It also carries special sensory fibers from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the chorda tympani branch, as well as parasympathetic fibers to the lacrimal, submandibular, and sublingual glands, and innervates the stapedius muscle of the middle ear. The constellation of decreased taste on the anterior tongue, mild facial asymmetry, and incomplete eyelid closure localizes the lesion to the facial nerve, likely affecting both motor and sensory components. Cranial nerve VII exits the brainstem at the pontomedullary junction, travels through the internal acoustic meatus, and exits the skull via the stylomastoid foramen. Peripheral facial nerve lesions (e.g., Bell palsy) produce weakness involving both the upper and lower face, whereas central lesions (e.g., stroke) spare the upper face due to bilateral cortical input to the frontalis muscle. The facial nerve’s role as the efferent limb of the corneal reflex explains why facial nerve injury can lead to failure of eye closure when the cornea is stimulated.
bottom_line: The facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) provides motor innervation to the muscles of facial expression, including orbicularis oculi, which acts as the efferent limb of the corneal reflex.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 65-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset lower back pain and weakness in both legs. She reports that the pain began abruptly this morning and has been accompanied by tingling and numbness in her inner thighs. Over the past few hours, she has developed difficulty initiating urination and describes a strange loss of sensation around her anus. She also says she feels “clumsy” when trying to walk. On examination, her lower extremities show bilateral weakness (4/5) and brisk reflexes. There is diminished sensation in the perianal region and inner thighs. Rectal tone is decreased. An urgent spinal MRI is obtained. Which of the following best explains this patient's condition?

options.A: Bilateral compression of the cauda equina nerve roots
options.B: Compression of the terminal portion of the spinal cord at L1
options.C: Demyelination of the thoracic dorsal columns
options.D: Ischemic infarction of the anterior spinal artery
options.E: Transverse inflammation of the lower spinal cord

answer_text: Compression of the terminal portion of the spinal cord at L1
explanation: This patient presents with conus medullaris syndrome (CMS), characterized by sudden-onset bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and perianal sensory loss. The conus medullaris, located around the L1-L2 vertebral level, contains the sacral segments that innervate the bladder, bowel, and perineum. Compression or infarction here leads to mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs, such as brisk reflexes and flaccid anal tone. In contrast to cauda equina syndrome, which develops gradually and involves peripheral nerve roots, CMS has a rapid onset with symmetric deficits. In this case, the combination of perianal anesthesia, decreased rectal tone, and bilateral motor weakness points toward CMS. The key distinction for USMLE between conus medullaris and cauda equina syndromes is that conus medullaris syndrome presents with sudden-onset symptoms and perianal anesthesia, whereas cauda equina syndrome has a gradual onset with saddle anesthesia; both can involve leg pain and urinary retention, but USMLE focuses mainly on this tempo and sensory distribution. Immediate MRI is essential for diagnosis, and prompt neurosurgical decompression is necessary to preserve neurologic function. Anatomy
bottom_line: This patient presents with conus medullaris syndrome (CMS), characterized by sudden-onset bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and perianal sensory loss.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 67-year-old right-handed man is brought to the emergency department after being found disoriented and speaking nonsensically. According to his wife, he was watching TV when he began talking "in gibberish" and seemed confused. He was unable to follow simple commands, and he became increasingly frustrated when people did not understand him. On exam, he speaks in long, fluent sentences with intact grammar and prosody, but his words lack meaning and are often nonsensical. He appears unaware of his language deficit. Motor and sensory strength are intact in all extremities.The diagram belows show the location of the lesion. Which of the following brain regions is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient demonstrates Wernicke aphasia, characterized by fluent speech that is grammatically intact but meaningless, with impaired comprehension and lack of awareness of the deficit (anosognosia). The lesion localizes to Wernicke’s area, located in the posterior superior temporal gyrus of the dominant (usually left) hemisphere, supplied by the inferior division of the middle cerebral artery (MCA). This type of aphasia contrasts with Broca’s aphasia, which involves non-fluent but meaningful speech with preserved comprehension. Since there are no motor or sensory deficits, the infarct likely involves the temporal branch of the left MCA, sparing the primary motor and sensory cortices. High-yield associations include paraphasic errors, poor repetition, and word salad-like speech. Because patients with Wernicke aphasia are often unaware of their deficit, they may appear confused or agitated when misunderstood. Types of Aphasia: 1) Broca Aphasia: Non-fluent, impaired repetition, but intact comprehension. Patient understands but struggles to speak. When asked, “What’s your name?” they reply, “Uh… my… na… name… J… John…”-effortful, broken speech. Frustrated, since they know what they want to say. 2) Wernicke Aphasia: Fluent speech, impaired repetition, and poor comprehension. Patient speaks fluently but makes no sense. You ask, “What’s your name?” and they respond, “Yeah the sky is blue… car… alphabet soup…”-nonsensical yet fluent. Not frustrated, unaware of deficit. 3) Conduction Aphasia: Only repetition is impaired. Speech is fluent, comprehension intact. Asked to repeat “apple,” patient says “ap… ape… applesauce…”-can’t repeat despite otherwise normal language. 4) Transcortical Motor Aphasia: Like Broca but repetition is preserved. Patient struggles to generate speech but can repeat accurately. You say “cat,” they say “C… cat.” Key clue: Broca-like output, but repetition intact. 5) Transcortical Sensory Aphasia: Like Wernicke but repetition is preserved. Patient produces nonsensical fluent speech but repeats perfectly. You say “banana,” they repeat “Banana!”-still no comprehension. 6) Mixed Transcortical Aphasia: Like global aphasia, but repetition is intact. Very limited speech and poor comprehension, but patient can still repeat words like “dog” when prompted. 7) Global Aphasia: Non-fluent, poor comprehension, and impaired repetition. Patient can barely speak or understand and gives minimal to no meaningful response-e.g., “…uh… huh…” when asked their name. Severe across all domains.
bottom_line: This patient demonstrates Wernicke aphasia, characterized by fluent speech that is grammatically intact but meaningless, with impaired comprehension and lack of awareness of the deficit (anosognosia).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the clinic with several days of tingling and aching pain in her right leg. She reports that the symptoms began after she started taking long evening walks in new running shoes that feel tighter than her previous pair. She describes a burning pain along the outer aspect of her right lower leg and top of her foot, which worsens with prolonged walking. She denies any recent trauma, falls, or back pain. She has also felt more fatigued lately due to final exams, eating poorly, and sleeping only 4-5 hours per night. On examination, she has mild tenderness to palpation over the lateral compartment of the leg. There is slightly reduced strength with foot eversion, but dorsiflexion and toe extension remain normal. Her gait is unaffected, and she does not demonstrate foot drop. Sensory testing reveals decreased sensation over the anterolateral shin and dorsum of the foot, excluding the web space between the first and second toes. Passive ankle inversion reproduces the tingling sensation. There is no calf tenderness or swelling, and pulses are symmetric bilaterally. Tinel’s sign is positive when tapping just anterior to the fibula. The rest of the neurologic exam is unremarkable. She is advised to rest, switch to wider shoes, and take NSAIDs as needed. Which of the following nerves is most likely affected?
explanation: The superficial peroneal nerve arises from the common peroneal nerve and supplies the fibularis longus and brevis muscles, which are responsible for foot eversion, as well as sensation to the lower lateral leg and dorsum of the foot (excluding the web space between the first and second toes). Injury to this nerve most commonly occurs due to entrapment or compression as it exits the lateral compartment of the leg-often caused by tight footwear, prolonged walking, or repetitive lateral leg strain. This patient’s new shoes and long walks are classic triggers. The nerve can be compressed where it pierces the deep fascia to become subcutaneous in the distal third of the leg. Clinically, patients present with burning or tingling pain over the lateral shin and dorsum of the foot, decreased eversion strength, and numbness sparing the first web space. In contrast to common peroneal nerve injury, dorsiflexion and toe extension are preserved, which helps distinguish this more distal lesion. A positive Tinel’s sign over the fibular area and reproduction of symptoms with ankle inversion further support the diagnosis. Mild weakness in eversion is often the only motor finding. The patient’s additional stress and poor nutrition can exacerbate the perception of pain and prolong nerve recovery, but they are not the primary cause. Initial management includes eliminating compressive triggers (e.g., changing footwear), NSAIDs, and activity modification. Most patients improve without invasive intervention, though persistent symptoms may warrant nerve conduction studies or, rarely, surgical decompression.
bottom_line: The superficial peroneal nerve arises from the common peroneal nerve and supplies the fibularis longus and brevis muscles, which are responsible for foot eversion, as well as sensation to the lower lateral leg and dorsum of the foot (excluding the web space between the first and second toes).

subject: Neuro
topic: Normal structure and function of the nervous system
stem: A 42-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his spouse because of progressive weakness over the past 5 days. One week ago, he developed low- grade fever, abdominal cramps, and watery diarrhea that resolved spontaneously after 2 days. Two days later, he began to notice tingling in his toes and difficulty climbing stairs. His weakness has since progressed upward to involve his thighs, and he now has trouble rising from a chair without using his arms. He reports no changes in vision, speech, or swallowing, but says his legs feel “heavy” and “sluggish.” He denies urinary incontinence or retention but has noticed mild shortness of breath when lying flat at night. On examination, his temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 130/78 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 20/min. Neurologic exam shows symmetric weakness in the lower extremities, diminished patellar and Achilles reflexes bilaterally, and decreased vibration sense in the toes. Upper extremity strength is normal, but deep tendon reflexes are absent in the arms as well. Cranial nerve exam is normal. Sensation to pinprick is preserved throughout. Nerve conduction studies are ordered, which reveal markedly slowed conduction velocities in peripheral nerves. Which of the following best explains the impaired action potentials in the affected nerves?

options.D: Disruption of synaptic vesicle docking at terminals

answer_text: Disruption of synaptic vesicle docking at terminals
explanation: This patient has thalamic pain syndrome (Dejerine-Roussy syndrome), which occurs as a delayed complication of a prior thalamic stroke. The infarct damages the left VPL/VPM nuclei, which receive sensory input from the right side of the body and face (contralateral). Initially, patients present with pure sensory loss. Weeks to months later, some develop burning, stabbing pain, dysesthesia, and allodynia affecting the contralateral face, arm, and leg. This is due to abnormal central processing of sensory input after thalamic injury. The most common cause is a small lacunar infarct from chronic hypertension or diabetes.
bottom_line: This patient has thalamic pain syndrome (Dejerine-Roussy syndrome), which occurs as a delayed complication of a prior thalamic stroke.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with burning, stabbing pain on the right side of her body that has been progressively worsening over several weeks. She reports hypersensitivity to light touch on her right arm, leg, and face. Medical history includes a lacunar stroke 3 months ago. Neurologic examination shows no weakness or sensory loss to pinprick or vibration, but she withdraws to light touch. MRI shows an old infarct in the left thalamus. Which of the following best explains her current symptoms?

options.C: Loss of spinothalamic fibers at the spinal cord level

explanation: This patient has thalamic pain syndrome, a delayed central post-stroke pain syndrome caused by thalamic injury. A prior left thalamic lacunar infarct can produce contralateral burning, stabbing pain and allodynia of the face, arm, and leg without weakness.
bottom_line: Thalamic pain syndrome causes delayed contralateral burning pain and allodynia after a thalamic stroke.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset weakness of the right side of her face. She is unable to close her right eye, raise her right eyebrow, or smile symmetrically. She denies rash, ear pain, or recent travel. She reports recovering from a cold a few days ago. Neurologic examination reveals complete paralysis of the upper and lower right face. There are no vesicular lesions, hearing changes, or other neurologic findings. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her condition?

options.A: Demyelination of corticobulbar fibers in the internal capsule
options.B: Inflammatory compression of the facial nerve within the facial canal
options.C: Ischemic infarct of the facial motor nucleus in the pons
options.D: Infection with Borrelia burgdorferi acquired in an endemic region
options.E: Reactivation of latent virus in the geniculate ganglion

answer_text: Inflammatory compression of the facial nerve within the facial canal
explanation: This patient has an isolated lower motor neuron lesion of the facial nerve, resulting in complete ipsilateral facial weakness, including the forehead. The most common cause is idiopathic post-viral inflammation causing nerve edema and compression within the narrow facial canal, referred to clinically as Bell’s palsy. The absence of ear vesicles, tick exposure, or progressive symptoms supports an idiopathic etiology rather than infectious, ischemic, or mass-related causes.
bottom_line: This patient has an isolated lower motor neuron lesion of the facial nerve, resulting in complete ipsilateral facial weakness, including the forehead.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 46-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left-sided flank pain that began 3 hours ago while he was at work. The pain is constant, comes in waves of intense severity, and has not improved with rest or changes in position. He also reports nausea and two episodes of vomiting but denies diarrhea, dysuria, or fever. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He drinks minimal water during the day and consumes large amounts of tea and red meat. Blood pressure is 152/92 mm Hg, pulse is 104/min, and temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F). Physical examination reveals an uncomfortable man who is pacing and repeatedly shifting position. There is left costovertebral angle tenderness but no abdominal rebound or guarding. Urinalysis shows microscopic hematuria without pyuria or bacteriuria. The pain in this patient is most likely to radiate to which of the following locations?
explanation: This patient’s acute onset of severe, colicky flank pain radiating in waves, microscopic hematuria without infection, costovertebral angle tenderness, and restlessness is classic for ureteral obstruction due to a urinary stone (nephrolithiasis). The most common type of urinary stone is calcium oxalate, which is radiopaque on x-ray and often associated with low fluid intake, high oxalate-rich diets such as tea and spinach, and hypercalciuria. Colicky pain occurs as the ureter contracts against the obstructing calculus, producing intermittent surges in discomfort that are often described as coming in waves and causing the patient to be unable to find a comfortable position. Calcium oxalate stones are the most common, but other types include calcium phosphate stones, which are radiopaque and associated with alkaline urine and some metabolic disorders; uric acid stones, which are radiolucent and linked to acidic urine, high purine intake from sources like red meat and shellfish, and conditions such as gout; struvite stones composed of magnesium ammonium phosphate, which are radiopaque and occur with urease-positive urinary tract infections such as those caused by Proteus or Klebsiella; and cystine stones, which are radiopaque and result from cystinuria, often occurring in younger patients. Diagnosis is suspected clinically and confirmed with imaging, with noncontrast helical CT of the abdomen and pelvis being the gold standard for detecting all stone types. Ultrasound is an alternative in pregnant patients or those avoiding radiation exposure. Urinalysis typically shows microscopic or gross hematuria without pyuria unless the stone is complicated by obstruction and infection. The pain’s location often correlates with the stone’s position within the urinary tract, as stones tend to lodge at common points of anatomic narrowing, which include the ureteropelvic junction between the renal pelvis and ureter, the pelvic brim where the ureter crosses over the iliac vessels, and the ureterovesical junction where the ureter enters the bladder and which is the narrowest point of the urinary tract. Pain referral to the groin occurs when the stone is in the distal ureter, near the ureterovesical junction. The ureter receives visceral afferent innervation that travels with sympathetic fibers from the T10-L2 spinal cord segments. As the stone descends, the location of referred pain changes: proximal ureteral stones typically produce pain in the flank corresponding to the T10-T12 dermatomes; mid-ureteral stones cause pain radiating anteriorly toward the lower abdomen; and distal ureteral stones cause pain radiating to the groin, scrotum in males, or labia majora in females, due to shared sensory pathways with the genitofemoral nerve (L1-L2). This referred pain mechanism occurs because visceral afferents converge on the same second-order neurons as somatic afferents from the skin in the spinal cord, leading the brain to perceive visceral pain as originating from somatic regions supplied by the same dermatomes.
bottom_line: This patient’s acute onset of severe, colicky flank pain radiating in waves, microscopic hematuria without infection, costovertebral angle tenderness, and restlessness is classic for ureteral obstruction due to a urinary stone (nephrolithiasis).

subject: Neuro
stem: Based on this presentation, which labeled structure is the most likely site of pathology?
explanation: Hemiballismus is caused by damage to the contralateral subthalamic nucleus, which plays a key role in the indirect pathway of basal ganglia circuitry by exciting the internal segment of the globus pallidus, which in turn inhibits the thalamus. When the subthalamic nucleus is damaged, inhibition of the thalamus is lost, resulting in excessive excitation of the motor cortex and uncontrolled, large-amplitude proximal limb movements (ballism). The most common cause of isolated subthalamic nucleus injury is lacunar infarction due to hypertensive small vessel disease, particularly involving penetrating branches of the posterior cerebral artery. The subthalamic nucleus is located inferior to the thalamus and adjacent to the internal capsule, making it susceptible to hypertensive arteriolar sclerosis. Although hemiballismus is rare, it is highly localizing and typically unilateral, contralateral to the lesion. In contrast to chorea (as seen in Huntington disease) which involves smaller-amplitude distal movements, hemiballismus is characterized by wild, flinging proximal movements. Recognition of this disorder is crucial in hypertensive patients because it directs focus to basal ganglia pathology, specifically involving the indirect pathway that normally suppresses movement.
bottom_line: Hemiballismus is caused by damage to the contralateral subthalamic nucleus, which plays a key role in the indirect pathway of basal ganglia circuitry by exciting the internal segment of the globus pallidus, which in turn inhibits the thalamus.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 72-year-old man presents with sudden onset of numbness on the right side of his face, arm, and leg. He reports no weakness, slurred speech, or visual changes. On exam, he has decreased pinprick, vibration, and proprioception over the entire right side of his body and face. Strength, coordination, gait, and cranial nerve function are otherwise normal. MRI reveals a small lacunar infarct. Which of the following structures is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient has a left thalamic stroke involving the VPL and VPM nuclei, which receive sensory input from the contralateral body and face, respectively. The ventral posterolateral (VPL) nucleus processes sensory information from the body (via the spinothalamic tract and dorsal columns), while the ventral posteromedial (VPM) nucleus handles sensory input from the face (via the trigeminal nerve). Damage causes pure sensory loss on the contralateral face and body. Lacunar infarcts from small vessel disease (often due to hypertension or diabetes) are a common cause.
bottom_line: This patient has a left thalamic stroke involving the VPL and VPM nuclei, which receive sensory input from the contralateral body and face, respectively.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 46-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-week history of numbness and tingling affecting his right hand. The symptoms began gradually and have been progressively worsening. He works in construction and often carries heavy loads on his shoulders. He also reports aching pain radiating from the right side of his neck to the lateral arm, with occasional difficulty lifting objects above shoulder level. He denies trauma, fever, or recent illness. Examination shows decreased sensation to pinprick over the lateral aspect of the right shoulder, forearm, thumb, and index finger. There is mild weakness with shoulder elevation and elbow flexion, as well as slight difficulty with forearm supination. Muscle bulk is preserved. The biceps and brachioradialis reflexes on the right are diminished, while grip strength and wrist extension remain intact. Neck range of motion is mildly restricted by discomfort, and the remainder of the neurological exam is normal. Which of the following movements on the right is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

options.E: Rotation of the head to the left

explanation: The sensory loss over the lateral shoulder, lateral forearm, thumb, and index finger along with diminished biceps and brachioradialis reflexes localizes to C5-C6 radiculopathy. These roots contribute to the axillary nerve, which innervates the deltoid muscle responsible for arm abduction between 15° and 90°. Weakness in this movement, combined with associated elbow flexion and forearm supination weakness, strongly supports the diagnosis. Additional symptoms may include external rotation weakness (due to infraspinatus involvement), aching neck-to-arm pain, fatigability with overhead tasks, and paresthesias in the C6 dermatome.
bottom_line: The sensory loss over the lateral shoulder, lateral forearm, thumb, and index finger along with diminished biceps and brachioradialis reflexes localizes to C5-C6 radiculopathy.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 66-year-old man presents because of sudden onset of double vision and weakness. On examination, his right eye is "down and out" with a dilated pupil. He has weakness of his left arm and leg. His facial sensation is intact, and he has no swallowing or speech difficulties. Brain on autopsy appears as follows. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?
explanation: This patient has right Weber syndrome (medial midbrain infarction). The key features are ipsilateral oculomotor nerve (CN III) palsy - with ptosis, dilated pupil, and the eye resting down and out - along with contralateral body weakness (left-sided arm and leg weakness). The lesion affects the right crus cerebri (corticospinal tract), causing contralateral hemiparesis, and the oculomotor fibers exiting the midbrain. The most common cause is infarction of the paramedian branches of the posterior cerebral artery (PCA). On NBME exams, when you see a stroke syndrome involving oculomotor nerve palsy, the diagnosis is almost always Weber syndrome (midbrain lesion).
bottom_line: This patient has right Weber syndrome (medial midbrain infarction).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department after being hit by a car while riding his bicycle. He sustained trauma to the lateral aspect of his right leg. He is unable to dorsiflex or evert his right foot and says that he keeps tripping while walking because his foot “slaps the ground.” On examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion and eversion of the right foot, as well as sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral shin. Plantarflexion and inversion are intact. Reflexes are normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: The common peroneal nerve (also called the common fibular nerve) is a branch of the sciatic nerve and wraps around the neck of the fibula, where it is highly superficial and vulnerable to trauma. It divides into the deep peroneal nerve, which controls dorsiflexion, and the superficial peroneal nerve, which controls eversion and provides sensation over the dorsum of the foot and lateral shin. Injury results in foot drop (loss of dorsiflexion), impaired eversion, and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral leg-exactly as described in this patient. Because plantarflexion and inversion are mediated by the tibial nerve, these functions remain intact, helping localize the lesion to the common peroneal nerve rather than a more proximal sciatic nerve injury. Foot drop causes patients to adopt a high-stepping gait to prevent tripping.
bottom_line: The common peroneal nerve (also called the common fibular nerve) is a branch of the sciatic nerve and wraps around the neck of the fibula, where it is highly superficial and vulnerable to trauma.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 46-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic due to persistent pain and weakness in his right leg for the past week. He reports that the pain began after he lifted a heavy toolbox at work, and it initially radiated from his lower back into his right buttock. Over the next few days, the pain extended down the lateral aspect of his right thigh and leg. He now notices difficulty when trying to walk upstairs and says he sometimes drags his right foot when walking on flat ground. He denies bowel or bladder incontinence, and his symptoms have been relatively stable over the past several days. On neurologic exam, motor strength is 4/5 with dorsiflexion of the right foot. Reflexes are 2+ at the knees and 1+ at the ankles bilaterally. Sensation is decreased over the lateral aspect of the right leg and dorsum of the right foot. Straight-leg raise test reproduces his leg pain at 40 degrees on the right. Which of the following nerve roots is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient presents with findings characteristic of L5 radiculopathy, which most commonly results from L4-L5 disc herniation compressing the L5 nerve root. The hallmark features of L5 involvement include pain radiating down the lateral thigh and leg, motor weakness in dorsiflexion (tibialis anterior), and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral leg. Importantly, reflexes are typically spared in isolated L5 radiculopathy, which matches this patient's preserved knee and ankle reflexes. His weakness in walking on stairs or foot dragging also points to dorsiflexion impairment, which is specific to L5 motor involvement. The neurologic findings strongly align with this root’s distribution: the tibialis anterior (responsible for dorsiflexion) and the extensor hallucis longus (toe extension) are L5-innervated muscles that become weak when this root is affected. The sensory findings in the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot are also textbook for L5 radiculopathy. Furthermore, his preserved ankle and patellar reflexes help localize the lesion to L5, since S1 and L4 control those reflexes, respectively. The positive straight-leg raise test is a classic sign of nerve root compression, often seen in L5 or S1 involvement. L5 is the most frequently affected nerve root in lumbar radiculopathy and often results from posterolateral herniation of the L4-L5 intervertebral disc. Disc herniation at this level is common because of high mechanical stress during lumbar flexion and loading, especially in individuals who perform heavy lifting.
bottom_line: This patient presents with findings characteristic of L5 radiculopathy, which most commonly results from L4-L5 disc herniation compressing the L5 nerve root.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A . A 34-year-old woman presents with 2 months of brief, severe, electric shock- like pain on the right side of her face. The pain is triggered by brushing her teeth, chewing, and touching her cheek. Each episode lasts a few seconds and recurs multiple times per day. She denies headache, jaw stiffness, scalp tenderness, rash, or travel history. Neurologic examination is normal. ESR is within normal limits. MRI of the brain shows no mass lesions. Which of the following best explains the most likely cause of her symptoms?

options.B: Microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve
options.D: Infection with Borrelia burgdorferi affecting cranial nerves
options.E: Inflammatory arthritis of the temporomandibular joint

answer_text: Microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve
explanation: This patient has trigeminal neuralgia, characterized by recurrent, brief, electric shock-like pain on the face triggered by minor sensory input. CN V originates from the pons, and demyelination at the nerve root (often due to MS or vascular compression) is a common underlying mechanism. The episodic, stereotyped nature of the pain, absence of systemic features, and normal imaging/ESR support this diagnosis.
bottom_line: This patient has trigeminal neuralgia, characterized by recurrent, brief, electric shock-like pain on the face triggered by minor sensory input.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-week history of recurring, severe, throbbing headaches that are most intense in the mornings and occasionally awaken her from sleep. She also reports brief episodes of visual dimming and flashing lights, particularly when standing up quickly. She denies vomiting, photophobia, or focal neurologic symptoms. She has no significant medical history. She is 162 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 88 kg (194 lb); BMI is 33.5 kg/m². Neurologic examination is normal except for bilateral papilledema. Pupils are equal and reactive to light. Visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes, and formal visual field testing is pending. MRI of the brain is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Parkinson disease is caused by progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, resulting in decreased dopamine delivery to the striatum. Dopamine normally stimulates the direct (movement-facilitating) pathway via D1 receptors and inhibits the indirect (movement-inhibiting) pathway via D2 receptors. Therefore, dopamine loss leads to inhibition of the direct pathway and disinhibition (activation) of the indirect pathway, causing reduced thalamocortical output and hypokinetic motor symptoms. This pathophysiologic imbalance leads to the bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, and postural instability characteristic of Parkinson disease. The basal ganglia circuitry functions as a gating mechanism for movement, and in the absence of dopamine, the globus pallidus internus remains tonically active, suppressing the thalamus and motor cortex. This explains the difficulty initiating voluntary movement in Parkinsonian patients.
bottom_line: Parkinson disease is caused by progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, resulting in decreased dopamine delivery to the striatum.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 68-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic for evaluation of progressive slowness and stiffness. Over the past year, he has developed a shuffling gait, decreased arm swing, and difficulty initiating movements. His wife also notes a resting tremor in his right hand. On exam, he has cogwheel rigidity, a masked facies, and a positive pull test. MRI of the brain shows age- appropriate atrophy and no focal lesions. The neurologist suspects a disorder involving dopaminergic degeneration. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology underlying this patient’s condition?

options.A: Inhibition of the direct pathway and activation of the indirect pathway in the basal ganglia
options.C: Disruption of GABAergic projections to the thalamus
options.D: Excessive stimulation of the subthalamic nucleus
options.F: Loss of serotonergic input from the raphe nuclei
options.G: Progressive destruction of the anterior horn cells

answer_text: Inhibition of the direct pathway and activation of the indirect pathway in the basal ganglia
explanation: Parkinson disease reflects loss of dopaminergic input from the substantia nigra pars compacta. Dopamine depletion inhibits the direct pathway and activates the indirect pathway in the basal ganglia, producing bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, and other hypokinetic features.
bottom_line: Parkinson disease inhibits the direct pathway and activates the indirect pathway because of nigrostriatal dopamine loss.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 56-year-old man presents to the clinic due to progressive weakness in his hands and legs over the past 8 months. He first noticed difficulty buttoning his shirt and a tendency to drop objects. More recently, he has developed trouble climbing stairs and getting out of chairs. He also reports occasional muscle cramping and twitching, especially in his calves. He denies numbness, tingling, or bowel or bladder dysfunction. He works as an accountant and has no recent travel or exposure history. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On neurologic examination, the patient has marked atrophy of the intrinsic hand muscles with visible fasciculations. Reflexes are brisk throughout, and Babinski sign is present bilaterally. Muscle strength is 4/5 in all extremities, and tone is increased. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with slowly progressive weakness in both upper and lower extremities, visible muscle atrophy and fasciculations (LMN signs), as well as increased tone, brisk reflexes, and positive Babinski sign (UMN signs). The coexistence of upper and lower motor neuron (UMN + LMN) findings with no sensory involvement is pathognomonic for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). ALS is caused by degeneration of the lateral corticospinal tracts (UMN) and anterior horn cells (LMN). He has no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no sensory loss, and no history of trauma or infection, which helps rule out mimics. This exact pattern-fasciculations and atrophy alongside spasticity and brisk reflexes-should immediately raise suspicion for ALS on the USMLE. USMLE repeatedly tests ALS as the “motor neuron disease” that presents with this specific combination and absolutely no sensory symptoms. High-yield findings include muscle cramps and visible twitching (fasciculations), which often occur in the legs early on, and the preserved sensation rules out many differentials. The next best step in diagnosis would be electromyography and nerve conduction studies. Though riluzole may slow disease progression, there is no cure, and management is supportive with emphasis on multidisciplinary care.
bottom_line: This patient presents with slowly progressive weakness in both upper and lower extremities, visible muscle atrophy and fasciculations (LMN signs), as well as increased tone, brisk reflexes, and positive Babinski sign (UMN signs).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 45-year-old man comes to the emergency department due to severe lower back pain that began two days ago and has worsened overnight. He now has difficulty urinating and reports numbness in his inner thighs and perineal region. He also feels unsteady when walking and notes weakness in his right leg. Past medical history is unremarkable. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 124/78 mm Hg, and pulse is 88/min. Neurologic examination shows 3/5 strength in right ankle dorsiflexion and absent right ankle reflex. Sensation is decreased over both posterior thighs and the perianal region. Anal wink is absent. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with classic features of cauda equina syndrome (CES), a surgical emergency caused by compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus medullaris. Key findings include severe lower back pain, saddle anesthesia, unilateral leg weakness, bladder dysfunction (such as retention), loss of ankle reflexes, and absent anal wink. These findings reflect lower motor neuron injury to the sacral and lumbar nerve roots. Common causes of CES include central disc herniation (often at L4-L5 or L5-S1), tumors, epidural abscesses, or trauma. The diagnosis is confirmed by MRI of the lumbar spine, and urgent surgical decompression is required to prevent permanent neurologic deficits. This syndrome must be distinguished from conus medullaris syndrome, which tends to present with bilateral symmetric symptoms and early bladder dysfunction, whereas CES is often more asymmetric and may begin with radicular leg pain. pain.
bottom_line: This patient presents with classic features of cauda equina syndrome (CES), a surgical emergency caused by compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus medullaris.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to her physician with complaints of progressive blurry vision in her right eye for 3 days, accompanied by pain when moving the eye and difficulty distinguishing red hues. She also reports intermittent episodes of limb weakness over the past year that resolved spontaneously. Neurologic examination reveals decreased visual acuity in the right eye, a central scotoma, and an abnormal pupillary light reaction on the right side such that both pupils constrict less when light is directed into the right eye but constrict briskly when light is directed into the left eye. Strength, reflexes, and sensation are intact. Fundoscopic exam is unremarkable. Which of the following best describes the most likely underlying pathology in this patient?
explanation: The superior oblique is innervated by cranial nerve IV (trochlear nerve). Its primary function is depression of the eye in the adducted position, as well as intorsion and slight abduction. Patients with trochlear nerve palsy commonly experience vertical diplopia that worsens with downward gaze, such as reading or descending stairs, and may adopt a compensatory head tilt toward the unaffected side. This pattern is highly characteristic and frequently tested.
bottom_line: The superior oblique is innervated by cranial nerve IV (trochlear nerve).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with complaints of double vision that worsens when walking down stairs or reading. He often tilts his head to the right to compensate. On neurologic examination, when asked to look downward while adducting his left eye, he is unable to fully depress the globe. No other neurologic deficits are noted. Which of the following extraocular muscles is most likely impaired?
explanation: Trochlear nerve palsy impairs the superior oblique muscle, which depresses the adducted eye. That causes vertical diplopia that is worse when reading or walking downstairs and is often partially relieved by compensatory head tilt.
bottom_line: Superior oblique dysfunction causes diplopia that worsens on downgaze in adduction.

subject: Neuro
topic: Normal structure and function of the nervous system
stem: Researchers are conducting an animal study to investigate neural mechanisms of toxin-induced emesis. In the study, two groups of rats are administered a sublethal dose of a novel compound that mimics organophosphate ingestion. In the control group, nearly all animals demonstrate repeated episodes of retching, salivation, piloerection, and vomiting-like behavior within 20 minutes of exposure. In the experimental group, a specific surgical lesion was made prior to exposure, targeting a circumscribed region on the dorsal surface of the medulla near the caudal portion of the fourth ventricle. Following toxin administration, these animals fail to demonstrate any retching or vomiting, although other cholinergic symptoms (e.g., bradycardia, lacrimation, pupillary constriction) are still observed. Subsequent histopathologic examination of the lesioned region reveals damage to a highly vascular, fenestrated structure lacking a traditional blood-brain barrier, which had previously stained positive for dopaminergic and serotonergic receptors involved in autonomic signaling. Which of the following brain structures was most likely lesioned in the experimental group?

options.C: Dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus

answer_text: Dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus
explanation: This patient with metastatic prostate cancer presents with thoracic spinal cord compression, a feared complication of epidural metastasis. The key localization clue is numbness around the nipples, which corresponds to the T4 dermatome. Dermatomes represent the cutaneous sensory distribution of spinal nerves, and T4 aligns with the level of the nipples, making it a reliable clinical landmark. Compression at or above T4 would explain his bilateral leg weakness, upper motor neuron signs (e.g., hyperreflexia, Babinski), and sensory level. Spinal cord compression often manifests as back pain, followed by motor and sensory deficits, gait instability, and autonomic dysfunction such as urinary retention or constipation. This patient's underlying malignancy makes epidural metastasis likely, particularly to the thoracic spine, which is the most common location for spinal metastases. Prompt diagnosis via MRI and emergent management-often with high-dose corticosteroids and possible surgical decompression or radiation-is essential to prevent permanent neurologic damage. Recognizing the relationship between specific dermatomal sensory loss and spinal cord levels is vital for USMLE and clinical practice. In this case, sensory loss at the nipple line localizes the lesion to T4, guiding urgent imaging and intervention.
bottom_line: This patient with metastatic prostate cancer presents with thoracic spinal cord compression, a feared complication of epidural metastasis.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 62-year-old man presents to the emergency department with progressive difficulty walking and numbness in his chest. He was diagnosed with metastatic prostate cancer a year ago and recently began experiencing new back pain and unsteady gait. Over the past three days, he has also noticed numbness around his nipples, which he initially attributed to cold weather, but it has persisted indoors. He denies urinary incontinence, but he does report occasional hesitancy and a sense of incomplete bladder emptying. He also describes new weakness in both lower extremities and finds it hard to rise from a chair without using his arms. On examination, his strength is decreased in both legs, and deep tendon reflexes are brisk. Babinski sign is present bilaterally. Sensation to pinprick and temperature is diminished below the level of the nipples, while sensation above this level is intact. Rectal tone is decreased. An urgent MRI of the thoracic spine is ordered. Involvement of which of the following spinal cord levels is most likely responsible for this patient's sensory deficit?
explanation: The nipple line corresponds to the T4 dermatome. In a patient with metastatic prostate cancer, bilateral leg weakness with a sensory level at the nipples strongly localizes the lesion to the thoracic cord at about T4, consistent with epidural spinal cord compression.
bottom_line: Sensory loss at the nipples localizes to the T4 dermatome.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset neck pain and right upper extremity weakness that began earlier in the day while he was unloading heavy boxes at work. He reports that as he lifted a particularly heavy item, he felt a sharp, stabbing pain shoot from the base of his neck down into his right shoulder and arm. Since then, any attempt to move his neck, especially rotation or extension, worsens the pain significantly. He has also noticed that his right arm feels weak, particularly when trying to lift objects or extend his wrist. The pain is exacerbated by coughing or sneezing. He has experienced occasional stiffness and soreness in his neck over the past 2 years, especially after physical activity, but it was previously mild and responsive to ibuprofen. He denies numbness or tingling in the fingers but says his right arm "just doesn't feel right." There is no history of recent trauma beyond lifting at work, and he has no bowel or bladder dysfunction. He works as a warehouse foreman and has been in good health aside from chronic lower back pain. He does not smoke or drink. On physical exam, he sits with his neck rigid and avoids turning his head. There is mild tenderness over the right cervical paraspinal region. Muscle strength is 4/5 in the right biceps and wrist extensors, while triceps and hand grip strength are intact. Deep tendon reflexes are decreased in the right biceps and brachioradialis. Sensory examination is normal. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.D: Radial nerve compression in the axilla

explanation: This patient presents with an acute cervical radiculopathy due to compression of the C6 nerve root, most likely from a herniated intervertebral disc at the C5-6 level. Cervical radiculopathy refers to dysfunction of a nerve root in the cervical spine, typically caused by mechanical compression from a protruding nucleus pulposus, osteophytes, or foraminal narrowing. The cervical nerve roots exit above their corresponding vertebrae, so a herniated disc at C5-6 compresses the C6 root. C6 innervates the biceps (elbow flexion) and wrist extensors, and its associated reflexes include the biceps reflex and brachioradialis reflex, both of which are diminished in this patient. Clinically, C6 radiculopathy manifests with radiating neck and shoulder pain, weakness in elbow flexion and wrist extension, and diminished reflexes, as seen here. Pain is often worsened by neck movement, Valsalva maneuvers (like coughing or sneezing), or axial loading, all of which increase pressure on the affected nerve root. Sensory disturbances, if present, may involve the lateral forearm, thumb, and index finger, though sensory findings may be subtle or absent. The acute onset while lifting a heavy object and the dermatomal/myotomal pattern strongly support disc herniation rather than a peripheral nerve or plexus lesion. MRI of the cervical spine is the preferred imaging modality to confirm the diagnosis, visualize the herniation, and assess for cord compression. Management typically begins with conservative therapy, including rest, NSAIDs, physical therapy, and sometimes corticosteroids. Most patients improve over weeks to months without surgery. However, surgical intervention (e.g., anterior cervical discectomy and fusion) may be necessary for those with progressive weakness, myelopathy, or intractable pain.
bottom_line: This patient presents with an acute cervical radiculopathy due to compression of the C6 nerve root, most likely from a herniated intervertebral disc at the C5-6 level.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 63-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife due to sudden-onset involuntary movements affecting the left side of his body. The symptoms began earlier in the day and have progressively worsened. His wife describes them as large, unpredictable flinging movements of the left arm and leg, making it difficult for him to walk or feed himself. He has no history of similar episodes. His medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, both poorly controlled. He takes lisinopril and metformin but reports nonadherence over the past several months. On examination, the patient is alert and oriented. He exhibits continuous, irregular, high-amplitude movements of the left upper and lower extremities. Muscle tone is difficult to assess due to the involuntary motion, but no spasticity or rigidity is appreciated. Reflexes are symmetric and there is no pronator drift or sensory deficit. Cranial nerves are intact. A non-contrast head CT reveals a small area of hyperdensity in the deep structures of the right cerebral hemisphere, consistent with an acute lacunar infarct. Which of the following structures is most likely affected in this patient?

options.E: Ventral posterolateral nucleus of the thalamus

explanation: This patient presents with acute-onset, involuntary, large-amplitude, flinging movements of the left arm and leg, a pattern consistent with hemiballismus, a hyperkinetic movement disorder. The classic underlying lesion in hemiballismus is the subthalamic nucleus, part of the indirect basal ganglia pathway. The subthalamic nucleus exerts excitatory input on the internal segment of the globus pallidus, which in turn inhibits the thalamus, thereby reducing movement. A lesion to the subthalamic nucleus disrupts this inhibitory circuit, resulting in excessive, involuntary movement. In this case, the patient has unilateral symptoms affecting the left side, implying a lesion in the right hemisphere, consistent with the contralateral nature of motor control. His history of hypertension and poorly controlled diabetes increases his risk of lacunar infarcts, which commonly affect deep brain structures, including the subthalamic nucleus. The CT finding of a hyperdense area in the deep right brain supports the diagnosis of a right-sided lacunar stroke affecting the subthalamic nucleus. Hemiballismus is rare but can be seen in vascular events, particularly small infarcts in the posterior limb of the internal capsule or subthalamic region. The involuntary movements are typically unilateral, violent, and continuous, most prominently involving the proximal limbs. Although symptoms may gradually improve, initial management may require pharmacologic suppression of movements (e.g., dopamine antagonists).
bottom_line: This patient presents with acute-onset, involuntary, large-amplitude, flinging movements of the left arm and leg, a pattern consistent with hemiballismus, a hyperkinetic movement disorder.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 72-year-old man is brought to the clinic for evaluation of dysphagia, hoarseness, and mild choking episodes while drinking liquids. Neurologic examination reveals loss of the gag reflex on the right and uvular deviation to the left. The following diagram shows the nerve damaged. Which of the following cranial nerve deficits best explains his hoarseness and uvular deviation?

options.A: Damage to the left vagus nerve
options.B: Damage to the right glossopharyngeal nerve
options.C: Damage to the right hypoglossal nerve
options.D: Damage to the right vagus nerve
options.E: Damage to the right trigeminal nerve

answer_text: Damage to the right vagus nerve
explanation: The vagus nerve (CN X) innervates the muscles of the soft palate and larynx. A lesion causes weakness of the ipsilateral palate, resulting in uvula deviation away from the affected side. It also innervates intrinsic muscles of the larynx (via recurrent laryngeal nerve), explaining the hoarseness. CN X also contributes to the efferent limb of the gag reflex.
bottom_line: The vagus nerve (CN X) innervates the muscles of the soft palate and larynx.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: ccessory nerve B. Axillary nerve C. Lateral pectoral nerve D. Long thoracic nerve E. Musculocutaneous nerve F. Radial nerve G. Suprascapular nerve H. Upper subscapular nerve A 32-year-old man presents to the emergency department after falling off his motorcycle and landing directly on his right shoulder. He reports severe shoulder pain and an inability to lift his arm. On examination, the right shoulder appears flattened with prominent acromion, and he holds his arm in slight abduction and external rotation. He is unable to abduct his arm beyond 15 degrees, and there is decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the right upper arm. Reflexes are intact, and motor strength in the elbow and hand is normal. X-ray of the shoulder shows an anterior dislocation of the humeral head without associated fracture. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: The axillary nerve, derived from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus (C5-C6), wraps around the surgical neck of the humerus and passes through the quadrangular space. It is most vulnerable to injury during anterior shoulder dislocations or fractures of the surgical neck of the humerus. The axillary nerve innervates the deltoid and teres minor muscles and provides sensory innervation to the skin over the lateral shoulder ("regimental badge" area). Injury leads to weakness of shoulder abduction from 15 to 90 degrees and loss of sensation in the lateral upper arm, as seen in this patient. The flattened shoulder contour is due to deltoid muscle atrophy and displacement of the humeral head. Because the nerve has no distal motor functions in the forearm or hand, strength in those areas remains preserved. Reflexes are intact, and supraspinatus (which initiates abduction to 15°) may still function via the suprascapular nerve. Immediate management of axillary nerve injury focuses on shoulder reduction and immobilization, with nerve recovery typically expected if the injury is neurapraxic.
bottom_line: The axillary nerve, derived from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus (C5-C6), wraps around the surgical neck of the humerus and passes through the quadrangular space.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic for evaluation of right shoulder weakness after a recent sports injury. He reports falling directly onto his shoulder while playing football two weeks ago. Since then, he has had difficulty raising his right arm away from his body. He also notes a persistent dull ache and slight numbness over the outer portion of the shoulder. There is no wrist or hand weakness, and he denies any tingling in the fingers. On physical examination, the patient is unable to abduct the right arm beyond 15 degrees. There is a visible flattening of the contour of the right shoulder compared to the left. Strength is intact in the forearm and hand, and reflexes are normal. Sensation is decreased over the lateral aspect of the upper arm. Based on the attached image, which of the labeled nerves (A-E) is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of axillary nerve injury, a condition often resulting from shoulder trauma, including direct impact or proximal humerus fractures. The axillary nerve arises from C5-C6 roots and innervates the deltoid muscle, which is responsible for shoulder abduction between 15 and 90 degrees. His inability to abduct the arm within this range, combined with a flattened shoulder contour, reflects deltoid dysfunction. Additionally, the axillary nerve provides cutaneous sensation over the lateral upper arm, which explains the patient’s reported numbness. Normal distal motor function and reflexes argue against a more widespread plexus injury, and the absence of hand symptoms localizes the deficit to the axillary distribution. The flattened contour of the shoulder is a subtle yet highly tested finding on USMLE, signaling underlying deltoid atrophy. In the image provided, label B accurately identifies the axillary nerve, which branches from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and winds around the surgical neck of the humerus, making it particularly susceptible to injury in shoulder trauma.
bottom_line: This patient presents with classic signs of axillary nerve injury, a condition often resulting from shoulder trauma, including direct impact or proximal humerus fractures.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 21-year-old college track athlete presents to the clinic due to difficulty running and lifting his foot while walking. He says the problem started after an ankle hyperflexion injury that occurred during long-jump practice one week ago. Since then, he has noticed that his right foot tends to "slap the ground" and that he has to lift his knee unusually high when walking, especially when trying to clear stairs. He has no pain, no numbness, and no difficulty pushing off with the foot or turning it outward. He denies prior injuries, back pain, or systemic symptoms. On examination, he has a mild limp with exaggerated hip flexion during swing phase on the right side. No calf tenderness or swelling is noted, and reflexes are symmetric. There is no thigh or hip weakness. Passive range of motion at the ankle is full and pain-free. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This patient presents with isolated dorsiflexion weakness, leading to a classic high-steppage gait, without loss of eversion or plantarflexion. These findings strongly indicate an injury to the deep peroneal (fibular) nerve, which innervates the anterior compartment of the leg, including the tibialis anterior, the primary dorsiflexor of the foot. The deep peroneal nerve is a branch of the common peroneal nerve, and its motor role is critical for lifting the foot during the swing phase of gait. The presence of a high-steppage gait - where the patient lifts the hip and knee exaggeratedly to clear the foot - is a hallmark of dorsiflexion loss and a classic USMLE indicator of deep peroneal nerve injury. Because eversion is preserved, the superficial peroneal nerve remains intact, helping localize the lesion specifically to the deep branch rather than the common trunk. On USMLE, deep peroneal nerve is the answer if the patient loses only dorsiflexion of the foot. This distinction is essential in neuromuscular localization. This type of nerve injury can occur in the setting of trauma, compression (e.g., tight boots, casts), or sports-related stress, particularly when the ankle or anterior shin is involved. The patient’s history of recent ankle hyperflexion during a jump, along with weakness isolated to dorsiflexion, is consistent with deep peroneal nerve involvement, without signs of broader neuropathy. Although the deep peroneal nerve provides sensation to the web space between the first and second toes, this finding is rarely emphasized or tested on USMLE. Most questions focus entirely on the motor deficit (dorsiflexion loss) and associated gait changes, making the sensory component functionally irrelevant for exam purposes.
bottom_line: This patient presents with isolated dorsiflexion weakness, leading to a classic high-steppage gait, without loss of eversion or plantarflexion.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A group of neuroscience researchers is conducting an experiment on thoracic somatosensory pain perception. As part of the study, healthy volunteers are subjected to graded mechanical pressure applied to different regions of the thoracic cage. The investigators record pain intensity scores and corresponding nerve conduction signals using microneurography and cutaneous field mapping. When pressure is applied to the left lateral thoracic wall near the 8th rib, participants report sharp, well-localized pain that appears rapidly and adapts quickly, consistent with somatic A-delta fiber activation. The pain is most pronounced when pressure is applied between adjacent ribs and diminishes when the stimulus is shifted toward the spine or sternum. In a follow-up part of the experiment, a subset of participants receives a local anesthetic injection just inferior to the left 8th rib at the midaxillary line. Following the injection, mechanical stimulation in that area no longer elicits pain, while sensation in neighboring segments remains intact. Deep visceral sensation and autonomic functions are unaffected. Which of the following nerves is most likely responsible for transmitting the pain in this experiment?
explanation: This experiment evaluates somatic pain perception from the thoracic wall, specifically over the lateral portion of the 8th rib, and demonstrates characteristics of sharp, well-localized pain mediated by A-delta fibers. Such pain arises from the skin, muscles, and parietal pleura, which are all somatically innervated. The pain’s reproducibility with external pressure and its precise localization suggest peripheral somatic afferent involvement rather than visceral or referred pain. Furthermore, the fact that pain is abolished by an injection just below the 8th rib-in the area of the intercostal space-indicates that the affected nerve runs along the rib and is responsible for cutaneous sensation in that dermatome. The intercostal nerves, which are the ventral rami of the thoracic spinal nerves (T1-T11), course within each intercostal space and give off lateral and anterior cutaneous branches. These nerves provide motor innervation to the intercostal muscles and sensory innervation to the overlying skin and parietal pleura. The nerve block described selectively affected the localized somatic pain without impairing autonomic or visceral responses, making it highly specific to the intercostal nerve in that segment. Additionally, the preserved sensation in other dermatomes confirms that the block was segmental and did not involve spinal roots or central pathways.
bottom_line: This experiment evaluates somatic pain perception from the thoracic wall, specifically over the lateral portion of the 8th rib, and demonstrates characteristics of sharp, well-localized pain mediated by A-delta fibers.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 42-year-old man presents with progressive weakness and numbness in his lower limbs over the past several days. He recently recovered from a diarrheal illness that resolved without treatment. He reports increasing difficulty climbing stairs and now notices mild shortness of breath. On examination, the patient has decreased strength symmetrically in both legs, with areflexia. Sensory examination shows decreased vibration sense in the toes. Cranial nerves are intact. Vital signs are stable. Chest examination reveals shallow respirations. What is an additional finding that supports the diagnosis?
explanation: In Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), albuminocytologic dissociation refers to an elevated cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) protein level with a normal white blood cell count, which is a classic diagnostic feature and reflects increased permeability of the blood-nerve barrier. This pattern appears after the first week of symptom onset and is useful to support the diagnosis. GBS typically presents with symmetric ascending weakness, areflexia, and sometimes autonomic symptoms (e.g., blood pressure fluctuations or arrhythmia). Early identification is important to monitor for respiratory failure and autonomic instability. Once GBS is suspected clinically, a lumbar puncture is performed to detect albuminocytologic dissociation. Management of GBS includes supportive care with close monitoring of respiratory status (vital capacity), blood pressure, and heart rhythm. Immunomodulatory treatment with either intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasmapheresis is indicated for patients who are nonambulatory or progressing rapidly. Corticosteroids are not effective and should not be used. Pain and autonomic symptoms are treated symptomatically. Early involvement of physical therapy is also recommended.
bottom_line: In Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), albuminocytologic dissociation refers to an elevated cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) protein level with a normal white blood cell count, which is a classic diagnostic feature and reflects increased permeability of the blood-nerve barrier.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 68-year-old man comes to the clinic due to difficulty writing and using utensils over the past year. He reports a gradual onset of hand shakiness that worsens when he drinks coffee, tries to hold a spoon, or signs his name. He drinks a glass of wine in the evening daily, which he says improves the shakiness. He also reports that the symptoms disappear when he is lying down. He denies weakness, numbness, gait instability, or changes in speech. He has no history of stroke or head trauma and takes only atorvastatin. His father had similar symptoms beginning in his 60s. Vital signs are: temperature 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure 124/74 mm Hg, pulse 68/min, and respiratory rate 14/min. On exam, he has a fine, bilateral tremor of the hands that is most pronounced when his arms are extended or during finger-to-nose testing. His gait and muscle strength are normal with no rigidity. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ throughout. Examination of other systems is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with a classic, bilateral action tremor affecting the upper extremities, which worsens with goal-directed movement and improves with alcohol, hallmark features of essential tremor (ET). ET frequently runs in families (often autosomal dominant) and is one of the most common causes of tremor in older adults. It usually occurs in the hands, and the tremor is high-frequency and symmetric, affecting fine motor tasks such as writing, eating, and drinking. It worsens with goal-directed movements, anxiety, and fatigue, and improves with alcohol consumption. This leads to many patients self-medicating with alcohol. Essential tremor is a clinical diagnosis, especially when the presentation is typical. No investigations are needed unless an underlying neurological disorder is suspected. This patient’s exam findings, course, and family history strongly support the diagnosis of essential tremor, and this vignette of an old man with bilateral symmetrical action tremors that improve with alcohol is the typical presentation of essential tremor on the USMLEs. Treatment of essential tremor depends on the severity of symptoms and their impact on quality of life. Many patients with mild tremors do not require intervention beyond reassurance. For those with functionally impairing or socially distressing tremor, first-line pharmacologic option is propranolol, a non-selective beta-blocker. Primidone, a barbiturate anticonvulsant, can also be used, especially if a contraindication to propranolol exists (e.g., asthma or bradycardia). Essential tremors should be differentiated from other causes of tremors. Parkinsonian tremor is a resting tremor, usually asymmetric at onset, and is accompanied by bradykinesia, rigidity, and postural instability. Cerebellar tremors are intention tremors, worsening as the limb approaches a target, and are often associated with ataxia, dysmetria, and nystagmus. Physiologic tremor is a high-frequency, low-amplitude tremor present in all individuals, typically not noticed unless amplified by triggers like caffeine, anxiety, or medications.
bottom_line: This patient presents with a classic, bilateral action tremor affecting the upper extremities, which worsens with goal-directed movement and improves with alcohol, hallmark features of essential tremor (ET).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 46-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic due to a change in the way he walks that he has noticed over the past week. He says that his pelvis seems to drop to one side when he lifts his foot off the ground and that he’s had to lean his upper body toward the opposite side just to keep himself from falling over. He does not have any pain, numbness, or muscle cramps. He recently began a new exercise routine involving barbell squats and glute-focused workouts but does not recall any specific injury. He has no prior neurologic or orthopedic history. On physical exam, there is no muscle tenderness, leg length discrepancy, or joint abnormality. When asked to stand on his left leg, the examiner observes that his right hip dips lower than the left, creating a pelvic tilt. The patient instinctively leans his trunk toward the left side while walking to compensate. Strength in hip flexion and knee extension is normal bilaterally, and sensation and reflexes are intact. Gait testing reproduces the same lateral trunk shift. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient presents with a classic example of a Trendelenburg gait, which is characterized by pelvic drop on the side opposite the affected leg during the single-leg stance phase of walking. The underlying cause is weakness of the gluteus medius and gluteus minimus, which are responsible for abducting the hip and stabilizing the pelvis when the contralateral leg is lifted. These muscles are innervated by the superior gluteal nerve, making it the most likely site of injury. When the left superior gluteal nerve is damaged, the left gluteus medius and minimus are unable to contract effectively during single-leg stance on the left leg, causing the right side of the pelvis to dip downward. To compensate, patients often develop a lateral trunk shift or lean toward the affected side (left) during ambulation, in an effort to maintain balance and minimize the pelvic drop - a phenomenon tested on USMLE. In this case, the pelvic drop when standing on the left leg and the compensatory leftward trunk lean during walking together indicate injury to the left superior gluteal nerve. The absence of pain, joint dysfunction, or lower extremity motor or sensory deficits helps exclude orthopedic and other neurologic causes, supporting a focal motor neuropathy rather than a more generalized problem. Since the patient had recently begun exercises that place strain on the gluteal region, such as squats, the superior gluteal nerve may have been compressed or stretched, especially if poor form or excessive load was involved.
bottom_line: This patient presents with a classic example of a Trendelenburg gait, which is characterized by pelvic drop on the side opposite the affected leg during the single-leg stance phase of walking.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: Which of the following best describes the mechanism underlying this patient's condition?

options.A: Arachnoid granulation scarring impairing CSF absorption
options.C: Cerebellar tonsillar herniation compressing the fourth ventricle
options.E: Decreased CSF production by the choroid plexus
options.F: Obstruction of CSF flow at the foramen of Monro
options.G: Overproduction of CSF due to choroid plexus papilloma
options.H: Vasogenic edema due to increased capillary permeability

answer_text: Arachnoid granulation scarring impairing CSF absorption
explanation: This patient presents with the classic triad of normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH): gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive impairment. Imaging shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy, with normal or narrow sulci, indicating increased ventricular size without global atrophy. The underlying pathophysiology is thought to involve impaired absorption of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) at the arachnoid granulations, leading to communicating hydrocephalus despite normal opening pressures on lumbar puncture. This condition is distinct from obstructive (non-communicating) hydrocephalus and does not involve a discrete blockage in ventricular flow. Gait is often the most prominent early feature, described as magnetic or shuffling, followed by cognitive symptoms and incontinence. Timely diagnosis is important because ventriculoperitoneal shunting can improve symptoms. Communicating hydrocephalus is caused by impaired CSF absorption at the arachnoid granulations, often due to meningitis or subarachnoid hemorrhage, and results in enlargement of all ventricles with preserved flow between them. Non-communicating (obstructive) hydrocephalus occurs when a mass or congenital anomaly (e.g., aqueductal stenosis, Chiari malformation, colloid cyst) blocks CSF flow within the ventricular system, leading to dilation of ventricles proximal to the obstruction and normal distal ones. Normal pressure hydrocephalus is a chronic form of communicating hydrocephalus seen in the elderly, presenting with the classic triad of magnetic gait, urinary incontinence, and dementia, with normal CSF pressure on lumbar puncture and ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy.
bottom_line: This patient presents with the classic triad of normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH): gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive impairment.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 39-year-old man presents to the emergency department after developing sudden- onset low back pain while lifting heavy boxes during a move. The pain began abruptly when he twisted to his left while holding a box, and he describes it as sharp, shooting, and radiating down the posterior aspect of his left leg to the lateral foot. He reports tingling in his left foot and difficulty walking due to pain. He denies fever, recent trauma, weight loss, or prior similar episodes. He has no history of malignancy or IV drug use. On physical examination, he is afebrile with stable vital signs. He leans forward slightly and favors the right leg while walking. Straight leg raise is positive on the left at 30 degrees. Sensory testing shows decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the left foot. Motor testing reveals mild weakness with plantar flexion on the left. The left Achilles reflex is diminished. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?

options.A: Bacterial infection of the lumbar spine

explanation: This patient’s sudden-onset, knife-like low back pain following physical activity, with radiation down the leg into the dorsum of the foot and great toe, is consistent with lumbar radiculopathy. The description strongly suggests an acute herniated disc - specifically at the L4-L5 level - compressing the L5 nerve root. The annulus fibrosus is the tough outer layer of the intervertebral disc that can tear with sudden stress, allowing the nucleus pulposus to protrude and impinge on adjacent spinal nerve roots. The tilt in his posture is known as "sciatic scoliosis," a compensatory leaning away from the affected side to relieve nerve root tension. The diminished Achilles reflex suggests S1 involvement, which may also occur with an L5-S1 disc herniation. Patients with acute disc herniation often have radicular symptoms without systemic signs (fever, weight loss) and a normal neurological exam apart from localized reflex and sensory deficits. This presentation is classic for a mechanical, compressive etiology rather than infectious or malignant causes. Lumbar disc herniation due to tearing of the annulus fibrosus is a common cause of low back pain in otherwise healthy adults. It typically follows heavy lifting or twisting injuries and causes sharp, radiating pain that worsens with movement or strain. The reflex findings and specific dermatomal radiation help localize the affected root. In this case, the pain distribution into the great toe (L5) and diminished ankle reflex (S1) suggest a herniation at the L5-S1 junction. The diagnosis is clinical but may be confirmed with MRI if symptoms persist or neurological deficits worsen. Most cases improve with conservative therapy, including NSAIDs, physical therapy, and activity modification. Only patients with red flags (e.g., bowel/bladder dysfunction, progressive weakness) require urgent imaging and surgical referral.
bottom_line: This patient’s sudden-onset, knife-like low back pain following physical activity, with radiation down the leg into the dorsum of the foot and great toe, is consistent with lumbar radiculopathy.

subject: Neuro
