stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with double vision and difficulty focusing while reading. She mentions a past episode of numbness in both legs and, more recently, transient right arm weakness, both of which resolved without treatment. On neurologic examination, her left eye fails to adduct during rightward gaze, while the right eye abducts with nystagmus. Convergence is preserved, and pupils are equal and reactive to light. Vertical gaze and the remainder of her cranial nerve exam are normal. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her current symptoms?

options.A: Demyelination of the left medial longitudinal fasciculus
options.B: Demyelination of the right medial longitudinal fasciculus
options.C: Lesion of the left abducens nerve (CN VI)
options.D: Lesion of the left oculomotor nerve (CN III)
options.E: Uncal herniation with compression of CN III

answer_text: Demyelination of the left medial longitudinal fasciculus
explanation: This pattern is internuclear ophthalmoplegia from a left medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion. Failure of the left eye to adduct on rightward gaze with preserved convergence is classic for demyelination of the left MLF in multiple sclerosis.
bottom_line: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia in MS localizes to the ipsilateral medial longitudinal fasciculus.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department after sustaining a stab wound to the upper right arm during a street altercation. He reports weakness in bending his elbow and difficulty turning his palm upward. On physical examination, there is a superficial laceration over the anterolateral aspect of the upper arm. He is unable to flex the elbow against resistance, and supination is markedly weakened. Sensation is diminished over the lateral forearm. The remainder of the neurologic examination is normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: The musculocutaneous nerve arises from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus and innervates the biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis muscles, all of which contribute to flexion at the elbow and supination of the forearm. After supplying these muscles, it continues as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm, providing sensory innervation to the lateral forearm. Injury to this nerve results in weakness in elbow flexion and forearm supination, along with numbness or paresthesia over the lateral forearm. Because the nerve innervates muscles proximal to the wrist, hand and wrist movements remain unaffected. This pattern is most commonly seen with penetrating trauma to the anterolateral upper arm. The localization of both motor and sensory deficits to the proximal upper extremity helps differentiate musculocutaneous nerve injury from damage to the median or radial nerves. Median nerve injury typically presents with impaired wrist flexion and thenar muscle weakness, while radial nerve injury results in wrist drop and dorsal hand sensory loss. Isolated sensory loss without weakness might suggest involvement of purely sensory branches like the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm. In contrast, a musculocutaneous lesion includes both motor and sensory deficits, but only in specific regions, making the diagnosis anatomically distinct. The preservation of distal motor function further supports localization to a more proximal, selective nerve injury.
bottom_line: The musculocutaneous nerve arises from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus and innervates the biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis muscles, all of which contribute to flexion at the elbow and supination of the forearm.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 20-year-old amateur boxer presents to the clinic with right arm weakness that began after an intense training session five days ago. He recalls straining during a grappling exchange and feeling a sharp pull in his upper arm. Since then, he has had difficulty flexing the elbow, particularly when trying to bring food to his mouth or lift a water bottle. He denies numbness in the hand or fingers but notes that the outer part of the forearm feels strange when touched. He has no prior medical problems and takes no medications. On examination, the right arm shows no swelling, ecchymosis, or deformity. There is weakness in elbow flexion and forearm supination. The biceps reflex is reduced compared to the left. Strength in wrist and finger extension is intact. Sensory testing reveals decreased sensation over the lateral forearm, while sensation in the hand and upper arm remains normal. Triceps strength and reflexes are preserved. Based on the diagram provided, which of the labeled nerves (A-E) is most likely affected in this patient?
explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of musculocutaneous nerve injury, which is a rare but well-defined brachial plexus lesion that causes weakness of elbow flexion and sensory loss over the lateral forearm. The musculocutaneous nerve arises from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus (C5-C7) and innervates the biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis muscles. These muscles are responsible for elbow flexion and forearm supination, especially when lifting with the palm up. Injury may result from traction, such as in combat sports, or compression during anterior shoulder surgeries. The key motor deficit is weakness in elbow flexion, often most noticeable during supination. A diminished biceps reflex is another clue on USMLE. Importantly, there is no involvement of the wrist or hand, helping to distinguish this injury from more extensive brachial plexus damage or distal nerve injuries. Preserved triceps and wrist function help localize the lesion to the musculocutaneous nerve rather than the radial nerve or entire lateral cord. In addition to motor signs, the musculocutaneous nerve provides cutaneous sensation to the lateral forearm through its terminal branch, the lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve. This patient's sensory complaint in that exact region - paired with an otherwise intact upper extremity - supports an isolated musculocutaneous lesion. In the image provided, label A correctly corresponds to the musculocutaneous nerve. This structure branches from the lateral cord, pierces the coracobrachialis, travels between the biceps and brachialis, and terminates as the sensory nerve to the lateral forearm.
bottom_line: This patient presents with classic signs of musculocutaneous nerve injury, which is a rare but well-defined brachial plexus lesion that causes weakness of elbow flexion and sensory loss over the lateral forearm.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely associated clinical finding?

options.C: Loss of sensation over the palmar fifth digit

explanation: The radial nerve runs along the spiral groove of the humerus and is especially vulnerable to injury in midshaft humeral fractures. As it wraps posteriorly around the humerus, trauma in this region can cause neuropraxia or more serious axonotmesis. Injury to the radial nerve impairs motor function in the extensor compartment of the forearm, resulting in classic "wrist drop"-inability to extend the wrist and fingers. This patient’s wrist resting in flexion, along with finger extension weakness and sensory loss over the dorsal web space between the thumb and index finger, confirms radial nerve involvement. Wrist extension is necessary for proper grip strength and hand positioning. Without wrist stabilization by the extensor muscles, flexor function is weakened, leading to reduced grip. Sensory loss in the dorsum of the hand, particularly the first web space, is a reliable early clue to radial nerve injury. Most radial nerve injuries associated with closed, non- displaced humeral fractures are neuropraxias and recover spontaneously with conservative treatment, such as wrist splinting and close follow-up. However, persistent motor or sensory deficits may require electromyography or surgical exploration. This pattern-midshaft humeral fracture with radial nerve palsy-is a classic USMLE association. It is important to monitor neurologic status closely and avoid unnecessary surgical intervention unless there are signs of entrapment, open fracture, or failure to improve over time.
bottom_line: The radial nerve runs along the spiral groove of the humerus and is especially vulnerable to injury in midshaft humeral fractures.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 71-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after his wife noticed he was "missing things" on the left side of his visual field. He denies vision loss but has started bumping into objects on his left and leaves food uneaten on the left side of his plate. He is right-handed and has no difficulty with speech. Visual field testing reveals a left homonymous hemianopia, but central vision is preserved. Neurologic exam is otherwise normal. Which of the following vascular territories is most likely affected?

options.H: Watershed zone between MCA and ACA

explanation: This patient presents with a left homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing, which is classic for an infarct in the right occipital cortex, supplied by the right posterior cerebral artery (PCA). The occipital cortex processes visual information from the contralateral visual field, so a right-sided lesion results in loss of the left visual field in both eyes. Macular sparing occurs because the macula receives dual blood supply from both the PCA and collateral branches of the middle cerebral artery (MCA), preserving central vision despite a PCA infarct. The absence of language deficits or motor weakness supports an isolated PCA infarct rather than an MCA stroke. This presentation is common in older adults with embolic disease or atherosclerosis affecting the PCA.
bottom_line: This patient presents with a left homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing, which is classic for an infarct in the right occipital cortex, supplied by the right posterior cerebral artery (PCA).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 29-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motorcycle crash. Imaging shows a right lateral frontal lobe contusion. He is found to have complete paralysis of the left upper extremity. Additional injuries include a fractured right femur and left tibia, both requiring long-term cast immobilization. After 10 weeks, his lower extremity casts are removed, and deep tendon reflexes are evaluated. Which of the following reflexes is most likely to be increased in this patient?
explanation: The triceps reflex is mediated by the radial nerve and spinal levels C7 to C8. This patient has a right frontal lobe contusion, which represents an upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion affecting the contralateral (left) upper limb. UMN issues lead to loss of descending inhibition, which classically results in hyperreflexia and spasticity. So in this case, the left triceps tendon reflex would be increased. UMN findings are classically hyperreflexia, hypertonia (or spastic paralysis), Babinski sign, and clonus. Lower motor neuron (LMN) findings are classically hyporeflexia, hypotonia (or flaccid paralysis), atrophy, and fasciculations.
bottom_line: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: High Yield USMLE Audio Answer:

subject: Neuro
stem: A 62-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife due to progressively worsening difficulty walking over the past 4 months. He has been experiencing frequent falls and describes his legs as feeling “stuck” when he tries to walk. His wife adds that he has started having episodes of urinary incontinence, often wetting himself when they are out of the house. She is also concerned about increasing forgetfulness, such as misplacing items and repeating questions. He has no significant psychiatric or neurologic history and does not take any medications. On physical examination, his gait is wide- based and unsteady. He has difficulty initiating steps, and his movements are slow and shuffling. No tremors or rigidity are noted. Cognitive testing shows mild deficits in attention and recall. MRI of the brain reveals ventricular enlargement out of proportion to cortical atrophy. Which of the following is most likely to be seen in this patient?

options.B: Autoimmune demyelination of central nervous system white matter
options.D: Decreased CSF absorption by arachnoid granulations

answer_text: Autoimmune demyelination of central nervous system white matter
explanation: This patient exhibits Gerstmann syndrome, a classic constellation of four deficits: agraphia (impaired writing), acalculia (impaired calculation), finger agnosia (inability to identify fingers), and left-right disorientation. These functions are localized to the angular gyrus, part of the dominant inferior parietal lobule, typically in the left hemisphere. Although language comprehension and fluency are intact, the patient's difficulties with writing and calculation reflect disruption of multimodal integration in this region. The angular gyrus is also involved in reading, spatial processing, and arithmetic, making it a high-yield cortical area for cognitive syndromes. It lies just posterior to Wernicke’s area and superior to the temporal lobe, bridging language and spatial attention pathways. Gerstmann syndrome can result from vascular insults (e.g., MCA branch infarcts) or focal cortical lesions.
bottom_line: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: High Yield USMLE Audio Answer:

subject: Neuro
stem: A 62-year-old right-handed woman is brought to the clinic by her daughter due to increasing confusion over the past few weeks. The daughter notes that her mother can speak fluently and seems to understand conversations but has been making frequent errors when writing checks or addressing envelopes. On examination, the patient is alert and fluent, but she cannot perform simple subtraction or identify her fingers when asked. She has difficulty distinguishing left from right and is unable to write her name when given pen and paper. Language comprehension and motor strength are intact. Which of the following brain regions is most likely affected?
explanation: This is Gerstmann syndrome: agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and left-right disorientation with preserved fluency and comprehension. Those findings localize to the dominant inferior parietal lobule, specifically the angular gyrus.
bottom_line: Gerstmann syndrome localizes to the dominant angular gyrus.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which letter on the sagittal brain cross-section most likely corresponds to the site of her injury?
explanation: The cerebellar hemispheres regulate the planning and fine coordination of movements in the ipsilateral limbs by integrating input from the contralateral cerebral cortex and peripheral proprioceptive feedback. The hemispheres ensure that voluntary movements are smooth and accurate, explaining why this patient presents with right-sided dysmetria, intention tremor, and difficulty with precision tasks. The double-crossed nature of cerebellar circuitry results in ipsilateral clinical findings: corticopontocerebellar fibers (from cortex to cerebellum) cross in the pons before reaching the cerebellum, and then the cerebellar efferents (from cerebellum to cortex) cross again before reaching the motor cortex. This anatomic relationship explains why cerebellar hemisphere lesions result in ipsilateral limb incoordination without weakness. Cerebellar hemisphere damage can result from infarction, hemorrhage, trauma, or mass lesions such as tumors. The superior cerebellar and posterior inferior cerebellar arteries supply this region, making it vulnerable to ischemic stroke in these distributions. Management depends on the etiology but often includes supportive care, treatment of the underlying vascular risk factors, and rehabilitation to improve coordination and functional recovery.
bottom_line: The cerebellar hemispheres regulate the planning and fine coordination of movements in the ipsilateral limbs by integrating input from the contralateral cerebral cortex and peripheral proprioceptive feedback.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Primary CNS lymphoma is a high-yield and often underrated diagnosis on USMLE exams. It should be strongly suspected in AIDS patients with focal neurological deficits, CD4 count <100, and a ring-enhancing lesion on imaging, particularly if they are already on TMP-SMX prophylaxis (which reduces likelihood of toxoplasmosis). Pathogenesis involves EBV-driven B-cell proliferation and the lymphoma is a type of high-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma confined to the CNS. CT or MRI may show a solitary or few lesions with homogeneous ring enhancement and a predilection for deep structures such as basal ganglia. Unlike toxoplasmosis, which typically presents with multiple lesions, primary CNS lymphoma is often solitary and associated with immunosuppression. It can also occur in non-AIDS patients who are immunosuppressed (e.g., transplant recipients, chemotherapy patients). Additionally, autoimmune diseases (e.g., SLE, dermatomyositis, Hashimoto thyroiditis) are associated with an increased risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma including CNS involvement. Treatment typically involves high-dose methotrexate-based regimens rather than empiric antibiotics.
bottom_line: Primary CNS lymphoma is a high-yield and often underrated diagnosis on USMLE exams.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: A child with bitemporal visual field loss, growth failure, delayed puberty, polyuria, and a suprasellar mass is most consistent with craniopharyngioma. These tumors arise from Rathke pouch remnants and commonly cause visual and endocrine dysfunction.
bottom_line: Craniopharyngioma causes suprasellar mass effects with visual loss and endocrine dysfunction in children.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of hypertension and diabetes presents with sudden onset of right-sided weakness. On examination, he has weakness of the right face, arm, and leg. Strength is equally reduced in both proximal and distal muscles. Sensation, coordination, language, and cranial nerve examination are normal. MRI reveals a small lacunar infarct. Which of the following structures is most likely involved?
explanation: This patient has a pure motor stroke due to infarction of the posterior limb (horn) of the internal capsule, which carries corticospinal and corticobulbar fibers. Damage here produces contralateral weakness of the face, arm, and leg without any sensory loss, speech disturbance, or cranial nerve findings. Lenticulostriate vessels (lacunar) strokes from chronic hypertension and diabetes are the most common cause as lipohyalunosis occurs. Anatomy
bottom_line: This patient has a pure motor stroke due to infarction of the posterior limb (horn) of the internal capsule, which carries corticospinal and corticobulbar fibers.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 67-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after slipping on ice and hitting the back of his head. He briefly lost consciousness but was alert and responsive within minutes. Emergency personnel report that he vomited twice en route to the hospital. In the ED, he is mildly confused but oriented and reports a lingering dull headache. His symptoms have gradually improved over the last hour. He denies visual changes, seizures, or weakness. There are no focal neurological deficits on exam, and he is speaking coherently. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He is not taking any anticoagulant medications. After a brief period of observation, he is awake, cooperative, and ambulatory, though still mildly fatigued. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Concussion is a mild traumatic brain injury that results from blunt force trauma to the head, often accompanied by brief loss of consciousness, vomiting, headache, and confusion. This patient demonstrates these features without ongoing neurologic deficits or signs of deterioration, and his symptoms are resolving over time. Although imaging is often performed to exclude more serious pathology-particularly in older adults-the diagnosis of concussion remains clinical and is supported by the rapid improvement in his condition. Most patients recover fully within days to weeks. The mechanism involves transient functional disruption of neuronal signaling rather than structural brain damage, which is why standard imaging studies often appear normal. This differentiates concussion from more serious injuries like contusions or intracranial hemorrhages, which would present with prolonged or worsening symptoms and clear abnormalities on CT. Management is primarily supportive, emphasizing physical and cognitive rest and gradual return to normal activities. Monitoring is essential in the first 24-48 hours to identify any signs of delayed complications.
bottom_line: Choice A. Cerebral contusion typically produces CT-visible parenchymal bruising and presents with more persistent neurologic symptoms. This patient’s improving condition makes contusion less likely. Choice C. Epidural hematoma is classically associated with trauma to the middle meningeal artery and can present with an initial lucid interval followed by deterioration of mental status. Patients may develop symptoms such as headache, vomiting, seizures, and even coma. Epidural hematomas can rapidly expand and cause brain herniation syndromes, making them a neurosurgical emergency. However, this patient's symptoms are improving rather than worsening, and there is no mention of a lucid interval followed by decline. Choice D. Intracerebral hemorrhage refers to bleeding within the brain parenchyma, most commonly due to hypertension or vascular malformations. It typically presents with acute neurologic symptoms such as headache, vomiting, altered mental status, and focal deficits like hemiparesis or hemisensory loss. Some patients may rapidly decline into stupor or coma, especially with larger hemorrhages. In this case, the patient's vomiting and transient confusion have improved without focal findings or ongoing mental status changes. Choice E. Subarachnoid hemorrhage usually presents with sudden, severe headache often described as a “thunderclap,” which this patient does not report. Choice F. Subdural hematoma results from rupture of bridging veins, often after head trauma, and may be acute or chronic. Acute cases usually present with persistent or worsening symptoms in patients with more severe trauma or underlying coagulopathy, while chronic subdural hematomas in older adults develop gradually over days to weeks. This patient’s immediate but improving symptoms after trauma are inconsistent with either form, making subdural hematoma unlikely.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely risk factor that predisposed this patient to this condition?
explanation: Aging is the most important risk factor for chronic subdural hematoma, especially in the absence of significant trauma. With advancing age, cerebral atrophy leads to stretching of the bridging veins across the widened subdural space, making them more vulnerable to rupture even with trivial trauma, such as a minor fall. These tears allow venous blood to slowly accumulate in the subdural space, resulting in a crescent-shaped lesion that may take days to weeks to cause symptoms such as confusion, gait disturbances, and focal neurological deficits. Additionally, aging is often associated with other factors that can compound the risk of bleeding, including reduced vessel compliance and neurodegenerative conditions like dementia, which further reduce brain volume. The slow accumulation of blood explains the delayed presentation in many elderly patients, and there is no lucid interval, which differentiates it from epidural hematoma. CT imaging typically shows a hypodense or isodense crescent-shaped lesion that can cross suture lines.
bottom_line: Subarachnoid hemorrhage.

subject: Neuro
stem: Where on the diagram is the most appropriate location for surgical entry into the patient's spinal canal?
explanation: The lamina forms the posterior wall of the vertebral canal and is the key structure removed during a laminectomy to expose and decompress the spinal cord and nerve roots. Laminectomy is a classic posterior approach to spinal decompression used to treat spinal stenosis and involves excision of the lamina and potentially the ligamentum flavum to directly decompress neural elements. Removal of the lamina creates space for the neural elements by widening the spinal canal posteriorly and relieving compression from structures such as hypertrophied ligamentum flavum and osteoarthritic facet joints. The lamina can be differentiated from other vertebral structures because it forms part of the vertebral arch directly overlying the canal, unlike the transverse process or vertebral body. The spinous process itself does not provide entry but may be removed in the exposure phase of surgery; true canal entry requires lamina resection.
bottom_line: The lamina forms the posterior wall of the vertebral canal and is the key structure removed during a laminectomy to expose and decompress the spinal cord and nerve roots.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 70-year-old right-handed man is brought to the emergency department due to sudden-onset weakness of his right arm and face. He is unable to grip objects with his right hand, cannot raise his right eyebrow, and has drooping of the right corner of his mouth. His right leg strength is preserved, and he has no sensory loss or visual changes. He is alert and oriented, with intact language and cognitive function. Medical history includes hypertension and atrial fibrillation. A non-contrast head CT confirms the diagnosis. Which of the following best describes the location of the lesion responsible for this patient’s symptoms?
explanation: ABCD are all the left side of the brain. EFGH are all the right side of the brain. When the cerebral hemisphere is involved, the stroke findings will always be contralateral because of decussation of the nerve fibers as they travel through the brain. So the fact that his right face and arm are affected, we know the lesion is on the left cerebral hemisphere. Since the stroke is specifically of the face and arm, so we know we're dealing with the lateral cerebral hemisphere based on the homunculus (our cartoon drawing below). The leg, in contrast, would instead be the medial cerebral hemisphere.
bottom_line: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: High Yield USMLE Audio Answer:

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles

Recurrent branch of median nerve G. Superficial branch of radial nerve H. Ulnar nerve at cubital tunnel A 34-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being found unconscious on a park bench. He had been drinking heavily and was lying with his right arm draped over the backrest for several hours. On awakening, he notices that he cannot extend his wrist or fingers. On examination, there is weakness of wrist and finger extension, and the hand hangs limply. Sensation is diminished over the dorsal surface of the forearm and the dorsum of the hand, particularly the first web space. Triceps reflex is preserved. There is no swelling, trauma, or tenderness of the elbow or wrist. Which of the following nerves is most likely affected?

options.B: Deep branch of radial nerve (posterior interosseous nerve)
options.E: Radial nerve in the spiral groove of humerus

answer_text: Radial nerve in the spiral groove of humerus
explanation: This patient has classic findings of radial nerve injury in the midshaft (spiral groove) of the humerus, often referred to as “Saturday night palsy.” This occurs when prolonged compression of the arm-such as draping it over a bench or during anesthesia-leads to neuropraxia of the radial nerve. The radial nerve innervates the extensor muscles of the wrist and fingers, and damage results in wrist drop due to loss of extension. Sensory loss typically includes the dorsal hand, especially the first web space. The triceps muscle (elbow extension) is spared because the branch to triceps comes off proximal to the spiral groove. This injury is motor and sensory, unlike more distal radial nerve injuries which may spare sensation or extensor function. Management is usually conservative with splinting and physical therapy, and most cases recover spontaneously within weeks to months.
bottom_line: This patient has classic findings of radial nerve injury in the midshaft (spiral groove) of the humerus, often referred to as “Saturday night palsy.” This occurs when prolonged compression of the arm-such as draping it over a bench or during anesthesia-leads to neuropraxia of the radial nerve.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 29-year-old man presents to the emergency department after falling onto his outstretched arm during a recreational soccer match. He reports pain and a feeling of weakness in his upper limb, especially when trying to lift or carry items. On physical examination, he has noticeable difficulty with elbow flexion and reports decreased grip strength. He denies numbness in the hand or shoulder. Sensory testing reveals diminished pinprick sensation over the outer portion of his forearm. There is no visible deformity, and passive range of motion at the shoulder and elbow is preserved. Reflexes at the biceps are reduced on the affected side. Radiographs of the humerus and forearm are unremarkable. He has no prior history of neurologic or musculoskeletal disease. The physician suspects a peripheral nerve injury due to trauma involving a specific upper limb structure. Which of the following nerve roots is most likely affected in this patient?
explanation: This patient’s decreased elbow flexion and loss of pinprick sensation over the lateral forearm indicate injury to the musculocutaneous nerve, which carries fibers from C5 to C7. The musculocutaneous nerve arises from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus and innervates the coracobrachialis, biceps brachii, and brachialis muscles, all of which contribute to flexion at the elbow and supination of the forearm. After supplying these muscles, it continues as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm, which provides sensory innervation to the skin over the lateral forearm.
bottom_line: This patient’s decreased elbow flexion and loss of pinprick sensation over the lateral forearm indicate injury to the musculocutaneous nerve, which carries fibers from C5 to C7.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 37-year-old man presents to the clinic with a complaint of recurrent, sharp headaches over the past three weeks. He describes the pain as excruciating, strictly localized around his right eye, and says it peaks within 10 minutes of onset. Each episode lasts about 75 minutes and occurs almost daily, often at the same time each evening. He reports nasal congestion and tearing of the right eye during attacks but denies visual changes, phonophobia, or nausea. He has not experienced any recent trauma or fever. He has had similar bouts in the past lasting several weeks, with pain-free intervals of months to years. Neurologic examination between attacks is normal, and there is no family history of headaches. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Cluster headache is characterized by severe, unilateral, periorbital or temporal pain that is strictly one-sided, typically lasting 15-180 minutes (1-3 hours). Attacks often occur in clusters (bouts) over several weeks, followed by periods of remission. Autonomic symptoms such as ipsilateral lacrimation, conjunctival injection, and nasal congestion are hallmark features due to activation of the trigeminal-autonomic reflex. The pathophysiology involves hypothalamic activation and subsequent stimulation of the trigeminal nerve and parasympathetic outflow. Patients often describe episodes as occurring at the same time each day, sometimes waking them from sleep. First-line abortive therapy is sumatriptan (a 5-HT1B/1D agonist) or high-flow oxygen, and prevention includes verapamil. Cluster headaches are much more common in men and are associated with restlessness or agitation during attacks (patients may pace or rock back and forth).
bottom_line: Cluster headache is characterized by severe, unilateral, periorbital or temporal pain that is strictly one-sided, typically lasting 15-180 minutes (1-3 hours).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic because of recurrent headaches for the past two months. The headaches are described as sudden, severe, stabbing pain around his right eye, associated with tearing and nasal congestion on the same side. Episodes last approximately 45 minutes and occur once or twice daily, often waking him from sleep in the early morning. He reports pacing around the room during these attacks due to the intensity of the pain. He denies photophobia, phonophobia, or aura. Neurological examination is normal. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. He does not smoke but drinks two beers per night. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy in this patient?
explanation: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder characterized by severe, unilateral periorbital or temporal pain that is sharp and lancinating, typically lasting 15 minutes to 3 hours. Attacks often occur in "clusters," meaning repeated episodes daily for weeks followed by periods of remission, and classically wake patients from sleep. Autonomic features such as lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, and ptosis are common and help differentiate cluster headache from migraine or trigeminal neuralgia. The condition is much more common in males. The most appropriate acute treatment is 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask, which aborts attacks quickly by reversing hypothesized vasodilation and trigeminovascular activation. Other abortive options include subcutaneous sumatriptan but oxygen is typically preferred as first-line because of its rapid effect and favorable side-effect profile. Prophylactic treatment is necessary during a cluster period, and verapamil is the drug of choice for prevention. Importantly, cluster headache differs from migraine in duration, associated symptoms, and response to therapy, making recognition of its clinical features critical for correct management.
bottom_line: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder characterized by severe, unilateral periorbital or temporal pain that is sharp and lancinating, typically lasting 15 minutes to 3 hours.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Dandy-Walker malformation is a congenital brain malformation characterized by agenesis or hypoplasia of the cerebellar vermis, cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, and enlargement of the posterior fossa. It often presents in infancy with signs of hydrocephalus, such as vomiting, macrocephaly, and a bulging fontanelle. Hypotonia and delayed motor development are common due to cerebellar dysfunction. MRI is diagnostic and shows the characteristic findings described in this vignette. This malformation results from disruption of normal cerebellar development and is often associated with other CNS anomalies, including agenesis of the corpus callosum.
bottom_line: Dandy-Walker malformation is a congenital brain malformation characterized by agenesis or hypoplasia of the cerebellar vermis, cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, and enlargement of the posterior fossa.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 62-year-old man is evaluated in clinic due to difficulty getting around at home. He says that over the past few weeks, he has found it increasingly hard to stand up from his couch or toilet seat, and he often needs to use his hands to push off. He has also begun avoiding stairs, as going both up and down feels “weak and awkward.” He denies any numbness, tingling, pain, or leg instability. His symptoms are isolated to strength-based movements involving the hip. He does not recall any injury, fall, or new physical activity. On examination, motor strength testing reveals difficulty extending the hip from a seated position. When asked to perform a squat, the patient requires support and cannot maintain the posture without assistance. He is able to abduct and adduct the legs against resistance. Reflexes and sensation are intact throughout the lower extremities, and gait appears stable once upright. Straight leg raise is negative, and no tenderness or joint abnormalities are noted. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient’s presentation of difficulty rising from seated positions, inability to squat, and trouble with stair navigation localizes the problem to hip extension, a movement largely driven by the gluteus maximus. The inferior gluteal nerve, which innervates the gluteus maximus, is the primary motor source responsible for these powerful extension activities. Because the patient denies numbness or pain, and only experiences weakness in tasks requiring hip extension, a focal motor lesion is the most likely etiology. The clinical vignette carefully isolates symptoms to difficulty activating the gluteus maximus, with preserved abduction, adduction, and gait once standing, pointing specifically to impaired extension, not balance or stability. The gluteus maximus is especially important in initiating movement from a seated position, stair climbing, and rising from squats, which are the precise activities the patient struggles with. Importantly, his sensation and reflexes are intact, making a broader lumbosacral or sciatic nerve issue less likely. Injury to the inferior gluteal nerve can occur during posterior hip surgeries, injections, trauma, or compression near the greater sciatic foramen. While this patient reports no clear inciting event, age-related gluteal atrophy or repetitive compression in sedentary postures may cause subtle neuropathic dysfunction.
bottom_line: This patient’s presentation of difficulty rising from seated positions, inability to squat, and trouble with stair navigation localizes the problem to hip extension, a movement largely driven by the gluteus maximus.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 72-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his daughter due to progressively worsening memory and difficulty walking over the past 3 months. She reports that he has become increasingly forgetful, often misplacing items and repeating questions. He has also had multiple falls, and she has noticed he sometimes urinates on himself before reaching the bathroom. He denies headaches, nausea, or recent illness. On examination, the patient walks with short, hesitant steps and needs assistance to maintain balance. Neurologic examination reveals mild cognitive impairment and impaired tandem gait but no focal neurological deficits. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition?
explanation: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the central nervous system caused by autoreactive T lymphocytes attacking oligodendrocytes, which produce myelin in the CNS. This demyelination disrupts saltatory conduction, leading to slowed neural transmission and diverse neurologic deficits that occur with dissemination in space and time. MS typically affects young women in temperate climates and presents with a relapsing-remitting course characterized by optic neuritis (painful monocular vision loss), internuclear ophthalmoplegia, limb weakness, sensory disturbances, ataxia, and neurogenic bladder (urge incontinence). MRI shows multifocal hyperintense plaques, especially in periventricular white matter, and cerebrospinal fluid analysis reveals oligoclonal IgG bands. Acute MS relapses are treated with high-dose intravenous corticosteroids such as methylprednisolone to reduce inflammation and speed recovery. Long-term disease-modifying therapy (e.g., interferon-β, glatiramer acetate, or monoclonal antibodies such as natalizumab) is used to reduce relapse frequency and slow disease progression. Symptom-specific treatments include baclofen for spasticity, anticholinergic agents for bladder dysfunction, and physical therapy for mobility and balance issues. Careful monitoring for complications like depression, fatigue, and progressive disability is essential in comprehensive MS management.
bottom_line: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: High Yield USMLE Audio Answer: Choice A. Seen in Parkinson disease and Lewy body dementia. Parkinson presents with resting tremors, bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, and postural instability. However the "wet, wobby, wacky" triad is not classic in Parkinson disease the way it is in NPH. Lewy body dementia presents as dementia + visual hallucinations and Parkinsonism. Alpha-synuclein deposits are in the substantia nigra pars compact of the midbrain in Parkinson disease, and additionally in the cerebral cortex and hippocampus in Lewy body dementia. Choice B. This is the hallmark of Alzheimer disease. It causes progressive cognitive decline, presenting earlier with episodic memory loss and later progressing to language deficits and personality changes. Gait instability and incontinence occur late. Alzheimer doesn't traditionally present with "wet, wobbly, wacky" or Parkinsonism. There will be no motor or sensory abnormalities in Alzheimer. Brain imaging can show diffuse cerebral atrophy. There is early-onset Alzheimer in Down syndrome. Choice C. This is characteristic of frontotemporal dementia (FTD), which on USMLE presents as a triad of apathy, disinhibition, and personality change. Choice E. This causes non-communicating (obstructive) hydrocephalus, typically leading to increased intracranial pressure with headache, nausea, vomiting, and papilledema. It often occurs in children or patients with congenital malformations, tumors, or hemorrhage. In NPH, CSF flows freely but is impaired in absorption, and there are no signs of raised ICP.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 53-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic for a routine follow-up appointment. She appears well and reports that she has been recovering steadily since undergoing a modified radical mastectomy for breast cancer approximately one year ago. She denies pain or swelling in the chest wall but notes occasional discomfort in her upper back and some difficulty with overhead activities, such as placing objects on high shelves. On physical examination, she demonstrates full range of motion in the upper extremities but has visible prominence of the medial border of her right scapula when she pushes against a wall with outstretched arms. There is no associated redness, swelling, or warmth. Strength is intact in the upper limbs, except for mild weakness in shoulder abduction past 90 degrees. Sensory exam is normal. Which of the following nerves was most likely injured during this patient’s surgery?
explanation: The long thoracic nerve is responsible for innervating the serratus anterior muscle, which plays a vital role in stabilizing the scapula against the thoracic wall, particularly during arm elevation. Injury to this nerve results in the characteristic “winged scapula,” where the medial border of the scapula protrudes posteriorly, especially when the patient performs pushing movements such as pressing against a wall. This sign reflects weakness or paralysis of the serratus anterior, which fails to hold the scapula in place. This patient had a mastectomy one year ago, and although she has generally recovered well, she now shows signs of long thoracic nerve injury-a known complication of surgical disruption to the lateral chest wall, especially during axillary lymph node dissection or aggressive mobilization of tissues. The long thoracic nerve courses superficially along the midaxillary line, making it particularly vulnerable during breast surgery. The clinical finding of a winged scapula, in conjunction with mild difficulty in arm elevation, further supports serratus anterior dysfunction. The preserved sensation and largely intact strength in other muscle groups also point to a localized motor deficit rather than a more global plexopathy. Moreover, the lack of scapular retraction or downward rotation indicates the rhomboid and trapezius muscles are intact, ruling out other nerve injuries.
bottom_line: The long thoracic nerve is responsible for innervating the serratus anterior muscle, which plays a vital role in stabilizing the scapula against the thoracic wall, particularly during arm elevation.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic due to a 1-week history of pain radiating down the back of his right leg. He says the pain began after lifting a heavy box and initially started in the lower back, later spreading to the buttock, posterior thigh, and the bottom of his foot. He has difficulty walking and says pushing off with his right foot feels weak. He denies bowel or bladder issues but mentions that the pain worsens when standing or walking for long periods. On physical exam, strength is 4/5 with plantarflexion on the right. There is diminished sensation over the posterior calf and sole of the right foot. The right Achilles reflex is absent. Straight leg raise reproduces his pain at 30 degrees on the affected side. Which of the following nerve roots is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient exhibits classic signs of S1 radiculopathy, including posterior leg pain, sensory loss over the sole, weak plantarflexion, and absent Achilles reflex. The symptoms began acutely after lifting, which is consistent with an L5-S1 disc herniation, the most common cause of S1 root compression. The gastrocnemius and soleus, innervated by S1, are responsible for plantarflexion, and weakness in these muscles explains the difficulty pushing off during gait. The Achilles tendon reflex is mediated by S1 and is often lost in such cases-this reflex loss is a hallmark sign that helps localize the lesion. The sensory changes in the posterior calf and sole of the foot match the S1 dermatome, and the pain reproduced by straight-leg raising supports nerve root irritation. These findings distinguish S1 radiculopathy from L4 and L5, which involve different reflexes, dermatomes, and motor functions. Importantly, L4 radiculopathy affects the patellar reflex and knee extension, and L5 radiculopathy causes dorsiflexion weakness with sensory changes over the dorsum of the foot. In contrast, this patient’s presentation includes plantarflexion weakness, loss of ankle reflex, and sensory symptoms in the sole, all pointing clearly to S1. The absence of bowel/bladder issues or widespread deficits also rules out more severe central lesions.
bottom_line: This patient exhibits classic signs of S1 radiculopathy, including posterior leg pain, sensory loss over the sole, weak plantarflexion, and absent Achilles reflex.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic due to progressive difficulty walking and muscle cramping over the past several months. He has also noticed twitching in his arms and legs and has recently begun tripping when walking. He denies any numbness, tingling, or bladder or bowel symptoms. He has no history of trauma or back pain. Neurologic exam shows increased muscle tone in the lower extremities and brisk patellar reflexes. There is muscle wasting and fasciculations in both arms, with 4/5 strength in the hand grip bilaterally. Sensation to pinprick, vibration, and proprioception is intact. Babinski sign is present bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with both upper motor neuron (UMN) signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign) and lower motor neuron (LMN) signs (muscle atrophy, fasciculations, and weakness), with no sensory involvement-a hallmark of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). ALS involves progressive degeneration of both anterior horn cells (LMNs) and the lateral corticospinal tract (UMNs). The disease typically presents in middle-aged or older adults with asymmetric limb weakness, eventually spreading to involve bulbar muscles, respiratory muscles, and ultimately causing death. Importantly, sensation remains intact in ALS because sensory tracts are spared, which distinguishes it from most other spinal cord syndromes. There is no bowel/bladder dysfunction early in the disease..
bottom_line: This patient presents with both upper motor neuron (UMN) signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign) and lower motor neuron (LMN) signs (muscle atrophy, fasciculations, and weakness), with no sensory involvement-a hallmark of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 69-year-old man presents because of sudden weakness that started while having breakfast. He has weakness of his right arm and right leg, decreased vibration and proprioception on the right side of his body, and his tongue deviates to the left when protruded. He has no facial weakness, hoarseness, or swallowing difficulty. MRI shows a small infarct in the medulla. The image below shows a cross-section of the medulla at the level of his lesion. Where is the lesion most likely located?
explanation: Medial medullary syndrome results from an infarct involving the paramedian branches of the anterior spinal artery or the vertebral artery, damaging the medial portion of the medulla. The characteristic triad includes contralateral hemiparesis due to corticospinal tract involvement, contralateral loss of vibration and proprioception from medial lemniscus damage, and ipsilateral tongue weakness with deviation toward the lesion side from hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) involvement. In this case, right-sided weakness and impaired vibration/proprioception, along with tongue deviation to the left, indicate a lesion on the left side of the medulla. Preservation of facial strength and the absence of dysphagia or hoarseness help differentiate it from lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome, which involves the posterior inferior cerebellar artery and lateral medullary structures. The lesion localizes to the medial medulla at the level of the hypoglossal nucleus and exiting fibers. MRI confirmation often shows a small infarct in this paramedian region. Understanding the vascular supply is critical because the anterior spinal artery supplies the medial medulla bilaterally, and occlusion of one branch can produce unilateral findings. Early recognition is essential to distinguish it from cortical or pontine strokes, as well as from peripheral hypoglossal nerve lesions, which would lack contralateral sensory and motor deficits. Prompt management involves acute stroke protocols, vascular risk factor control, and rehabilitation to address motor and sensory deficits.
bottom_line: Choice A and B: (Inferior cerebellar peduncle): Lateral medulla - involved in Wallenberg syndrome, hoarseness, dysphagia, and sometimes Horner syndrome (ptosis and small pupil). Just remember: if you see hoarseness or dysphagia, pick the lateral medulla on the same side as the cranial nerve findings. This is most often due to a problem with PICA (posterior inferior cerebellar artery). If they don’t list PICA, you can choose vertebral artery. Choice C and D: (Medial longitudinal fasciculus): Causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia (eye movement problems), not weakness or tongue deviation. Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) is caused by a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), which connects the abducens nucleus (CN VI) of one side to the oculomotor nucleus (CN III) of the opposite side. The result is impaired adduction of the eye on the side of the lesion during horizontal gaze, while the abducting eye shows nystagmus. For example, a right MLF lesion means the right eye can't adduct when looking left. The most common cause in young adults is multiple sclerosis (MS) due to demyelination. In older adults, stroke (brainstem ischemia) is a common cause.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of sudden-onset facial asymmetry. He reports that food frequently collects between his teeth and right cheek, and he has been unintentionally biting the inside of his right lip. He also notes difficulty keeping liquids from dribbling out of the right corner of his mouth when drinking. There is no history of head trauma, skin rash, ear pain, or recent upper respiratory infection. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, there is flattening of the right nasolabial fold, drooping of the right corner of the mouth, and incomplete closure of the right eye with attempted blinking. The forehead on the right also shows absent wrinkling with eyebrow elevation. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact over the entire face, and hearing is grossly normal. Cranial nerve examination is otherwise normal. Which of the following additional findings is most likely to be present in this patient?

options.C: Loss of gag reflex on the affected side
options.D: Loss of taste sensation on the posterior third of the tongue
options.E: Ptosis with impaired pupillary constriction on the affected side

explanation: This patient has unilateral lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) palsy, evidenced by weakness involving both the upper and lower face on one side, including inability to wrinkle the forehead and close the eye, flattening of the nasolabial fold, and drooping of the mouth. The LMN lesion affects the facial nerve nucleus or nerve after it exits the brainstem, disrupting innervation to the muscles of facial expression, stapedius muscle, posterior belly of the digastric muscle, and stylohyoid muscle. Damage to the stapedius muscle results in hyperacusis (increased sensitivity to sound) on the affected side, due to loss of the muscle’s ability to dampen excessive oscillation of the stapes in the oval window. This is a well-recognized associated finding in Bell’s palsy, an idiopathic LMN facial nerve palsy. The facial nerve has both motor and sensory components. Motor fibers control muscles of facial expression and stapedius function, while parasympathetic fibers provide innervation to the lacrimal, submandibular, and sublingual glands. Special sensory fibers convey taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the chorda tympani branch, and general sensory fibers supply part of the external ear. LMN lesions (as in Bell’s palsy) present with complete ipsilateral facial weakness, including both upper and lower face. In contrast, upper motor neuron (UMN) lesions (eg, due to stroke involving the corticobulbar tract) spare the forehead because the upper facial muscles receive bilateral cortical innervation.
bottom_line: This patient has unilateral lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) palsy, evidenced by weakness involving both the upper and lower face on one side, including inability to wrinkle the forehead and close the eye, flattening of the nasolabial fold, and drooping of the mouth.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 67-year-old man with a history of colon cancer presents to the emergency department with worsening difficulty walking, progressive leg weakness, and a “tight belt” sensation around his abdomen. He reports that over the past several days, he has experienced numbness beginning around his belly button, which has now spread downward to both legs. He also notes new-onset urinary hesitancy and a feeling of incomplete bladder emptying. His bowel movements have become less frequent, and he describes a dull, aching mid-to-lower back pain that is worse when lying flat. On neurologic examination, his upper extremity function is intact. However, he exhibits bilateral lower extremity weakness, hyperreflexia, and sustained ankle clonus. Babinski signs are present bilaterally. Sensory testing reveals diminished pinprick and temperature sensation beginning at the level of the umbilicus and extending caudally. Light touch, proprioception, and vibration are preserved. Rectal tone is reduced. An urgent MRI of the spine reveals a compressive epidural mass impinging on the spinal cord at a thoracic level. Which of the following spinal cord levels is most likely affected?
explanation: The T10 spinal level corresponds to the umbilicus, making it the most likely level involved in a patient reporting sensory loss that begins at the navel. This patient’s findings-including lower extremity weakness, hyperreflexia, Babinski signs, and a defined sensory level at the umbilicus-are classic for thoracic spinal cord compression with upper motor neuron involvement. In patients with known malignancy, such as colon cancer, epidural metastasis is a common cause of spinal cord compression and most frequently affects the thoracic spine. The presence of urinary hesitancy and decreased rectal tone suggests autonomic involvement, which is common as spinal cord compression worsens. The patient’s “belt-like” sensation is another classic description of a sensory level. Early identification of the sensory dermatome involved-here, T10 for the umbilicus-is critical for rapid localization and appropriate imaging. MRI is the diagnostic modality of choice, and high-dose corticosteroids should be started immediately to reduce inflammation and prevent further neurologic damage. Depending on tumor sensitivity and stability, surgical decompression and/or radiation may be indicated. Spinal cord compression is a neurologic emergency, and prompt recognition of dermatomal sensory loss can make a major difference in outcome, particularly when paired with a history of known malignancy.
bottom_line: The T10 spinal level corresponds to the umbilicus, making it the most likely level involved in a patient reporting sensory loss that begins at the navel.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted to the ICU for altered mental status and severe hyponatremia (serum Na⁺ = 112 mEq/L). He is treated with IV hypertonic saline. Over the next 48 hours, his sodium level rises to 137 mEq/L. On day 3, he becomes acutely unresponsive. Neurologic exam reveals complete quadriplegia, but he can open his eyes and blink to command. He cannot speak, move his face, or breathe spontaneously. MRI shows symmetric hyperintensity in the central pons, sparing the tegmentum. Which of the following structures is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient has developed central pontine myelinolysis (CPM), now more broadly categorized as osmotic demyelination syndrome, following rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia. The hallmark is selective demyelination of the central pons, particularly the basis pontis, which contains descending corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts. Destruction of these motor tracts leads to locked-in syndrome: patients are awake and aware, with preserved vertical eye movements and blinking, but are completely paralyzed and unable to speak (anarthria). MRI reveals T2 hyperintensity in the central pons, sparing the tegmentum and other dorsal structures that control consciousness and eye movement, which explains why these patients remain alert and can blink. This condition mimics basilar artery stroke, but the cause here is metabolic rather than vascular. The key association for Step 1 is overcorrection of hyponatremia, especially when sodium rises >8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours. T10 innervates the umbilical dermatome, and sensory loss starting at the umbilicus suggests thoracic spinal cord compression at T10. This is a medical emergency in patients with metastatic cancer and warrants urgent MRI and corticosteroids to prevent irreversible damage. Anatomy
bottom_line: This patient has developed central pontine myelinolysis (CPM), now more broadly categorized as osmotic demyelination syndrome, following rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 72-year-old man is brought to the hospital because of sudden weakness. On exam, he has weakness of his right arm and leg, decreased vibration and position sense on the right side, and his tongue deviates to the left when protruded. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?
explanation: This patient has medial medullary syndrome. You know this because of three key findings: weakness on one side of the body, loss of vibration and position sense on the same side, and tongue deviation that points to the side of the lesion. In this case, his tongue deviates to the left, so the lesion is on the left medial medulla. This is usually due to occlusion of the anterior spinal artery (ASA). Just remember: tongue deviation = medial medulla; it points toward the side of the lesion.
bottom_line: This patient has medial medullary syndrome.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after his wife noticed that he is unable to swallow liquids without choking and that his voice has become hoarse. On examination, he has a diminished gag reflex and no cough reflex when the posterior pharyngeal wall is stimulated. Neurologic examination reveals normal motor strength and intact sensation in the upper and lower extremities. His uvula deviates to the left upon phonation. Imaging reveals a lesion affecting the cranial nerves responsible for these findings. The sensory afferents responsible for these symptoms pass through which of the following foramina?
explanation: The jugular foramen transmits cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal), X (vagus), and XI (accessory), as well as the internal jugular vein, making it a key structure for multiple vital neurologic and vascular pathways. Cranial nerve IX (glossopharyngeal) provides sensory innervation to the posterior third of the tongue and the oropharynx, including the afferent limbs of both the gag and cough reflexes, and also contributes parasympathetic fibers to the parotid gland. Cranial nerve X (vagus) supplies motor fibers to the muscles of the soft palate, pharynx, and larynx, which are essential for phonation, swallowing, and the efferent limb of the gag and cough reflexes, and it also mediates autonomic innervation to thoracic and abdominal viscera. The accessory nerve (XI) innervates the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, supporting head rotation and shoulder elevation. Damage to the structures traversing the jugular foramen produces jugular foramen (Vernet) syndrome, a constellation of deficits including dysphagia due to pharyngeal muscle paralysis, hoarseness from vocal cord paralysis, diminished gag and cough reflexes, and possible ipsilateral shoulder weakness from trapezius and sternocleidomastoid involvement. Uvular deviation away from the lesion side reflects impaired vagus innervation to the ipsilateral palatal muscles, resulting in unopposed contralateral contraction. The combination of impaired swallowing, hoarseness, loss of gag and cough reflexes, and uvular deviation strongly localizes the lesion to the jugular foramen, distinguishing it from lesions involving other cranial nerve exit foramina. Anatomy
bottom_line: The jugular foramen transmits cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal), X (vagus), and XI (accessory), as well as the internal jugular vein, making it a key structure for multiple vital neurologic and vascular pathways.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 33-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right foot weakness and pain after sustaining a deep laceration to the popliteal fossa during a construction site accident. He is unable to stand on his toes or push off while walking. On examination, there is weakness in plantarflexion and inversion of the right foot, and decreased flexion of the toes. Sensation is diminished over the sole of the foot. Dorsiflexion, toe extension, and eversion are intact. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: The tibial nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve, runs through the popliteal fossa and down the posterior leg, innervating the posterior compartment muscles responsible for plantarflexion, inversion, and toe flexion. It also provides sensory innervation to the sole of the foot. This patient’s inability to plantarflex (can’t stand on tippytoes) or invert the foot, along with sensory loss over the sole, is characteristic of tibial nerve injury, especially following trauma to the popliteal fossa. Because dorsiflexion, toe extension, and eversion are preserved (functions of the peroneal nerve branches), the lesion is localized to the tibial nerve rather than a more proximal sciatic lesion. Reflexes are intact, suggesting the lesion is distal to the spinal nerve roots but proximal to the ankle (as in tarsal tunnel syndrome).
bottom_line: The tibial nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve, runs through the popliteal fossa and down the posterior leg, innervating the posterior compartment muscles responsible for plantarflexion, inversion, and toe flexion.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with progressive numbness and tingling in her right hand for the past three months. She works as a graphic designer and spends several hours daily at a keyboard. She describes intermittent burning and paresthesias that affect the thumb, index, middle, and half of the ring finger on the right hand. The symptoms are worse at night and occasionally wake her from sleep. She also reports dropping objects more frequently over the last few weeks. She denies neck pain, trauma, or weakness in the other limb. On physical exam, sensation is decreased over the palmar surface of the thumb and index finger, with mild thenar muscle flattening. Thumb abduction and opposition are weak. Tinel’s and Phalen’s signs are not elicited. The rest of the neurologic exam is normal. Based on the diagram provided, which of the labeled nerves (A-E) is most likely involved in this patient's condition?
explanation: This patient presents with a classic presentation of median nerve entrapment, also known as carpal tunnel syndrome, which results from compression of the median nerve within the carpal tunnel at the wrist. The median nerve provides motor innervation to the thenar muscles, which control thumb abduction and opposition, and sensory innervation to the palmar surfaces of the lateral 3½ fingers-the thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger. The symptoms of numbness and tingling in the lateral hand, especially worsening at night and during repetitive hand use (e.g., typing), are typical of carpal tunnel syndrome. Additionally, this patient demonstrates early thenar muscle atrophy and impaired thumb opposition, which further supports median nerve involvement. The presence of symptoms despite negative Tinel’s and Phalen’s signs is possible and underscores why electrophysiological testing (nerve conduction studies and electromyography) is often the next best diagnostic step on USMLE. The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve is a commonly tested detail-it branches off before the carpal tunnel and provides sensation to the thenar region. Its sparing helps distinguish median nerve entrapment from more proximal lesions. In the image, label D correctly identifies the median nerve, which courses from the lateral and medial cords of the brachial plexus and travels down the anterior forearm before passing through the carpal tunnel into the hand.
bottom_line: This patient presents with a classic presentation of median nerve entrapment, also known as carpal tunnel syndrome, which results from compression of the median nerve within the carpal tunnel at the wrist.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 34-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic due to difficulty walking after slipping and falling down a set of concrete steps two days ago. He landed on his right foot with his ankle twisted inward and has since had trouble “pushing off the ground” with that foot. He also says he feels unsteady when walking and can't “get up on his toes” on the right side. He denies numbness, back pain, or weakness in the thigh or knee. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, the patient is unable to plantarflex the right foot against resistance and struggles to rise onto the toes of that foot while standing. Inversion of the foot is also weak, but dorsiflexion and eversion are preserved. Passive ankle range of motion is intact and non-tender. Strength testing in the proximal lower extremities is normal. The remainder of the neurologic exam, including reflexes and gait, is unremarkable. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient presents with loss of plantarflexion and inversion of the foot following an ankle injury, while dorsiflexion and eversion remain intact. This pattern of deficits strongly points toward injury to the tibial nerve, which innervates the posterior compartment of the leg, including the gastrocnemius, soleus, tibialis posterior, and flexor digitorum longus muscles. These muscles are responsible for plantarflexion (pushing off the foot) and inversion, both of which are impaired here. The tibial nerve is a branch of the sciatic nerve and courses posteriorly through the leg, making it vulnerable to injury at the posterior ankle or tarsal tunnel. The inability to stand on the toes of the affected foot is a classic sign of tibial nerve dysfunction. Since dorsiflexion (deep peroneal) and eversion (superficial peroneal) are intact, the injury must spare the anterior and lateral compartments of the leg, localizing the lesion to the tibial distribution. On USMLE, tibial nerve is the answer if the patient loses plantarflexion of the foot. Furthermore, the absence of thigh weakness or hip adduction problems effectively rules out more proximal nerve injuries such as the femoral or obturator nerves. The tibial nerve is the only option that accounts for both the motor pattern and the clinical context, including the mechanism of injury.
bottom_line: This patient presents with loss of plantarflexion and inversion of the foot following an ankle injury, while dorsiflexion and eversion remain intact.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with progressive weakness and difficulty walking. Over the past three days, he has developed tingling in his feet, which has progressed to bilateral lower extremity weakness. He reports mild shortness of breath and says his hands now feel weak as well. He was recently treated for a respiratory infection. He has a 30- pack-year smoking history but no known chronic illnesses. Vital signs show a blood pressure of 122/78 mm Hg, heart rate of 96/min, respiratory rate of 20/min, and oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Neurologic examination reveals diminished reflexes in all extremities and reduced strength in the lower limbs. There is no muscle tenderness or fasciculations. The remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute immune-mediated polyneuropathy that typically presents with ascending weakness, paresthesias, and areflexia following a preceding infection. This patient’s history of recent respiratory illness, progressive weakness, and reduced deep tendon reflexes supports the diagnosis. GBS frequently begins with distal paresthesias followed by ascending, symmetric weakness and hyporeflexia or areflexia. Respiratory muscle involvement can cause shortness of breath and necessitate ventilatory support. Diagnosis is clinical but can be supported by nerve conduction studies or lumbar puncture, which often shows albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein with normal cell count). Early recognition is critical, and patients should be admitted for monitoring of respiratory status and autonomic dysfunction. Treatment typically includes intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasma exchange. GBS results from autoimmune damage to peripheral nerves and nerve roots, most commonly triggered by infections like Campylobacter jejuni or other respiratory or gastrointestinal pathogens. The demyelination seen in GBS slows nerve conduction, which can be confirmed by electrophysiologic studies.
bottom_line: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute immune-mediated polyneuropathy that typically presents with ascending weakness, paresthesias, and areflexia following a preceding infection.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic with a 9-month history of progressive weakness and muscle cramps. He first noticed difficulty playing his weekly tennis matches due to loss of power in his right arm, which has gradually worsened. Over the last several months, he has also developed twitching in his shoulder muscles and a tendency to drop objects. His wife reports that his speech has occasionally become slurred, particularly after long conversations, and that he sometimes coughs while eating or drinking. He denies numbness, tingling, visual changes, or changes in bladder or bowel habits. The patient has no history of recent infections, toxin exposures, or new medications. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit 10 years ago. His father died of an unknown neurological illness in his 60s. On examination, the patient is alert and oriented. There is visible fasciculation of the right biceps and deltoid muscles, along with mild atrophy of the thenar eminence bilaterally. Strength testing reveals 4/5 power in right upper and lower extremities, with preserved strength on the left. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk throughout, with bilateral Babinski signs. Sensory examination is normal, including proprioception, vibration, and pain sensation. Gait is spastic but steady. Cranial nerve testing shows mild dysarthria but no facial weakness. There is no evidence of ophthalmoplegia or fatigability with repeated muscle use. MRI of the brain is unremarkable. Routine laboratory testing, including vitamin B12 levels, is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that involves both upper motor neurons (UMNs) and lower motor neurons (LMNs). UMN involvement produces spasticity, hyperreflexia, and pathologic reflexes (eg, Babinski sign), while LMN involvement leads to fasciculations, muscle atrophy, and weakness. The combination of these findings in the absence of sensory loss is characteristic and helps distinguish ALS from other neurologic conditions. This patient’s progressive asymmetric limb weakness, fasciculations, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, and normal sensory examination are classic features. The disease may also involve bulbar muscles, leading to dysarthria and dysphagia, as seen in this case. The pathogenesis of ALS is thought to involve glutamate-mediated excitotoxicity, oxidative stress, and abnormal protein aggregation, leading to degeneration of motor neurons in the spinal cord, brainstem, and motor cortex. Most cases are sporadic, but approximately 10% are familial, often involving mutations in superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) or other genes related to RNA processing or cytoskeletal integrity. There is no curative therapy, but riluzole, a glutamate release inhibitor, and edaravone, a free radical scavenger, may modestly slow progression. Supportive measures such as noninvasive ventilation, speech therapy, and gastrostomy feeding can improve quality of life and survival. Prognosis is generally poor, with median survival of 3-5 years from symptom onset, primarily due to respiratory failure. Recognition of the hallmark combination of UMN and LMN signs without sensory deficits is critical for diagnosis. Electromyography and nerve conduction studies can confirm widespread denervation and reinnervation. Early diagnosis allows for timely initiation of supportive interventions, which can significantly impact patient comfort and function even if they do not alter the disease course.
bottom_line: ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that involves both upper motor neurons (UMNs) and lower motor neurons (LMNs).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of intermittent urine leakage. She says the leakage typically occurs when she washes her hands, when she hears water running, or occasionally when walking toward the bathroom. She has no dysuria, hematuria, flank pain, or fever. She reports that the volume of leakage is usually small but bothersome and sometimes prevents her from leaving the house for long periods. She has no history of pelvic surgery, trauma, or neurologic disease. Her bowel habits are normal, and she denies constipation. She drinks two cups of coffee daily but otherwise no caffeinated or alcoholic beverages. Pelvic examination shows normal external genitalia and no visible prolapse. Neurologic examination reveals normal lower extremity sensation, motor function, and reflexes. Postvoid residual volume is within the normal range. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s incontinence?
explanation: This patient’s symptoms-urine leakage triggered by sensory cues such as hearing running water and the urge to void immediately before reaching the toilet-are classic for urge incontinence, caused by involuntary detrusor contractions during the bladder filling phase. The normal pelvic and neurologic examination, absence of prolapse, and normal postvoid residual volume help exclude overflow and stress incontinence. The episodic, sudden nature of leakage indicates a functional control problem rather than a mechanical outflow issue. From a pathophysiologic standpoint, urge incontinence involves disruption of normal suprapontine and pontine inhibition of the sacral micturition reflex arc. Normally, bladder filling sends afferent signals via pelvic nerves (S2-S4) to the sacral spinal cord and onward to higher centers, which suppress detrusor contraction until voiding is socially appropriate. In detrusor overactivity, this inhibition is diminished, leading to premature activation of parasympathetic efferents that release acetylcholine onto M3 muscarinic receptors in detrusor smooth muscle, causing involuntary contraction and leakage. This overactivity can be idiopathic, or secondary to neurologic lesions (eg, multiple sclerosis, stroke, Parkinson disease) that impair inhibitory pathways. Repeated uninhibited contractions may also cause bladder remodeling, with increased afferent sensitivity to low volumes of urine and exaggerated reflex activity. In some patients, “conditioned reflexes” can form, in which sensory triggers such as running water or approaching the bathroom initiate involuntary detrusor activity. Risk factors include aging, estrogen deficiency, bladder outlet obstruction (post-decompression overactivity), and chronic bladder irritation (eg, stones, infection). Management targets the neural-muscular pathway by using behavioral interventions, pelvic floor therapy, and pharmacologic agents-most commonly antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, tolterodine) that block M3-mediated contraction, or beta-3 agonists (mirabegron) that promote detrusor relaxation.
