bottom_line: Radial nerve injury is the most common neurologic complication of midshaft humeral fractures, owing to the nerve's close anatomical relationship to the spiral groove along the posterior surface of the mid-humerus.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following histopathologic findings would most likely be seen in this patient’s disease?

options.C: Multiple lacunar infarcts of subcortical white matter

explanation: This patient presents with a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized primarily by personality change, apathy, and disinhibition rather than the early memory impairment seen in Alzheimer disease. His wife describes a decline in social appropriateness, personal hygiene, and emotional responsiveness, which are all hallmarks of a disorder affecting the frontal lobes. The Mini-Mental State Examination reveals prominent executive dysfunction, which also supports a frontally mediated dementia process. The normal neurologic exam further reinforces that this is not a vascular or motor neurodegenerative process, but rather a cortical degenerative syndrome. Frontotemporal dementia, also known as Pick disease, is the classic cause of this presentation. The disease involves accumulation of abnormal tau protein within neurons, but instead of forming neurofibrillary tangles (as in Alzheimer), it aggregates as Pick bodies. These inclusions are classically round, intracytoplasmic, and silver-staining. Their presence correlates with degeneration of the frontal and temporal lobes, producing the behavioral and executive dysfunction observed in this patient. The MRI provided shows atrophy of the frontal and anterior temporal lobes, consistent with the clinical syndrome. Unlike Alzheimer disease, which is dominated by episodic memory loss, Pick disease disproportionately affects judgment, social conduct, and motivation early in its course. The absence of motor deficits, hallucinations, or stepwise decline argues against Lewy body dementia or vascular dementia. Similarly, the lack of rapid progression or myoclonus makes prion disease unlikely. The chronic, insidious progression here is most compatible with a tauopathy localized to the frontal lobes, further supported by the radiographic evidence of frontal atrophy. Histopathology is critical for distinguishing dementias on USMLE exams. In Pick disease, the pathognomonic finding is round, silver-staining inclusions known as Pick bodies. These inclusions differentiate it from Lewy bodies (alpha-synuclein), Alzheimer pathology (beta-amyloid plaques and tangles), vascular lesions (lacunes), and prion disease (spongiform vacuolation).
bottom_line: This patient presents with a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized primarily by personality change, apathy, and disinhibition rather than the early memory impairment seen in Alzheimer disease.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following best describes the affected structure?
explanation: The posterior communicating artery is the classic location for aneurysmal compression of CN III (oculomotor nerve). This nerve controls most extraocular muscles, eyelid elevation (levator palpebrae), and parasympathetic innervation to the pupil. Aneurysmal compression affects the superficial parasympathetic fibers first, causing ipsilateral mydriasis (blown pupil) and loss of accommodation. As the lesion progresses, motor fibers are involved, resulting in ptosis and an eye that rests “down and out” due to unopposed action of the lateral rectus and superior oblique. ADPKD is a high-yield association for saccular aneurysms, including PCoM aneurysms.
bottom_line: The posterior communicating artery is the classic location for aneurysmal compression of CN III (oculomotor nerve).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department due to progressive weakness in his legs over the past 3 days. He says it started as tingling in his feet and now he has trouble rising from a chair and climbing stairs. He also notes new-onset difficulty gripping objects. Examination reveals symmetric weakness in the lower extremities, both proximally and distally, and mild weakness in the intrinsic hand muscles. Deep tendon reflexes are absent throughout. Sensation is intact. Cranial nerves are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute, immune-mediated demyelinating disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by T-cell and antibody-mediated destruction of Schwann cells. It typically presents with ascending symmetric weakness, loss of deep tendon reflexes, and often begins as tingling in the hands or feet. Weakness may involve proximal and distal muscles and may later affect the upper limbs or cranial nerves. Reflexes are absent throughout, a key feature. Rarely will USMLE give you the buzzy description of food poisoning/Campylobacter jejuni symptoms preceding, so don't use this as a crutch. Treatment is IVIG + plasmapheresis. Steroids are the wrong answer.
bottom_line: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute, immune-mediated demyelinating disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by T-cell and antibody-mediated destruction of Schwann cells.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He was the restrained driver and sustained blunt trauma to the neck and chest. He is currently alert but complains of shortness of breath, especially when lying flat. Vitals are stable. Chest x-ray reveals an elevated right hemidiaphragm. Neurologic exam shows no limb weakness or sensory deficits. He is able to walk unassisted but reports mild tingling over his right shoulder. There is no paradoxical chest wall motion or signs of pneumothorax. His oxygen saturation decreases when placed in a supine position. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s respiratory findings?

options.C: C3-C5 nerve root injury affecting the phrenic nerve

answer_text: C3-C5 nerve root injury affecting the phrenic nerve
explanation: The phrenic nerve arises from the C3-C5 nerve roots and provides motor innervation to the diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration. In this patient with neck trauma, the presence of orthopnea (difficulty breathing when supine) and elevated hemidiaphragm on chest X-ray strongly suggests phrenic nerve palsy. The phrenic nerve also carries sensory fibers to the diaphragmatic pleura and pericardium, explaining the referred shoulder tingling (via C5 dermatomal overlap). Since respiration is mostly diaphragm-driven, a unilateral lesion is often asymptomatic at rest but causes positional dyspnea, worsened when lying flat due to loss of gravity-assisted abdominal content displacement. Importantly, there are no limb motor or sensory deficits, helping localize the lesion specifically to the phrenic nerve or its roots, sparing the rest of the brachial plexus. In trauma, this can occur due to traction or compression at the cervical roots (especially C5), without broader spinal cord or plexus involvement. Recognition is crucial because bilateral phrenic nerve injury can cause severe respiratory compromise, especially in ventilated or surgical patients.
bottom_line: The phrenic nerve arises from the C3-C5 nerve roots and provides motor innervation to the diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 40-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive numbness and weakness in his right hand for the past month. He works as a delivery driver and spends several hours daily gripping a steering wheel. He reports tingling and decreased sensation in his right pinky and the adjacent side of the ring finger. Over the past week, he’s had difficulty opening jars and performing fine motor tasks like buttoning his shirt. He denies neck pain, trauma, or any symptoms in the opposite limb. On exam, there is mild atrophy of the hypothenar eminence and decreased sensation over the medial aspect of the hand. Strength testing reveals impaired finger abduction and adduction. The patient is asked to hold a piece of paper between the thumb and index finger; he compensates by flexing the thumb at the interphalangeal joint. The rest of the neurologic exam is normal. Based on the diagram provided, which of the labeled nerves (A-E) is most likely affected?
explanation: This patient presents with signs and symptoms characteristic of ulnar nerve compression, specifically involving motor and sensory deficits in the ulnar distribution of the hand. The ulnar nerve innervates the medial 1½ digits (the pinky and half of the ring finger) and controls finger abduction and adduction via the interosseous muscles. The patient’s impaired fine motor function, hypothenar atrophy, and weakness of interosseous muscles are all consistent with ulnar nerve dysfunction. A key physical exam finding in this case is the positive Froment sign-inability to adduct the thumb without compensatory flexion at the interphalangeal joint. This occurs because the adductor pollicis, innervated by the ulnar nerve, is weak, and the flexor pollicis longus (median nerve) takes over. This subtle yet classic sign is heavily emphasized on USMLE and reflects high-yield understanding of ulnar nerve function. The likely cause in this vignette is cubital tunnel syndrome, a proximal ulnar nerve entrapment at the medial elbow, leading to combined forearm and hand symptoms. This is the "ulnar-side equivalent of carpal tunnel syndrome" and frequently overlooked by students. Treatment for cubital tunnel syndrome is typically overnight elbow splinting, and surgery is not the preferred initial step. In the image, label E corresponds to the ulnar nerve, which branches from the medial cord and courses down the medial arm to the elbow and wrist.
bottom_line: This patient presents with signs and symptoms characteristic of ulnar nerve compression, specifically involving motor and sensory deficits in the ulnar distribution of the hand.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being tackled during a recreational soccer match. He says that another player collided with his right leg while he was mid-stride. He immediately felt pain along the outer aspect of his knee and had difficulty walking afterward. He denies head trauma, back pain, or numbness in the contralateral limb. He has no prior surgeries or chronic medical conditions. He did not lose consciousness and denies bowel or bladder dysfunction. On physical examination, there is mild swelling and tenderness around the lateral aspect of the right knee, just distal to the joint line. He is unable to dorsiflex or evert the right foot against resistance. Plantarflexion and inversion are preserved. When attempting to walk, he lifts his right knee higher than usual to avoid dragging the foot. Sensation is reduced over the upper lateral leg, while sensation elsewhere in the lower extremity remains intact. Reflexes are symmetric. The remainder of the neurologic and musculoskeletal exam is normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This patient presents after a lateral knee trauma and has a neurological deficit characterized by loss of both dorsiflexion and eversion, with preserved plantarflexion and inversion. He also has sensory loss over the upper lateral leg. These findings are most consistent with an injury to the common peroneal nerve, which winds around the fibular neck just below the lateral aspect of the knee and is highly vulnerable to trauma in this location. The common peroneal nerve branches into the superficial peroneal nerve, which provides eversion and sensation to the dorsum of the foot, and the deep peroneal nerve, which provides dorsiflexion and sensation to the first web space. Injury to the common peroneal nerve results in foot drop, as both of its terminal branches are affected. On USMLE, common peroneal nerve is the answer if the patient loses both eversion and dorsiflexion of the foot. Patients often develop a high-stepping gait to compensate for foot dragging - a classic finding in common peroneal nerve injury. The presence of motor loss in two distinct compartments (anterior and lateral leg), combined with sensory loss localized to the upper lateral leg, confirms that the lesion is proximal, affecting the common trunk before it divides. This also explains the absence of inversion/plantarflexion deficits, which are mediated by the tibial nerve and remain intact here.
bottom_line: This patient presents after a lateral knee trauma and has a neurological deficit characterized by loss of both dorsiflexion and eversion, with preserved plantarflexion and inversion.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic due to difficulty walking that began three days ago. He reports that his right knee has been giving out unexpectedly while walking, especially when going down stairs. He has nearly fallen several times but catches himself by holding on to railings. He denies trauma, pain, or joint swelling. He also has not noticed numbness, tingling, or weakness in the lower leg or foot. He does not take any medications and has no known history of diabetes, spinal disease, or prior surgeries. On physical examination, the patient has reduced strength in right knee extension, with difficulty resisting pressure when attempting to straighten the knee against resistance. Hip flexion is mildly reduced on the same side. Strength in the contralateral leg and in the lower portions of both legs (including foot dorsiflexion and plantarflexion) is normal. Reflexes are symmetric and sensation is intact. Gait is cautious, and he favors the right leg. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient presents with difficulty extending the knee and episodes of buckling, both of which point toward quadriceps weakness. The femoral nerve innervates the quadriceps femoris group, which is primarily responsible for knee extension. Weakness in this muscle group makes the leg unstable during stance phase and can cause sudden knee collapse or giving way, as seen here. While the patient has preserved lower leg and foot strength, the isolated weakness in knee extension and hip flexion suggests femoral nerve involvement. The femoral nerve arises from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and passes beneath the inguinal ligament into the anterior thigh, making it vulnerable to injury due to retroperitoneal hemorrhage, pelvic trauma, or iatrogenic causes such as catheterization or surgery. The involvement of hip flexion in this case is consistent with iliopsoas weakness, which also receives input from the femoral nerve, strengthening the localization. The absence of foot involvement rules out more distal lesions. Although the femoral nerve also provides sensation to the anterior thigh and medial leg, sensory findings are not emphasized in USMLE questions involving femoral neuropathy. Most presentations focus on motor impairment, particularly quadriceps weakness, because it has a more functionally obvious and disabling impact on gait and balance.
bottom_line: This patient presents with difficulty extending the knee and episodes of buckling, both of which point toward quadriceps weakness.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 38-year-old woman comes to the physician for a 1-month history of 4 episodes of stabbing pain behind her right eye, with each episode lasting only approximately 20 seconds. There is no specific cause that she can pinpoint and the episodes have been extremely distressful. She says the episodes occurred at different times during the day and the last episode was 2 days ago. She has no past medical history and her only medication is a combined oral contraceptive pill, which she has been taking for approximately 20 years. She does not drink or smoke. Family history is unremarkable. Physical and neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. Vitals are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia (TN) is the answer on USMLE for 11/10 lancinating/stabbing pain behind the eye (V1; ophthalmic distribution), along the cheek (V2; maxillary distribution), or along the jaw (V3; mandibular distribution) that lasts up to 60 seconds, and is classically brought on by a minor stimulus, such as gust of wind, brushing one's hair, or brushing one's teeth. These episodes can occur at pretty much any point during the day. This contrasts with cluster headache, which tends to occur while the patient is lying in bed. The mechanism for TN is thought to be due to pressure/compression of the trigeminal branches at their exit points from the skull. The mnemonic for remembering the exit points is Standing Room Only → V1 (Superior orbital fissure), V2 (foramen Rotundum), V3 (foramen Ovale). The USMLE can also have "pons" as the answer if they force you to choose a brain location, since the trigeminal nerve branches from the pons, as sometimes irritation of the nerve occurs more centrally. Prophylaxis is carbamazepine, but there is no real abortive treatment, since episodes are so fast anyway. Anatomy
bottom_line: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: High Yield USMLE Audio Answer:

subject: Neuro
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with brief episodes of severe facial discomfort that have been occurring for the past month. She describes the pain as sudden, sharp, and electric in quality, lasting only a few seconds at a time, but recurring multiple times throughout the day. The episodes are often triggered by chewing or light touch to certain areas of her cheek. She denies fever, vision changes, limb weakness, or sensory loss between episodes. Neurologic examination between attacks is normal, with symmetric facial movement, intact corneal reflexes, and preserved sensation. Her past medical history includes controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. An MRI of the brain shows no mass lesion or demyelinating plaques. Which of the following best explains this patient’s condition?

options.A: Axonal demyelination of facial motor fibers
options.B: Compression of the geniculate ganglion by tumor
options.C: Microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve
options.D: Small-vessel ischemia in the brainstem sensory nuclei
options.E: Viral reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion

answer_text: Microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve
explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia is a neuropathic pain syndrome involving the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) that manifests as recurrent, unilateral episodes of intense, electric shock-like facial pain. The episodes are abrupt in onset, last seconds to a couple of minutes, and may occur in rapid succession over hours. In this patient, the pain is triggered by light tactile stimulation of the face while chewing, with completely normal neurologic function between attacks. These features strongly suggest a paroxysmal cranial nerve disorder rather than a continuous pain syndrome. The most common underlying cause is chronic microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root at its entry into the pons, most often by an aberrant loop of the superior cerebellar artery. The pathophysiology involves constant pulsatile contact between the arterial loop and the nerve root entry zone, which is located at the junction between central myelin (produced by oligodendrocytes) and peripheral myelin (produced by Schwann cells). This area is highly susceptible to mechanical injury. Over time, vascular compression causes focal demyelination of large myelinated sensory fibers, leading to reduced electrical insulation and aberrant ephaptic transmission-where action potentials from one axon directly depolarize neighboring axons. This cross-talk amplifies sensory input, lowers the threshold for firing, and results in the sudden, severe pain characteristic of the condition.
bottom_line: Trigeminal neuralgia is a neuropathic pain syndrome involving the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) that manifests as recurrent, unilateral episodes of intense, electric shock-like facial pain.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to recurrent facial pain that began two months ago. She says the pain comes in sudden bursts several times a day and feels like a “stab” or “jolt” deep in her right cheek and jaw. She finds it hard to predict when it will happen, but notices that talking or chewing often brings it on. The pain does not radiate, is not associated with headache, nausea, or visual symptoms, and lasts only a few seconds at a time. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical and neurologic examinations are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia presents with classic features of sudden, severe, electric shock-like or knife-like pain in the distribution of the trigeminal nerve (CN V). The pain is typically described as lancinating, knife-like, and lasts less than 1 minute per episode. This woman’s recurrent, stabbing pain in the right cheek and jaw corresponds to the V2 (maxillary) and V3 (mandibular) branches of CN V. A key diagnostic clue is that her pain is triggered by minor stimuli, such as chewing or talking - this is a hallmark of trigeminal neuralgia, where even light tactile stimulation (e.g., brushing teeth, gusts of wind) can precipitate an episode. The pain is strictly unilateral, localized, and not associated with systemic symptoms or headache features, which helps exclude other diagnoses like migraine or temporal arteritis. Pathophysiologically, trigeminal neuralgia is thought to result from compression or irritation of the trigeminal nerve root at its entry zone into the brainstem. The most common site of involvement is at the pons, where the trigeminal nerve emerges - this is a high-yield anatomical fact tested in USMLE. Treatment of choice is carbamazepine, which is used as prophylaxis to reduce frequency and severity of attacks. Carbamazepine works by stabilizing inactivated sodium channels in hyperexcitable neurons, thereby reducing ectopic nerve firing. Notably, there is no effective acute therapy since the attacks are extremely brief and resolve spontaneously.
bottom_line: Trigeminal neuralgia presents with classic features of sudden, severe, electric shock-like or knife-like pain in the distribution of the trigeminal nerve (CN V).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 27-year-old woman comes to the neurology clinic due to intermittent visual disturbances and difficulty focusing when shifting her gaze. She reports that, over the past few weeks, she has developed double vision when looking to the left side. The symptom is more noticeable when she tries to read or watch television. She denies headache, vertigo, photophobia, or hearing loss. She has no history of trauma or recent infection. Her medical history is significant for episodic limb numbness and tingling over the past year, which spontaneously resolved each time. She does not take any regular medications and has no known allergies. Her family history is unremarkable. On neurologic examination, when the patient is asked to look to the left, her left eye abducts normally with a conjugate saccade, but the right eye fails to adduct. When looking straight ahead or to the right, both eyes move symmetrically. Pupillary responses are normal bilaterally, and there is no ptosis or nystagmus in the resting gaze. MRI of the brain is scheduled to further evaluate the cause of her symptoms. Damage to which of the following structures is most likely responsible for this patient’s condition?
explanation: This patient presents with impaired adduction of the right eye during leftward gaze and preserved function during rightward gaze and midline. This pattern is characteristic of internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), which occurs due to damage of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) on the side of the adduction deficit - in this case, the right MLF. The MLF is a heavily myelinated tract in the brainstem that coordinates conjugate horizontal eye movements by linking the abducens nucleus (cranial nerve VI) of one side to the contralateral oculomotor nucleus (cranial nerve III). During horizontal gaze, the abducens nucleus activates the ipsilateral lateral rectus and sends a signal via the MLF to activate the contralateral medial rectus. Damage to the MLF disrupts this communication, resulting in failure of adduction in the eye ipsilateral to the lesion and nystagmus in the abducting eye due to unopposed activity and attempted compensation. Internuclear ophthalmoplegia is strongly associated with multiple sclerosis (MS), especially in young women. MS causes demyelination of central nervous system tracts, and the MLF is particularly vulnerable due to its dense myelin content and central location in the dorsal pons and midbrain. INO can be bilateral in MS, but unilateral cases are also common and diagnostically helpful. In contrast, unilateral INO in older adults may be due to brainstem stroke. This patient’s past episodes of transient neurological symptoms (limb paresthesias) and the subacute onset of ocular findings strongly point toward demyelinating disease rather than ischemia or trauma.
bottom_line: Choice A and C: The abducens nerve (cranial nerve VI) innervates the lateral rectus muscle, which abducts the ipsilateral eye. This is remembered with the mnemonic “LR6,” meaning the lateral rectus is supplied by cranial nerve VI. Meanwhile, the abducens nucleus controls not only the ipsilateral lateral rectus but also coordinates contralateral medial rectus function by projecting through the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) to the contralateral oculomotor nucleus. This pathway allows for conjugate horizontal gaze. A lesion in the abducens nerve would result in impaired abduction of the ipsilateral eye, leading to horizontal diplopia that worsens on gaze toward the affected side. On the other hand, a lesion in the abducens nucleus would produce a horizontal gaze palsy, in which both eyes fail to move toward the side of the lesion due to interruption of both direct and internuclear signaling. In this case, the patient has intact abduction of both eyes and preserved conjugate gaze to the right side, making both a right abducens nerve palsy and right abducens nucleus lesion highly unlikely.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to a rehabilitation facility after being discharged from the hospital, where he had undergone surgical stabilization for a traumatic injury several weeks ago. He initially presented after a fall from a ladder, which resulted in multiple fractures and prolonged immobilization. He reports persistent weakness in both lower limbs but no new complaints today. On physical exam, he is alert and cooperative, with stable vital signs and no signs of systemic infection. Neurologic evaluation shows minimal voluntary movement in both legs with decreased tone. Deep tendon reflexes in the lower extremities are difficult to elicit. His upper extremity strength is preserved, and sensation is intact in the upper body. There is no evidence of acute thrombosis or signs of compartment syndrome. He has no prior history of neuromuscular disorders or systemic autoimmune disease. After one month of limited mobility, a follow-up evaluation is requested to assess the current state of the affected muscles. What is the most likely histologic change that would be observed in the patient’s affected muscles after one month?

options.C: Decreased blood supply to the muscles

answer_text: Decreased blood supply to the muscles
explanation: This patient is experiencing muscle changes following spinal cord injury with prolonged disuse of the lower limbs. Disuse atrophy begins within days of inactivity and is especially prominent after central nervous system injuries that limit voluntary movement. Although the neurons themselves may remain intact in such cases, the muscles they innervate undergo a progressive decline in function and structure due to lack of contractile activity. One of the key histologic findings after a month of disuse is a measurable reduction in blood supply to the muscle, caused by decreased capillary density and regression of microvasculature. This diminished perfusion reflects both decreased metabolic demand and mechanical unloading of the affected muscle groups. Muscle atrophy in disuse is driven primarily by suppressed anabolic signaling (notably via the IGF-1-Akt-mTOR pathway) and activation of catabolic processes such as the autophagy-lysosome pathway. Reduced calcium transients, downregulation of mitochondrial biogenesis regulators (like PGC-1α), and impaired insulin sensitivity further exacerbate the loss of muscle mass and endurance. These adaptations are compounded by reductions in local capillary density, impairing oxygen delivery and accelerating fatigue during any attempted recovery of function. Disuse atrophy also results in fiber-type switching, typically with a loss of oxidative type I (slow-twitch) fibers and relative increase in glycolytic type IIb (fast-twitch) fibers. This shift decreases fatigue resistance and further reduces capillary requirement, reinforcing vascular regression. In contrast to direct denervation injuries that result in rapid, angular fiber atrophy and structural collapse, disuse atrophy is more gradual and often reversible with early rehabilitation. However, prolonged disuse leads to irreversible fibrosis, fatty infiltration, and vascular rarefaction, ultimately limiting the capacity for functional restoration.
bottom_line: This patient is experiencing muscle changes following spinal cord injury with prolonged disuse of the lower limbs.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 55-year-old man comes to the clinic due to persistent discomfort in his right leg that began 2 weeks ago. He describes a burning sensation that starts in the front of his thigh and radiates down to the inner aspect of his calf. The pain worsens when walking or climbing stairs. He denies any bowel or bladder issues. On physical examination, he has diminished sensation to light touch along the anterior thigh and medial lower leg. His strength is slightly reduced during knee extension, and his right patellar reflex is decreased compared to the left. He is otherwise neurologically intact. Which of the following is the most likely affected nerve root?
explanation: This patient's presentation is consistent with L4 radiculopathy, a condition that often results from compression of the L4 nerve root, typically due to a herniated disc at the L3-L4 level. The hallmark features of L4 radiculopathy include diminished patellar reflex, weakness of knee extension, and sensory changes along the anterior thigh and medial leg, which align with this patient's symptoms. The quadriceps femoris muscle, innervated by L4 via the femoral nerve, is primarily responsible for knee extension. The reduced knee jerk reflex further supports involvement of the L4 root. While many radiculopathies share overlapping features, L4 involvement is specifically associated with both motor and sensory impairment in the distribution noted here. The absence of bowel or bladder involvement rules out central causes like cauda equina syndrome. Since this presentation is classic and localized, it is highly suggestive of L4 radiculopathy. Radiculopathies are diagnosed based on a combination of history, physical examination, and imaging if needed. Sensory deficits and reflex loss often help in localizing the affected nerve root. L4 is the only nerve root whose compression affects the patellar reflex, making this a high-yield clue on USMLE. The pain in the anterior thigh and medial leg follows the typical dermatomal map for L4. For USMLE, remember that L4 radiculopathy presents with diminished knee reflex and medial leg sensory loss, whereas L5 causes dorsiflexion weakness and S1 affects the Achilles reflex and plantar flexion.
bottom_line: This patient's presentation is consistent with L4 radiculopathy, a condition that often results from compression of the L4 nerve root, typically due to a herniated disc at the L3-L4 level.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 72-year-old man presents with severe, persistent burning pain and allodynia involving his entire left arm and leg that began several weeks ago. He reports no history of trauma or prior neuropathy. Four months prior, he suffered a cerebrovascular accident resulting in decreased sensation to light touch, vibration, pain, and temperature on the left side of his body. Neurological examination reveals normal muscle strength and deep tendon reflexes in all extremities. Sensory testing shows marked hypoesthesia to all modalities over the left side, while the right side is intact. There is no evidence of muscle atrophy, fasciculations, or abnormal movements. He now has difficulty sleeping due to the intensity of the pain and describes it as "burning and electric." Which of the following regions, of the central nervous system, when damaged, is most likely responsible for the development of his current symptoms?
explanation: Thalamic pain syndrome is a type of central post-stroke pain that occurs due to injury to the ventral posterolateral (VPL) or ventral posteromedial (VPM) nuclei of the thalamus-areas responsible for relaying somatosensory input to the cortex. It typically arises weeks to months after a stroke and is characterized by chronic, severe, and often burning or electric-like pain on the side of the body contralateral to the thalamic lesion. Patients often have allodynia (pain from non-painful stimuli) and hypoesthesia (reduced sensation), which can paradoxically coexist. Motor function is usually preserved, and reflexes remain intact, helping to distinguish it from other neurologic syndromes. This condition reflects disrupted central processing of sensory input and can be difficult to treat, often requiring medications like gabapentin, amitriptyline, or other neuropathic pain agents.
bottom_line: Thalamic pain syndrome is a type of central post-stroke pain that occurs due to injury to the ventral posterolateral (VPL) or ventral posteromedial (VPM) nuclei of the thalamus-areas responsible for relaying somatosensory input to the cortex.

subject: Neuro
stem: Neurosurgery is consulted for evaluation. Over the next 48 hours, the patient remains comatose despite normalization of vitals, normal intracranial pressure, and absence of sedation. EEG shows preserved background activity but no signs of seizure. MRI is pending. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Basilar skull fracture with brainstem herniation

explanation: This patient sustained a high-speed deceleration injury, a classic mechanism for diffuse axonal injury (DAI), which is caused by rotational shearing forces that disrupt axonal connections at vulnerable junctions between tissues of different densities-such as the gray-white matter interface, corpus callosum, and brainstem. Clinically, DAI is characterized by persistent coma or impaired consciousness immediately following trauma, despite no evidence of mass effect, herniation, or significant hemorrhage on CT. The CT findings here are critical and diagnostic. The scan shows multiple small punctate hyperdensities, most prominently at the gray-white junction and deep structures, which represent small hemorrhagic foci caused by axonal shearing. While MRI is more sensitive and can show hyperintense T2 lesions, early CT may still reveal these subtle findings in severe DAI cases. The absence of midline shift, mass lesion, or extra-axial bleed further supports the diagnosis. The clinical scenario also aligns: a young individual in a high-impact MVA, low GCS, and prolonged unresponsiveness out of proportion to imaging findings. The persistence of coma without elevated ICP or sedative influence suggests primary axonal injury rather than secondary complications like herniation or hemorrhage. EEG findings of preserved background activity further support a non-epileptic encephalopathy, which is characteristic of DAI. Management is supportive, and prognosis is guarded depending on the severity of injury and degree of brainstem involvement. Pathophysiology
bottom_line: Epidural hematoma.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 29-year-old man presents to the emergency department for evaluation of recurrent, severe pain localized deep behind his right eye. He describes the pain as sharp and excruciating, coming in discrete episodes that last around 20 minutes and occur multiple times daily, often waking him from sleep in the middle of the night. He notes that during these episodes, he feels restless and often walks around his apartment to cope. He also reports ipsilateral tearing and mild nasal congestion. On examination, there is mild ptosis and a constricted pupil on the right, but no skin eruptions or rashes are seen. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. The remainder of the neurologic exam is normal. Which of the following best explains his clinical syndrome?
explanation: Cluster headache is characterized by recurrent, severe, unilateral periorbital pain with associated autonomic symptoms such as lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, ptosis, and miosis. Episodes typically last 15-180 minutes (most often 20-30 minutes) and occur in clusters, especially at night, often awakening the patient from sleep. The pathophysiology involves hypothalamic activation leading to trigeminal-autonomic reflex dysfunction and release of vasoactive peptides. First-line abortive therapy is 100% oxygen by non-rebreather mask; prophylaxis is typically with high-dose verapamil, a calcium channel blocker that prevents attack clustering. Cluster headaches occur more frequently in young to middle-aged men. They are distinguished from migraine by their shorter duration, lack of aura, and prominent ipsilateral autonomic features. The presence of ptosis and miosis is due to sympathetic dysfunction in the pericarotid plexus, but anhidrosis is less prominent. Key clinical pearls include association with restlessness (patients pace or rock), while migraineurs prefer to lie still.
bottom_line: Cluster headache is characterized by recurrent, severe, unilateral periorbital pain with associated autonomic symptoms such as lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, ptosis, and miosis.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following neurologic structures is most likely affected?
explanation: Supracondylar fractures of the humerus are the most common elbow fractures in children and typically occur from a fall on an outstretched hand. The mechanism causes hyperextension at the elbow and leads to a transverse fracture just proximal to the elbow joint. The median nerve is the most commonly injured nerve in this setting because it runs anteriorly through the antecubital fossa, close to the distal humerus and brachial artery. As the proximal fragment shifts posteriorly during hyperextension injuries, the median nerve can be stretched, contused, or entrapped. Classic signs of median nerve injury include weakness of thumb opposition and flexion of the first and second digits-explaining why this patient cannot make an “OK” sign. Sensory loss typically involves the lateral palm and palmar surfaces of the thumb, index, and middle fingers. In more severe cases, especially with a flexor digitorum profundus injury, you may also see a “hand of benediction” when the child attempts to make a fist. The anterior interosseous branch of the median nerve, a motor branch, may be selectively affected and can present with pure motor findings (e.g., loss of thumb IP flexion and index finger DIP flexion). Prompt diagnosis of a median nerve injury is critical for determining both the severity of the fracture and the need for surgical intervention. If there is neurovascular compromise, urgent reduction and orthopedic consultation are required. Even in nondisplaced fractures, serial neurovascular exams are essential to monitor for evolving injury due to swelling or compartment syndrome.
bottom_line: Supracondylar fractures of the humerus are the most common elbow fractures in children and typically occur from a fall on an outstretched hand.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being stabbed during an altercation. The knife entered the left side of his lower back. He is hemodynamically stable and alert. On physical exam, he has 0/5 strength in the left lower extremity and diminished proprioception and vibration sense on the same side below the umbilicus. Pain and temperature sensation are intact on the left side but absent on the right side beginning just below the umbilicus. Rectal tone is normal. Upper extremity strength and sensation are intact bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with the classic triad of Brown-Séquard syndrome, which results from hemisection of the spinal cord, typically due to penetrating trauma such as a knife or gunshot wound. The pattern includes: 1)Ipsilateral loss of motor function below the lesion due to corticospinal tract involvement. 2)Ipsilateral loss of vibration and proprioception, carried by the dorsal columns, which do not cross until the medulla. 3)Contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation, which reflects damage to the decussated spinothalamic fibers, which cross 1-2 levels above their entry. The patient has weakness and loss of vibration on the left, and loss of pain and temperature on the right, which is the classic pattern for Brown-Séquard syndrome. The symptoms start around the belly button, which points to a spinal cord injury near T10 on the left side.
bottom_line: This patient presents with the classic triad of Brown-Séquard syndrome, which results from hemisection of the spinal cord, typically due to penetrating trauma such as a knife or gunshot wound.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 36-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He is intubated for airway protection due to decreased level of consciousness. Neurological examination reveals his right pupil is dilated and nonreactive to light, and his right eye is deviated laterally and inferiorly. The patient is unable to adduct, elevate, or depress the right eye, and ptosis is noted on the same side. Corneal reflexes are preserved bilaterally, and no facial asymmetry is observed. He does not demonstrate limb weakness or altered sensation in the extremities. A noncontrast CT scan of the head shows no acute hemorrhage, but a focal area of contusion is seen near the base of the midbrain. No other cranial nerves appear to be involved. Which of the following structures is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: Oculomotor nerve (cranial nerve III) controls most extraocular muscles including the medial rectus, superior rectus, inferior rectus, and inferior oblique. It also innervates the levator palpebrae superioris (eyelid elevation) and carries parasympathetic fibers for pupillary constriction and lens accommodation. A lesion to the oculomotor nerve results in a classic 'down and out' eye due to unopposed action of the lateral rectus (CN VI) and superior oblique (CN IV), ptosis from levator palpebrae paralysis, and mydriasis from loss of parasympathetic input. The most common causes include trauma, posterior communicating artery aneurysm (compressing superficial parasympathetic fibers), and microvascular ischemia (e.g., diabetes affecting deep fibers). In trauma, direct injury to the nerve at the midbrain or within the subarachnoid space can produce these findings. Pupil-involving palsy suggests compression (e.g., aneurysm or herniation), while sparing of the pupil often points to microvascular disease. Clinically, oculomotor nerve palsy presents with impaired adduction, elevation, and depression, ptosis, and a non-reactive, dilated pupil.
bottom_line: Oculomotor nerve (cranial nerve III) controls most extraocular muscles including the medial rectus, superior rectus, inferior rectus, and inferior oblique.

subject: Neuro
topic: Normal structure and function of the nervous system
stem: A 45-year-old man with suspected meningitis is scheduled for a lumbar puncture. The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position with his knees pulled to his chest. The physician palpates the iliac crests to identify the L4 vertebral level and inserts a spinal needle in the midline between the L3 and L4 spinous processes. As the needle advances through the soft tissues, a distinct "pop" is felt just before cerebrospinal fluid is obtained. Which of the following structures provides the greatest resistance before this "pop" is felt during lumbar puncture?
explanation: During a lumbar puncture, the spinal needle passes sequentially through the skin, subcutaneous tissue, supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, and then the ligamentum flavum, which connects the laminae of adjacent vertebrae. This elastic ligament provides the greatest resistance, and the distinct “pop” felt during the procedure occurs as the needle punctures it to enter the epidural space. From there, the needle advances through the dura mater and arachnoid mater to reach the lumbar cistern, where CSF is sampled. If the needle is inserted posteriorly between the L3 and L4 vertebral arches, the ligamentum flavum must be traversed to access the spinal canal. It’s important to note that while the ligamentum flavum is punctured during lumbar puncture, the posterior longitudinal ligament is not. Rather, the posterior longitudinal ligament lies along the posterior vertebral bodies and forms the anterior wall of the spinal canal. It may be displaced or pushed anteriorly during pathology (e.g., disc herniation) and can compress or contact the spinal nerve roots, but it is not penetrated during a routine midline spinal tap. The needle path for a lumbar puncture is midline and posterior, avoiding this anterior structure.
bottom_line: During a lumbar puncture, the spinal needle passes sequentially through the skin, subcutaneous tissue, supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, and then the ligamentum flavum, which connects the laminae of adjacent vertebrae.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset weakness of the right side of her face. She is unable to close her right eye, raise her right eyebrow, or smile symmetrically. She reports recent recovery from an upper respiratory illness. She denies rash, ear pain, and tick exposure. Neurologic examination reveals complete right-sided facial paralysis. Strength and sensation in the limbs are normal. Which of the following best explains the underlying cause of her condition?

options.A: Infarction of corticobulbar fibers in the left internal capsule
options.C: Inflammatory demyelination of the right facial nerve distal to the facial nucleus
options.D: Reactivation of varicella-zoster virus in the geniculate ganglion
options.E: Infection with Borrelia burgdorferi acquired during recent hiking

answer_text: Inflammatory demyelination of the right facial nerve distal to the facial nucleus
explanation: This is a lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve palsy. LMN lesions of CN VII result in weakness of both the upper and lower facial muscles on the affected side, including the forehead. The absence of systemic findings or vesicles supports idiopathic inflammatory neuropathy (post-viral), the most common cause of LMN facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). The nerve is affected after it exits the brainstem, distal to the facial nucleus.
bottom_line: This is a lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve palsy.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 61-year-old man comes to the emergency department due to worsening lower back pain and difficulty walking over the past week. He reports that the pain began as a dull ache in the lumbar region, but has progressively increased in intensity and is now associated with shooting pains down both legs. He also notes recent difficulty initiating urination and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying. Over the past two days, he has developed numbness in the inner thighs and buttocks. He has a 30- pack-year smoking history and was diagnosed with metastatic prostate cancer 6 months ago. Vital signs are within normal limits. Neurologic exam reveals decreased patellar and Achilles reflexes bilaterally and reduced sensation over the perineum. Motor strength is 3/5 in the lower extremities bilaterally. Rectal tone is decreased. Which of the following best explains this patient’s condition?

options.A: Bilateral infarction of the anterior spinal arteries
options.B: Compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots
options.D: Ischemic injury of the conus medullaris
options.E: Transverse inflammation of the thoracic spinal cord

answer_text: Compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots
explanation: This patient's constellation of symptoms - progressive lower back pain, bilateral leg weakness, decreased reflexes, perineal numbness, and urinary retention - is consistent with cauda equina syndrome (CES). CES results from compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots within the spinal canal, which are responsible for sensory and motor innervation of the lower limbs and pelvic organs. Because these are peripheral nerves, findings include lower motor neuron signs, such as decreased tone and reflexes, and sensory loss in a "saddle" distribution. The symptoms of CES are typically progressive and asymmetric, although they may eventually become bilateral, as in this patient. The decreased rectal tone, sensory loss in the perineal area, and urinary retention are hallmark features of CES, due to disruption of the S2-S4 nerve roots. Prostate cancer is a common malignancy with a predilection for spinal metastases, and in this case, mass effect on the cauda equina is the likely cause. Emergency MRI is necessary for confirmation, and urgent surgical decompression is the treatment of choice to prevent permanent deficits. The key distinction for USMLE between conus medullaris and cauda equina syndromes is that conus medullaris syndrome presents with sudden-onset symptoms and perianal anesthesia, whereas cauda equina syndrome has a gradual onset with saddle anesthesia; both can involve leg pain and urinary retention, but USMLE focuses mainly on this tempo and sensory distribution.
bottom_line: This patient's constellation of symptoms - progressive lower back pain, bilateral leg weakness, decreased reflexes, perineal numbness, and urinary retention - is consistent with cauda equina syndrome (CES).

subject: Neuro
stem: A 70-year-old man is brought to emergency by his wife for a 4-hour history of sudden-onset dizziness, nausea, and difficulty walking. He reports feeling like the room is spinning and has vomited twice since the symptoms began. On examination, he has horizontal nystagmus, worse with gaze to the right. There also appears to be a mild Horner syndrome on the right. There is right limb ataxia and dysmetria of the right upper and lower limbs on finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing. Muscle strength is preserved, and there are no signs of weakness or sensory loss. His speech is mildly slurred, and he veers to the right when attempting to walk. He has a 30-year history of hypertension and type II diabetes. His medications are lisinopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, metformin, glyburide, and a daily multi-vitamin. A stroke at which of the following locations is the most likely explanation for this patient's acute presentation?
explanation: The labeled structure on this 2D cross-section of the medulla is the right inferior cerebellar peduncle (ICP). This structure connects the cerebellum to the rest of the brainstem and spinal cord. Despite the name, the ICP is actually part of the medulla. This patient has dysmetria, which is inability to judge distance and perform smooth movements, resulting in under- or over-shooting targets on physical exam. He also has limb ataxia. These two findings are consistent with an ipsilateral cerebellar lesions (although as we said, the ICP is technically part of the medulla). The peduncle also transmits vestibular (balance) input from the vestibular nuclei. Disruption causes balance problems, vertigo, and nystagmus. The Horner syndrome is likely due to lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome), which can be caused by a stroke at the ipsilateral posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or vertebral artery. "PICA chew" (for Pikachu) is the mnemonic --> dysphagia/hoarseness and Horner (ptosis, miosis, anhydrosis) are what we see with Wallenberg syndrome. The inferior cerebellar peduncle is actually part of the lateral medulla, not the cerebellum itself, and can be affected in lateral medullary syndrome.
bottom_line: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: High Yield USMLE Audio Answer:

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely location of her lesion?
explanation: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) arises from the medial medulla and innervates the intrinsic muscles of the tongue. A lesion to the hypoglossal nerve or its nucleus causes the tongue to deviate toward the side of the lesion due to unopposed action of the contralateral genioglossus. In medial medullary syndrome, infarction of the paramedian branches of the anterior spinal artery causes ipsilateral tongue weakness and contralateral hemiparesis. This patient’s left-sided tongue deviation and right-sided motor deficits localize to the left medial medulla.
bottom_line: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) arises from the medial medulla and innervates the intrinsic muscles of the tongue.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 49-year-old woman presents to the clinic for evaluation of progressive difficulty with daily activities. She reports accidentally burning her hands while cooking and describes intermittent numbness and tingling in both upper extremities. On further questioning, she reports weakness when gripping objects and says she often drops items unintentionally. Neurologic examination reveals decreased pain and temperature sensation over both shoulders and arms, but preserved vibration and proprioception throughout. There is mild atrophy and fasciculations of the intrinsic hand muscles bilaterally. Lower extremity strength and sensation are normal. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

options.B: Infarction of the anterior spinal artery
options.C: Lesion of the anterior horn cells at lumbar levels
options.D: Multiple sclerosis plaques in the cervical cord
options.E: Syrinx formation in the cervical spinal cord

answer_text: Syrinx formation in the cervical spinal cord
explanation: This patient has classic features of syringomyelia, caused by a syrinx (fluid-filled cavity) that expands within the central canal of the cervical spinal cord. The syrinx compresses the decussating fibers of the spinothalamic tract at the anterior white commissure, causing bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation in a cape-like distribution over the shoulders and arms. If the syrinx expands, it can involve the anterior horn cells, leading to weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations in the intrinsic hand muscles, while proprioception and vibration remain intact because the dorsal columns are unaffected. Syringomyelia may be idiopathic, post- traumatic, or associated with Chiari malformation type I.
bottom_line: This patient has classic features of syringomyelia, caused by a syrinx (fluid-filled cavity) that expands within the central canal of the cervical spinal cord.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Subdural hematoma is caused by rupture of the bridging veins (also known as superior cerebral veins), which extend from the cortical surface of the brain to the dural venous sinuses. These veins are especially susceptible to tearing in situations of rapid acceleration-deceleration, such as falls or motor vehicle collisions, particularly in individuals with brain atrophy. This patient, with a history of both dementia and chronic alcohol use, is at increased risk due to age-related cerebral atrophy that stretches the bridging veins, making them more vulnerable even to minor trauma, such as a ground-level fall. Clinically, subdural hematomas often lack a lucid interval-unlike epidural hematomas-meaning the patient does not typically have a period of regained consciousness and normalcy after initial trauma. Instead, symptoms such as confusion, drowsiness, or neurologic deficits may evolve slowly over hours to days. In this case, the patient’s progressive confusion and drowsiness several days after a fall, without any mention of loss of consciousness, is classic for a slowly accumulating subdural hematoma. On non-contrast CT scan, subdural hematomas appear as crescent-shaped hyperdensities that may be heterogenous due to a mix of fresh and old blood, reflecting different bleeding episodes over time. This contrasts with the lens-shaped (biconvex) appearance of epidural hematomas. The slow accumulation can delay presentation, as seen in this case, where the patient deteriorates days after the initial injury. For treatment, craniotomy is the standard management for symptomatic or enlarging subdural hematomas. If the patient is comatose or has signs of herniation, intubation and hyperventilation may be needed as a temporizing measure to reduce intracranial pressure before surgery.
bottom_line: Epidural hematoma.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with double vision that worsens when looking toward the right. She reports a recent history of headache and facial pressure. On examination, her right eye fails to abduct when asked to look to the right, while all other extraocular movements are intact. MRI reveals a mass compressing the medial aspect of the cavernous sinus. Which of the following cranial nerves is most likely affected?
explanation: The abducens nerve innervates the lateral rectus muscle, which abducts the eye. CN VI is the most medial nerve within the cavernous sinus and is highly susceptible to compression by mass lesions, elevated intracranial pressure, or thrombosis. A lesion causes impaired lateral gaze on the affected side, resulting in horizontal diplopia that worsens when looking toward the lesion. This clinical presentation-isolated inability to abduct the eye-is classic for CN VI palsy.
bottom_line: The abducens nerve innervates the lateral rectus muscle, which abducts the eye.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of worsening headaches, nausea, and vomiting for the past 2 weeks. His mother reports that he vomits most mornings when waking up. On exam, he has bilateral papilledema, impaired upward gaze, retracted eyelids, and pupils that react to accommodation but poorly to light. He also walks with a shuffling gait. Which of the following is the most likely location of the mass?
explanation: This is Parinaud syndrome (dorsal midbrain syndrome), caused by compression from a pineal region mass (usually germinoma or pinealoma). The classic triad is impaired upward gaze (vertical gaze palsy), light-near dissociation, and eyelid retraction (Collier sign). Morning vomiting is a sign of increased intracranial pressure from obstructive hydrocephalus due to blockage of the aqueduct. The location of the mass is the pineal gland, which lies dorsal to the midbrain.
bottom_line: This is Parinaud syndrome (dorsal midbrain syndrome), caused by compression from a pineal region mass (usually germinoma or pinealoma).

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic for evaluation of right shoulder weakness after a recent sports injury. He reports falling directly onto his shoulder while playing football two weeks ago. Since then, he has had difficulty raising his right arm away from his body. He also notes a persistent dull ache and slight numbness over the outer portion of the shoulder. There is no wrist or hand weakness, and he denies any tingling in the fingers. On physical examination, the patient is unable to abduct the right arm beyond 15 degrees. There is a visible flattening of the contour of the right shoulder compared to the left. Strength is intact in the forearm and hand, and reflexes are normal. Sensation is decreased over the lateral aspect of the upper arm. Based on the attached image, which of the labeled nerves (A-E) is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of axillary nerve injury, a condition often resulting from shoulder trauma, including direct impact or proximal humerus fractures. The axillary nerve arises from C5-C6 roots and innervates the deltoid muscle, which is responsible for shoulder abduction between 15 and 90 degrees. His inability to abduct the arm within this range, combined with a flattened shoulder contour, reflects deltoid dysfunction. Additionally, the axillary nerve provides cutaneous sensation over the lateral upper arm, which explains the patient’s reported numbness. Normal distal motor function and reflexes argue against a more widespread plexus injury, and the absence of hand symptoms localizes the deficit to the axillary distribution. The flattened contour of the shoulder is a subtle yet highly tested finding on USMLE, signaling underlying deltoid atrophy. In the image provided, label B accurately identifies the axillary nerve, which branches from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and winds around the surgical neck of the humerus, making it particularly susceptible to injury in shoulder trauma.
bottom_line: This patient presents with classic signs of axillary nerve injury, a condition often resulting from shoulder trauma, including direct impact or proximal humerus fractures.

subject: Neuro
topic: Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles
stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the urgent care clinic after twisting his ankle while trail running. He states that his right foot rolled inward when he landed on uneven ground, and he felt a sharp pulling sensation along the outer part of his lower leg. He applied ice at home and was able to walk, although with some discomfort. He denies numbness or weakness in the toes, thigh pain, or back symptoms. He has no history of prior injuries or chronic joint disease. On examination, there is mild tenderness along the lateral lower leg without swelling or ecchymosis. The patient has normal strength in dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and inversion, but eversion of the foot is weak compared to the contralateral side. Sensation is decreased over the lateral lower leg and dorsum of the foot, while deep tendon reflexes are intact. There are no skin lesions, and no proximal motor or sensory deficits are noted. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: This patient presents with isolated eversion weakness and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral lower leg following an inversion-type ankle injury. These findings are characteristic of injury to the superficial peroneal nerve, which innervates the lateral compartment of the leg, including the fibularis (peroneus) longus and brevis muscles, responsible for eversion of the foot. On USMLE, superficial peroneal nerve is the answer if the patient loses only eversion of the foot. The preserved dorsiflexion rules out involvement of the deep peroneal nerve, which supplies the anterior compartment muscles, including the tibialis anterior. Likewise, the absence of plantarflexion or inversion deficits indicates that the tibial nerve is intact. The superficial peroneal nerve also provides cutaneous sensation to the dorsum of the foot (except the first web space) and lateral lower leg, consistent with the sensory loss described. This type of injury commonly occurs due to stretching or entrapment of the superficial peroneal nerve during ankle inversion injuries or direct trauma to the lateral leg. The nerve becomes superficial in the lower leg, making it vulnerable in this region. Symptoms are often mild and may resolve with conservative management, though persistent weakness or numbness may require further workup.
bottom_line: This patient presents with isolated eversion weakness and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and lateral lower leg following an inversion-type ankle injury.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 32-year-old man falls while jogging on a trail and sustains a fracture of the shaft of his left humerus. He is unable to extend his left wrist or fingers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The radial nerve is the main motor nerve responsible for extension of the elbow, wrist, and fingers. It arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and courses along the spiral (radial) groove of the humerus, making it highly susceptible to injury in the setting of midshaft humeral fractures. In this case, the patient’s fall resulted in a midshaft humeral fracture, and he now has inability to extend his wrist or fingers (wrist drop)-classic findings of radial nerve injury. This is due to the loss of innervation to the BEST muscles: Brachioradialis, Extensors, Supinator, and Triceps, all of which receive motor input from the radial nerve. The arm often rests in a pronated position due to loss of supinator function. Pronated arm with wrist drop = Radial nerve injury. Sensory deficits may accompany the motor findings, typically involving the posterior aspect of the arm and forearm. However, the motor findings are usually the most HY.
bottom_line: The radial nerve is the main motor nerve responsible for extension of the elbow, wrist, and fingers.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 42-year-old right-handed man is brought to the emergency department after a workplace accident in which a large metal beam fell and struck his right upper arm. He reports immediate sharp pain followed by difficulty moving his hand. He denies any loss of consciousness, chest trauma, or head injury. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On physical examination, there is noticeable swelling and deformity over the midshaft of the right humerus. The right upper limb is held in slight pronation. The patient is unable to actively extend his wrist and fingers at the metacarpophalangeal joints, resulting in a "wrist drop" posture. However, passive extension by the examiner is preserved. He reports numbness and tingling over the dorsum of his forearm and hand, particularly in the web space between the thumb and index finger. The triceps reflex is intact, and there is no weakness of elbow extension. Distal pulses are palpable, and capillary refill is normal. Radiographic imaging confirms a displaced spiral fracture of the midshaft of the right humerus. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: The radial nerve originates from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and innervates the posterior compartments of the arm and forearm. It travels posteriorly in the radial (spiral) groove of the humerus along with the deep brachial artery, which makes it particularly susceptible to injury in midshaft or spiral fractures of the humerus. When such fractures occur, the radial nerve can be contused, stretched, or transected within the groove, especially if there is significant displacement. Despite the proximity of the nerve to the fracture site, the triceps reflex is often preserved in midshaft humeral fractures. This is because the branches of the radial nerve that supply the long and lateral heads of the triceps typically arise proximal to the spiral groove. Thus, elbow extension remains intact, helping localize the injury distal to these motor branches. The most characteristic finding of radial nerve injury at this level is wrist drop, which results from paralysis of the extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis, and extensor digitorum, which are responsible for extending the wrist and fingers. Additionally, sensory loss over the dorsum of the hand, especially the dorsal first web space between the thumb and index finger, is highly suggestive of radial nerve involvement. This area is uniquely innervated by the superficial branch of the radial nerve and serves as a clinical clue.
bottom_line: The radial nerve originates from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and innervates the posterior compartments of the arm and forearm.

subject: Neuro
stem: Which of the following constellation of symptoms is most likely associated with this pathology?
explanation: The image shown is a gross coronal section of the midbrain from an autopsy specimen. It demonstrates bilateral pallor (loss of pigment) in the substantia nigra pars compacta-a classic hallmark of Parkinson disease (PD). Normally, this area appears dark due to neuromelanin in dopaminergic neurons. In PD, these neurons degenerate, leading to dopamine depletion in the nigrostriatal pathway, which causes the cardinal motor symptoms. The correct symptom cluster includes resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and postural instability-the classic motor features of PD. Other features include a masked facial expression, stooped posture, shuffling gait, and micrographia. Importantly, patients often respond to levodopa, especially in early stages. subject: Neuro
stem: A 67-year-old man is evaluated for memory loss 3 weeks after surviving a cardiac arrest. He was successfully resuscitated after 8 minutes of pulselessness and spent 2 weeks in the intensive care unit. Since discharge, he has difficulty forming new memories and often repeats the same questions minutes after being given the answers. He recalls the names of his children and major life events but cannot remember events that happened earlier the same day. Neurologic exam is unremarkable except for mild inattentiveness. Which of the following best describes this patient’s primary neurologic deficit?

options.A: Anomic aphasia due to inferior temporal lobe lesion
options.D: Broca aphasia from dominant inferior frontal lesion
options.E: Conduction aphasia due to arcuate fasciculus lesion
options.F: Procedural memory loss from basal ganglia damage
options.G: Retrograde amnesia due to prefrontal cortex injury
options.H: Wernicke aphasia from superior temporal gyrus lesion

explanation: This patient exhibits anterograde amnesia, the inability to form new memories, while remote memories remain intact. This pattern is classically caused by bilateral hippocampal damage, which is highly vulnerable to global cerebral hypoxia, such as during cardiac arrest. The hippocampus is essential for consolidating new declarative (explicit) memories, and its damage leads to profound short-term memory deficits. MRI showing bilateral signal abnormalities in the medial temporal lobes-where the hippocampus is located-supports this diagnosis. In contrast, retrograde amnesia involves loss of previously formed memories and often results from damage to the prefrontal cortex or diffuse cortical injury. The hippocampus is not a storage site for long-term memories but is required to encode and consolidate them. Therefore, hippocampal injury impairs learning of new information, while older memories, already stored elsewhere in the cortex, are spared. This is a classic clinical pattern in hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy.
bottom_line: This patient exhibits anterograde amnesia, the inability to form new memories, while remote memories remain intact.

subject: Neuro
topic: Normal structure and function of the nervous system
stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department due to a 2-week history of persistent morning headaches, nausea, and visual disturbances. His father reports that the patient has recently begun to tilt his head downward to see objects more clearly and complains of double vision when trying to look up. The child has also developed occasional urinary accidents and seems increasingly unsteady on his feet, often shuffling and bumping into objects when walking. He has no history of head trauma, fever, or prior neurologic issues. On examination, the boy is alert but uncomfortable. He exhibits a broad-based gait, and tandem walking is impaired. Fundoscopy shows bilateral optic disc swelling, and his upward gaze is limited bilaterally, though horizontal and downward gaze remain intact. Pupils are equal and round, but react sluggishly to light. Imaging reveals a midline mass compressing structures in the posterior aspect of the upper brainstem, resulting in expansion of upstream ventricular spaces. The mass is most likely located in which of the following structures?
explanation: This patient presents with signs of elevated intracranial pressure and a constellation of dorsal midbrain findings. Key features include morning-predominant headaches, vomiting, bilateral papilledema, vertical gaze limitation (specifically upward), and urinary incontinence with gait disturbance. These symptoms are highly suggestive of Parinaud syndrome, also known as dorsal midbrain syndrome, which occurs due to compression of the tectal region. The pineal gland, located just anterior to the superior colliculi and posterior to the third ventricle, is the most common site of mass lesions causing this syndrome in pediatric patients. Lesions of the pineal gland - such as pinealomas or germinomas - often affect the vertical gaze center, particularly the pretectal area, leading to impaired upward gaze. Additionally, the pineal region is situated above the cerebral aqueduct, so any enlargement can compress this channel, resulting in obstructive hydrocephalus. This explains the child’s early-morning headaches, vomiting, papilledema, and shuffling gait. Hydrocephalus can also stretch periventricular white matter tracts, leading to urinary incontinence and imbalance, especially when the pressure builds gradually. Another helpful finding is the presence of sluggish pupillary light response, which, along with preserved accommodation, suggests light-near dissociation - a classic sign in Parinaud syndrome.
bottom_line: This patient presents with signs of elevated intracranial pressure and a constellation of dorsal midbrain findings.

subject: Neuro
stem: A 66-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being found on the floor by his wife. He was lifting a heavy object at home when he suddenly collapsed and was unable to move his legs. He reports severe mid- thoracic back pain but denies any trauma to the head. On arrival, he is alert and oriented. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), blood pressure is 138/82 mm Hg, and pulse is 92/min. Neurologic examination shows flaccid paralysis of both lower extremities with areflexia. Pinprick and temperature sensation are absent below the umbilicus. Vibration and proprioception are intact. Babinski is absent. There is no perianal sensation or anal wink. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Anterior cord syndrome results from ischemia or infarction involving the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord, usually due to anterior spinal artery (ASA) occlusion. The corticospinal tracts and spinothalamic tracts are affected, resulting in bilateral motor paralysis and loss of pain and temperature sensation below the level of the lesion. However, the dorsal columns, which carry vibration and proprioception, are spared because they are supplied by the posterior spinal arteries. In the acute phase, the motor deficit is flaccid due to spinal shock, but later evolves into spasticity with hyperreflexia as upper motor neuron signs emerge. This condition is commonly seen in patients with arterial diseases, after aortic aneurysm repair, severe hypotension, or disc herniation causing compression of the anterior spinal artery, especially at the mid-thoracic “watershed” zone (T4-T8). MRI typically shows hyperintensity in the anterior cord, and bladder involvement may also be seen. The absence of vibration/proprioception loss with concurrent motor and pain/temperature deficits is the key distinguishing feature. Early diagnosis is essential, though prognosis is generally poor compared to other cord syndromes.
bottom_line: Anterior cord syndrome results from ischemia or infarction involving the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord, usually due to anterior spinal artery (ASA) occlusion.
